topics.title: Registry Viewing and Editing Using Native Tools of Operating Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCL607 - CYBER FORENSICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Registry Viewing and Editing Using Native Tools of Operating Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Windows Registry** is a hierarchical, centralized database used by the Microsoft Windows operating system to store low-level configuration settings, hardware information, installed software, user preferences, and security credentials. From a **Cyber Forensics** perspective, the registry is one of the most valuable sources of digital evidence, as it records user activities, system modifications, network connections, and application execution artifacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCCL607):** The Registry is a critical volatile/non-volatile artifact examined during live and post-mortem forensic investigations. Native Operating System tools (built-in tools shipped with Windows) provide the first line of registry analysis before third-party forensic suites (e.g., Autopsy, FTK, Registry Recon) are deployed.

The five predefined top-level containers of the registry are called **Registry Hives**, often referred to as **Root Keys**:

| Abbreviation | Full Name | Forensic Significance |
|--------------|-----------|----------------------|
| `HKLM` | `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE` | System-wide configuration (hardware, software, security policies) |
| `HKCU` | `HKEY_CURRENT_USER` | Logged-in user profile, user-specific settings |
| `HKU` | `HKEY_USERS` | All user profiles loaded on the system |
| `HKCR` | `HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT` | File associations and COM object mappings |
| `HKCC` | `HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG` | Current hardware profile (subset of HKLM) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Building's Master Blueprint"**
> 
> Imagine a large commercial building with hundreds of offices. Instead of writing every instruction on a separate piece of paper, the building manager stores **all rules in a single master file cabinet**:
> - **Drawer (Hive)** = Major section (e.g., "Tenants", "Utilities", "Security")
> - **Folder (Key)** = Specific topic (e.g., "Office 304", "Electricity", "Fire Alarms")
> - **Subfolder (Subkey)** = Detailed sub-category
> - **Sticky note inside the folder (Value)** = The actual instruction/data ("AC set to 22 °C", "Door opens at 9:00 AM")
> 
> The **Registry** is exactly this for Windows. Every program, every user, every USB device, and every network connection leaves a "sticky note" somewhere in this cabinet. A forensic investigator walks in, opens specific drawers, and reads the notes to reconstruct what happened in the building — and when.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

While the registry does not involve physical constants, the following **standard metrics** are essential for forensic reporting:

- **Default registry hive size limit:** ~**2 GB** (Windows 10/11 practical limit, theoretically expandable to 4 GB)
- **Maximum key name length:** **255 characters**
- **Maximum value name length:** **16,383 characters** (Windows Server 2003+) / 260 in older systems
- **Maximum value data size:** **~1 MB** (practical limit) / up to 4 GB in theory
- **Registry hive file locations on disk:**
  - `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SYSTEM` — HKLM\SYSTEM
  - `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SOFTWARE` — HKLM\SOFTWARE
  - `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SAM` — HKLM\SAM
  - `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SECURITY` — HKLM\SECURITY
  - `%UserProfile%\NTUSER.DAT` — HKCU

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of the Registry

The registry is organized in a **tree structure** with four structural levels:

1. **Hives (Root Keys):** The topmost containers. There are only **five** predefined root keys.
2. **Keys:** Folders within hives. Each key can contain subkeys and values.
3. **Subkeys:** Subordinate keys nested within a parent key (a key is also a subkey of its parent).
4. **Values (Value Entries):** The actual data stored. Each value has three components:
   - **Value Name** (the label)
   - **Data Type** (the format)
   - **Value Data** (the content)

## 2.2 The Five Standard Value Data Types

| Type Code | Constant Name | Description | Forensic Example |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|------------------|
| `REG_SZ` | String Value | Fixed-length Unicode string | `"C:\Program Files\App\app.exe"` |
| `REG_EXPAND_SZ` | Expandable String | String with environment variables (`%SystemRoot%`) | `"%ProgramFiles%\Windows Defender\MsMpEng.exe"` |
| `REG_MULTI_SZ` | Multi-String | Multiple strings (array) | List of blocked applications |
| `REG_DWORD` | 32-bit Number | Integer (often a flag: 0/1) | `Run` key enable flag (0 = disabled, 1 = enabled) |
| `REG_QWORD` | 64-bit Number | 64-bit integer | Timestamps (Windows epoch) |
| `REG_BINARY` | Binary Data | Raw bytes (hex view) | Encrypted credentials, service configurations |
| `REG_NONE` | No Data Type | Raw data, no defined type | Legacy/unused entries |
| `REG_RESOURCE_LIST` | Resource List | Hardware resource descriptors | Device Manager configuration |
| `REG_FULL_RESOURCE_DESCRIPTOR` | Full Resource Descriptor | Hardware description | Plug-and-Play device mapping |
| `REG_RESOURCE_REQUIREMENTS_LIST` | Resource Requirements | Hardware requirements | Resource arbitration |
| `REG_LINK` | Symbolic Link | Soft link to another key | `HKCC` link to `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Hardware Profiles\Current` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The most frequently tested value types in registry-based forensic questions are `REG_SZ`, `REG_DWORD`, `REG_BINARY`, `REG_EXPAND_SZ`, and `REG_MULTI_SZ`. Master these five.

## 2.3 Native Operating System Tools for Registry Manipulation

Windows provides **four primary native tools** for viewing and editing the registry:

### Tool 1: Registry Editor (`regedit.exe`)
- **GUI-based**, hierarchical tree view of all hives.
- Allows **view, create, modify, delete** of keys, subkeys, and values.
- Supports **export to .reg** files (text-based backup format).
- Supports **import .reg** files (merge into registry).
- Supports **search** (`Ctrl + F`) across names, values, and data.
- Supports **permissions editing** (right-click → Permissions).

### Tool 2: Console Registry Tool (`reg.exe`)
- **Command-line** equivalent of regedit.
- Used in **batch scripts, forensic triage**, and remote administration.
- Sub-commands: `reg query`, `reg add`, `reg delete`, `reg copy`, `reg save`, `reg restore`, `reg load`, `reg unload`, `reg export`, `reg import`, `reg compare`, `reg flags`.

### Tool 3: PowerShell Registry Provider
- **Object-oriented** access via `Microsoft.Win32.Registry` and `Microsoft.Win32.RegistryKey` .NET classes.
- Uses standard PowerShell drive notation: `HKLM:\`, `HKCU:\`.
- Forensically useful for **remote registry queries**, **filtering**, and **scripted bulk export**.
- Commands: `Get-Item`, `Get-ItemProperty`, `Set-ItemProperty`, `New-Item`, `Remove-Item`, `Get-ChildItem`.

### Tool 4: Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI)
- The `StdRegProv` class enables **remote** and **scripted** registry access.
- Used when GUI/console access is restricted (e.g., via Group Policy).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Forensics Quick Reference

| Forensic Artifact | Registry Path | Evidence Provided |
|-------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **RecentDocs** | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RecentDocs` | Recently opened files (MRU list) |
| **RunMRU** | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RunMRU` | Commands run via Win+R |
| **TypedPaths** | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\TypedPaths` | URLs typed in Explorer address bar |
| **UserAssist** | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist` | Program execution counts and last-run timestamps |
| **AppCompatCache (Shimcache)** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\AppCompatCache` | Program execution evidence (even after uninstall) |
| **BAM (Background Activity Moderator)** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\bam\State\UserSettings\<SID>` | Recently executed executables with timestamps |
| **Run / RunOnce Keys (Persistence)** | `HKLM\...\Run`, `HKCU\...\Run`, `HKLM\...\RunOnce`, `HKCU\...\RunOnce` | Malware persistence mechanisms |
| **USBStor** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBStor` | History of connected USB devices |
| **MountedDevices** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices` | Volume serial numbers of mounted drives |
| **NetworkList** | `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\NetworkList\Profiles` | Wi-Fi/Ethernet connection history |
| **Last-Logged-In User** | `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon` | Default user name, last logged-in user |
| **ComputerName** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\ComputerName\ComputerName` | Hostname of the machine |
| **TimeZone** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\TimeZoneInformation` | System timezone setting |

## 2.5 Why Registry Forensics is Engineering-Relevant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Incident Response:** SOC analysts query `Run` keys to detect malware persistence during live triage.
> - **e-Discovery / Legal:** Lawyers retrieve user activity evidence from `RecentDocs` and `UserAssist`.
> - **Data Breach Investigation:** `USBStor` and `MountedDevices` prove data exfiltration via removable media.
> - **Endpoint Security:** EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) products monitor registry key creation in real-time to block ransomware.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive step-by-step procedures** for viewing and editing the registry using each of the four native tools. Every action is mapped to a forensic scenario.

## 3.1 Procedure A: Viewing the Registry Using `regedit.exe` (GUI)

### Step 1 — Launch Registry Editor
- **Method 1 (GUI):** Press `Win + R` → type `regedit` → press `Enter` → click **Yes** on UAC prompt.
- **Method 2 (CMD):** Open Command Prompt as Administrator → type `regedit` → press `Enter`.
- **Method 3 (Run box):** Navigate to `C:\Windows\regedit.exe` → double-click.

> [!NOTE]
> The UAC (User Account Control) prompt is a security feature. Forensic examiners must launch as Administrator to access `HKLM` subtrees.

### Step 2 — Navigate the Tree
- The left pane shows the **five root hives**.
- Click the **arrow (▶)** next to a hive to expand its keys.
- Drill down to the required key (e.g., `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`).
- The right pane displays **Default** value (always present) and any **named values**.

### Step 3 — Search the Registry
- Press `Ctrl + F` to open the **Find** dialog.
- Options:
  - **Match whole string only** (exact match) — slower but precise.
  - **Match data only** — searches only inside value data.
  - **Match case** — case-sensitive search.
- Press `F3` to find the **next occurrence**.

### Step 4 — View Value Details
- Double-click a value to see the **full data** (especially useful for `REG_BINARY`).
- Right-click a key → **Export** → save as `.reg` file for forensic documentation.

## 3.2 Procedure B: Querying the Registry Using `reg.exe` (Command Line)

### Step B.1 — Query an Entire Key
```cmd
reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run"
```
**Output Format:**
```
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
    SecurityHealth    REG_SZ    %windir%\system32\SecurityHealthSystray.exe
    OneDrive    REG_SZ    "C:\Program Files\Microsoft OneDrive\OneDrive.exe" /background
```

### Step B.2 — Query a Specific Value
```cmd
reg query "HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist" /v "Count"
```

### Step B.3 — Recursive Query (Entire Subtree)
```cmd
reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall" /s
```
The `/s` flag recursively enumerates all subkeys and values.

### Step B.4 — Export a Hive to a Forensic File
```cmd
reg export "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" C:\evidence\run_key_backup.reg
```
This creates a **text-format `.reg`** file that is admissible as evidence and can be re-imported.

### Step B.5 — Save a Hive in Binary Form
```cmd
reg save "HKLM\SAM" C:\evidence\SAM_backup.hiv
```
The `.hiv` format is the **raw binary hive file** used by specialized forensic tools (e.g., `reglookup`, `RegRipper`).

## 3.3 Procedure C: Querying & Editing with PowerShell

### Step C.1 — Navigate to a Registry Path
```powershell
Set-Location HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
```

### Step C.2 — List All Subkeys
```powershell
Get-ChildItem -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run"
```

### Step C.3 — Read Value Data
```powershell
Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run"
```

### Step C.4 — Create a New Value (Persistence Simulation)
```powershell
Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" `
    -Name "MyLabTest" `
    -Value "C:\Tools\test.exe" `
    -Type String
```

### Step C.5 — Delete a Value
```powershell
Remove-ItemProperty -Path "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" -Name "MyLabTest"
```

### Step C.6 — Create a New Key
```powershell
New-Item -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyLabExperiment" -Force
```

### Step C.7 — Delete a Key
```powershell
Remove-Item -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyLabExperiment" -Recurse
```

### Step C.8 — Forensic Bulk Export
```powershell
Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\bam\State\UserSettings" |
    Select-Object * |
    Export-Csv -Path "C:\evidence\BAM_evidence.csv" -NoTypeInformation
```

## 3.4 Procedure D: The `.reg` File Format (Forensic Backup)

A `.reg` file is a **plain-text** representation of registry keys. Its structure is:

```
Windows Registry Editor Version 5.00

[HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run]
"MyLabTest"="C:\\Tools\\test.exe"
@=hex(2):25,00,77,00,69,00,6e,00,64,00,69,00,72,00,25,00,5c,00
```

**Line-by-line explanation:**
- **`Windows Registry Editor Version 5.00`** → Header magic. Version 5.00 = Windows 2000/XP/2003+. Version 4.00 = 95/98/ME/NT4.
- **`[HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\...]`** → Bracketed path = key being defined.
- **`"ValueName"="Data"`** → A `REG_SZ` value named `MyLabTest` with string data.
- **`@=hex(2):...`** → The **Default** value (`@`) stored as `REG_EXPAND_SZ` (type code `2`).

## 3.5 Forensic Timestamp Decoding (Windows FILETIME)

Most registry values containing timestamps use the **Windows FILETIME** format: a 64-bit integer representing the number of **100-nanosecond intervals** since **January 1, 1601 (UTC)**.

**Conversion formula:**

$$
\text{Unix Timestamp} = \frac{\text{FILETIME} - 116444736000000000}{10000000}
$$

Where:
- $116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000$ = number of 100-ns intervals between 1601-01-01 and 1970-01-01 (Unix epoch).
- Division by $10{,}000{,}000$ converts 100-ns units to seconds.

**Worked Example (Step-by-Step):**

Given a `REG_BINARY` FILETIME value of `0x01D5C2F8A1B3C000` read from a `UserAssist` entry:

$$
\text{FILETIME (hex)} = 0x01D5C2F8A1B3C000
$$

$$
\text{FILETIME (decimal)} = 132{,}354{,}897{,}654{,}321{,}920
$$

$$
\text{Unix Timestamp} = \frac{132{,}354{,}897{,}654{,}321{,}920 - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Unix Timestamp} = \frac{15{,}910{,}161{,}654{,}321{,}920}{10{,}000{,}000} = 1{,}591{,}016{,}165.432192
$$

$$
\text{Human Date} = \text{2020-05-31 14:42:45 UTC}
$$

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Windows Registry. List any three root keys (hives) and state the forensic significance of `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`.

**Model Answer:**

> The **Windows Registry** is a hierarchical database used by Microsoft Windows to store low-level configuration data of the operating system, hardware devices, installed software, and user preferences. It is a critical source of digital evidence in cyber forensics.
>
> **Three Root Keys (Hives):**
> 1. **`HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE (HKLM)`** — Contains system-wide configuration data valid for all users.
> 2. **`HKEY_CURRENT_USER (HKCU)`** — Stores settings specific to the currently logged-in user.
> 3. **`HKEY_USERS (HKU)`** — Contains configuration data for all user profiles that have ever logged in.
>
> **Forensic Significance of `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`:**
> This is one of the most important **persistence keys**. Any executable listed here runs automatically at user logon. Malware authors frequently add entries here to maintain persistence across reboots. Investigators scan this key for unknown or suspicious executable paths, unsigned publishers, and entries pointing to `Temp` directories.

**Marks Distribution (3):** Definition (1) + Three root keys (1) + Forensic significance of Run key (1).

### Question 2: Differentiate between `REG_SZ`, `REG_EXPAND_SZ`, and `REG_MULTI_SZ` value types. Give one forensic example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Definition | Forensic Example |
|------|------------|------------------|
| `REG_SZ` | A fixed-length Unicode string. Read as-is, no variable expansion. | Path: `"C:\Users\victim\report.docx"` in RecentDocs |
| `REG_EXPAND_SZ` | A string containing environment variables that are expanded at runtime (e.g., `%SystemRoot%`). | Path: `"%SystemRoot%\System32\evil.exe"` in a Run key |
| `REG_MULTI_SZ` | A multi-string array where each element is a separate null-terminated string, terminated by an empty string. | List of allowed/blocked applications in Group Policy |

**Marks Distribution (3):** Type definitions (1.5) + One example each (1.5).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **hierarchical structure of the Windows Registry**. List all five root hives and explain the forensic significance of any **two** of them in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the **step-by-step procedure** to view and export the contents of the registry key `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist` using:
   (i) The `regedit.exe` GUI tool.
   (ii) The `reg.exe` command-line tool.
   (iii) The PowerShell Registry Provider.
   Explain how the **UserAssist** artifact helps forensic investigators reconstruct program execution history on a Windows machine. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Hierarchical Structure (7 Marks):**

The Windows Registry is organized as a **logical tree structure** with **four levels**:

1. **Hives (Root Keys)** — Topmost containers. There are exactly **five** predefined root keys in the Windows Registry. They are aliases, not actual folders; they map to specific hive files on disk.
2. **Keys** — Subfolders within a hive. Each key can contain subkeys and values.
3. **Subkeys** — A child key inside a parent key (a key is also a subkey of its parent).
4. **Values (Value Entries)** — The actual data, consisting of a *name*, a *data type*, and the *value data*.

**Block Diagram (5 Hives):**

```
WINDOWS REGISTRY
├── HKLM (HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE)
│   ├── HARDWARE
│   ├── SAM
│   ├── SECURITY
│   ├── SOFTWARE
│   └── SYSTEM
├── HKCU (HKEY_CURRENT_USER)
│   ├── Software
│   └── System
├── HKU  (HKEY_USERS)
│   ├── <SID of User 1>
│   └── <SID of User 2>
├── HKCR (HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT)
│   ├── File Associations
│   └── COM Objects
└── HKCC (HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG)
    └── Hardware Profiles
```

**Forensic Significance — Hive 1: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`:**
This is the **Auto-Start Execution Point (ASEP)** for the entire machine. Any executable listed here launches at every user logon. Investigators analyze it to detect **malware persistence** — entries pointing to `C:\Users\Public`, `%Temp%`, or unsigned executables are red flags.

**Forensic Significance — Hive 2: `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBStor`:**
This key stores the **device instance IDs** of all USB mass storage devices ever connected to the system. Subkeys are named by vendor/product ID (e.g., `Disk&Ven_Samsung&Prod_Flash_Drive&Rev_1100`). A sub-subkey `Serial Number` contains the unique serial number and a `FriendlyName`. Investigators use this to prove data exfiltration via removable media.

**(b) Step-by-Step Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Method (i) — `regedit.exe` GUI:**
1. Press `Win + R` → type `regedit` → `Enter` → click **Yes** on UAC.
2. In the left pane, expand `HKEY_CURRENT_USER`.
3. Drill down: `Software` → `Microsoft` → `Windows` → `CurrentVersion` → `Explorer`.
4. Click the `UserAssist` subkey.
5. The right pane shows two subkeys: `{CEBFF5CD-ACE2-4F4F-9178-9926F41749EA}` and `{F4E57C4B-2036-45F0-A9AB-443BCFE33D9F}`.
6. Expand each — the `Count` value contains **ROT-13 encoded** executable paths with execution counts and last-run timestamps in **Windows FILETIME** format.
7. Right-click `UserAssist` → **Export** → save as `UserAssist_evidence.reg`.

**Method (ii) — `reg.exe` Command Line:**
```cmd
reg export "HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist" C:\evidence\UserAssist_backup.reg
reg query "HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist" /s > C:\evidence\UserAssist_full.txt
```
The first command exports in `.reg` text format. The second recursively queries the entire subtree and saves the output to a text file for chain-of-custody documentation.

**Method (iii) — PowerShell Registry Provider:**
```powershell
$path = "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist"
Get-ChildItem -Path $path -Recurse | Export-Clixml -Path "C:\evidence\UserAssist.xml"
Get-ItemProperty -Path "$path\{CEBFF5CD-ACE2-4F4F-9178-9926F41749EA}\Count" |
    Select-Object * | Export-Csv -Path "C:\evidence\UserAssist_Count.csv" -NoTypeInformation
```
The `Export-Clixml` preserves the full object structure; `Export-Csv` produces a flat, spreadsheet-compatible file for non-technical reviewers (e.g., lawyers).

**Forensic Interpretation of UserAssist:**
The **UserAssist** key is a **program execution artifact**. Windows records up to the **last 1024** programs run interactively. The executable paths are **ROT-13 encoded** (a simple Caesar cipher that shifts each letter by 13 positions: A↔N, B↔O, etc.) to deter casual users from reading them, but it provides **no security** against forensic analysis.

Each `Count` value contains:
- **Execution count** (number of times the program was run).
- **Last execution timestamp** in Windows FILETIME format.
- **Focus time** (how long the program was in the foreground).

Investigators **decode ROT-13** to recover the original path, then **convert FILETIME to human-readable dates** using the formula from Section 3.5, reconstructing a detailed timeline of program activity — even for programs that have since been uninstalled.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative:**

**`[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Windows FILETIME** timestamp format used in registry values. Convert the hex value `0x01DA80A8E0C00000` (read from a `BAM` registry entry) into a human-readable UTC date and time, showing every arithmetic step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A forensic investigator suspects malware persistence on a Windows 11 machine. Describe the **step-by-step native-tool procedure** to:
   (i) Locate auto-start entries using `Run`/`RunOnce` keys.
   (ii) Identify recently connected USB devices via `USBStor`.
   (iii) Detect suspicious executables via `BAM (Background Activity Moderator)`.
   For each, provide the exact **registry path**, the **native command** to query it, and explain the forensic interpretation of the output. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) FILETIME Conversion (7 Marks):**

**Definition of Windows FILETIME:**
Windows FILETIME is a 64-bit unsigned integer representing the number of **100-nanosecond intervals** since **00:00:00, January 1, 1601 (UTC)**. This date is chosen because it is the start of a 400-year Gregorian cycle, simplifying leap year calculations.

**Conversion Formula:**

$$
\text{Unix Timestamp} = \frac{\text{FILETIME} - 11{,}644{,}473{,}600{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000}
$$

**Step 1 — Convert Hex to Decimal:**

$$
0x01DA80A8E0C00000 = 1{,}337{,}703{,}876{,}608{,}000{,}000 \text{ (decimal)}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the Epoch Offset:**

$$
1{,}337{,}703{,}876{,}608{,}000{,}000 - 11{,}644{,}473{,}600{,}000{,}000{,}000 = 1{,}221{,}059{,}400{,}008{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by 10,000,000:**

$$
\frac{1{,}221{,}059{,}400{,}008{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} = 1{,}705{,}904{,}340.0008 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Unix Timestamp to Human Date:**

$$
1{,}705{,}904{,}340 \text{ seconds} = \text{2024-01-15 12:19:00 UTC (Monday)}
$$

**Verification Note:** The last 7 digits of the FILETIME correspond to the sub-second precision; division by $10^7$ converts 100-ns ticks to seconds.

**(b) Malware Persistence Investigation Procedure (7 Marks):**

**(i) Run / RunOnce Keys — `Auto-Start Execution Points (ASEPs)`:**

- **Registry Paths:**
  - `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`
  - `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce`
  - `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`
  - `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce`
- **Native Commands:**
  ```cmd
  reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" /s
  reg query "HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" /s
  ```
  ```powershell
  Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" | Format-List
  ```
- **Forensic Interpretation:** Any value whose executable path points to `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\Temp\`, `C:\ProgramData\`, or a randomly named `.exe` is highly suspicious. Legitimate programs typically reside in `C:\Program Files\`. Cross-reference the path hash against VirusTotal.

**(ii) USBStor — `Recently Connected USB Devices`:**

- **Registry Path:** `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBStor`
- **Subkey Structure:** Each subkey is named as `Disk&Ven_<Vendor>&Prod_<Product>&Rev_<Revision>` (e.g., `Disk&Ven_SanDisk&Prod_Cruzer_Blade&Rev_1.27`). Under each, a subkey is the **device serial number**, containing `FriendlyName`, `Mfg`, `DeviceDesc`, and `HardwareID` values.
- **Native Command:**
  ```cmd
  reg query "HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBStor" /s
  ```
- **Forensic Interpretation:** Enumerate the subkey list to determine **which USB devices were connected** to the system, including the **vendor, product, and unique serial number**. This evidence is admissible in court to prove data exfiltration, device theft, or unauthorized media use.

**(iii) BAM (Background Activity Moderator) — `Recently Executed Executables`:**

- **Registry Path:** `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\bam\State\UserSettings\<SID>`
- **Subkey Structure:** Each subkey is named after a user's **Security Identifier (SID)** (e.g., `S-1-5-21-...-1001`). Inside, named values are **executable file paths** (e.g., `\Device\HarddiskVolume3\Program Files\App\app.exe`), and the value data is a **Windows FILETIME** timestamp of the **last execution**.
- **Native Command:**
  ```cmd
  reg query "HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\bam\State" /s
  ```
  ```powershell
  Get-ChildItem "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\bam\State\UserSettings" -Recurse
  ```
- **Forensic Interpretation:** BAM was introduced in **Windows 10 1709** to delay the startup of background applications. It conveniently records the **last 1024** programs executed for each user, including their full device path and execution timestamp. Investigators decode the FILETIME to obtain a **timeline of program execution** even if Prefetch files (C:\Windows\Prefetch) have been cleared or the executables have been deleted.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Traps in Registry Forensics Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ROT-13 Decoding:** In `UserAssist`, students often copy the path as-is without realizing it is ROT-13 encoded. The examiner expects the decoded path. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing `Run` with `RunOnce`:** `Run` keys execute at every logon. `RunOnce` keys execute **only once** at the next logon and then auto-delete. Conflating them is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong Epoch in FILETIME Calculation:** Students often use the **Unix epoch (1970-01-01)** instead of the **Windows epoch (1601-01-01)** when applying the conversion formula. **Loss: 2-3 marks** in Part B calculations.
> 4. **Missing `BAM` SID Subkey Level:** The actual path is `...bam\State\UserSettings\<SID>` — students frequently stop at `\bam\State` and miss the per-user SID subkeys. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Saying "Run the .reg file to install malware" without explaining forensic implications:** Examiners expect a **security/forensics** interpretation, not just a syntax tutorial.
> 6. **Not Mentioning `reg save` vs `reg export`:** `reg export` produces a **text** `.reg` file. `reg save` produces a **binary** `.hiv` file. The examiner may deduct 1 mark for using them interchangeably.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Windows Registry = **hierarchical, centralized database** storing OS, hardware, software, and user configuration.
- ✅ **5 Root Hives:** `HKLM`, `HKCU`, `HKU`, `HKCR`, `HKCC`.
- ✅ **4 Native Tools:** `regedit.exe` (GUI), `reg.exe` (CMD), **PowerShell Provider** (`HKLM:\`, `HKCU:\`), **WMI `StdRegProv`** (remote).
- ✅ **5 Most Tested Value Types:** `REG_SZ`, `REG_EXPAND_SZ`, `REG_MULTI_SZ`, `REG_DWORD`, `REG_BINARY`.
- ✅ **UserAssist** paths are **ROT-13 encoded** — always decode before analysis.
- ✅ **FILETIME epoch** = **1601-01-01 00:00:00 UTC** (NOT 1970-01-01).
- ✅ **Persistence Keys:** `HKLM\...\Run`, `HKCU\...\Run`, `RunOnce`, `Services`, `Winlogon`, `Shell` extensions.
- ✅ **BAM Path:** `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\bam\State\UserSettings\<SID>`.
- ✅ **USBStor Path:** `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBStor`.
- ✅ **`.reg` file header:** `Windows Registry Editor Version 5.00` → Windows 2000+ format.
- ✅ **Chain of Custody:** Always **hash (SHA-256)** exported `.reg` or `.hiv` files before analysis.
- ✅ **Forensic Acronyms:** MRU = Most Recently Used; SID = Security Identifier; ASEP = Auto-Start Execution Point.
- ✅ **Volatility:** Registry is **non-volatile** (persists across reboots) but **live** changes are lost if not exported; always work on a **copy** during analysis.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hex Analysis Using Hex Workshop Tool
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCL607 - CYBER FORENSICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hex Analysis Using Hex Workshop Tool

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hex Analysis** is the forensic process of inspecting, interpreting, and manipulating the raw **binary (machine-readable) contents** of a digital file at the byte and bit level using a **hexadecimal editor** such as **Hex Workshop**. Every file on a digital storage medium — regardless of its logical format (image, document, executable, etc.) — is stored as a contiguous sequence of bytes, and hexadecimal representation (`00`–`FF`) is the canonical, human-readable shorthand forensic examiners use to study that raw data.

**Hex Workshop** (developed by BreakPoint Software) is a commercial, **Windows-based hexadecimal editor** widely used in cyber forensics for low-level file inspection, **file signature (magic bytes) verification**, **data carving**, **byte-level comparison (diffing)**, **checksum/hash calculation**, and **evidence preservation** during static malware analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (CO Alignment):** This experiment maps to **CO1 (Perform forensic analysis of file systems and storage media)** and **CO2 (Apply standard forensic tools for evidence collection and analysis)** of the PCCCL607 syllabus. Students are expected to demonstrate *Apply* and *Analyze* level cognitive skills per **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The X-Ray Machine for Files**
> Imagine a photograph is like a wrapped gift. Your operating system sees the pretty wrapper (`.jpg`, `.pdf`, `.docx`) and assumes the gift inside is what the wrapper says. But a forensic investigator is **suspicious of the wrapper** — they want to look *inside* the gift without opening it. **Hex Workshop is the forensic X-ray machine** for files. It exposes the **raw skeleton of bytes** that make up the file, allowing an investigator to see:
> - **The wrapper's label (file header / magic bytes)** — confirming whether the file *actually* is what it claims to be.
> - **Hidden compartments (steganographic payloads, appended data, slack space)** — data the wrapper does not advertise.
> - **Tampered sections (modified timestamps, injected shellcode)** — signs of malicious alteration.
> - **Damaged or corrupted structures** — critical for file recovery.

### 1.3 Why Hexadecimal and Not Decimal?

Computers natively operate in **binary (base-2)**. Each byte contains **8 bits**, which can represent values from **0 to 255 (decimal)**. Writing every byte in binary produces 8 digits per byte — visually exhausting. Hexadecimal (**base-16**, using digits `0`–`9` and `A`–`F`) collapses this elegantly: **two hex digits = exactly one byte**.

$$1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ hex digits} = \text{values from } \texttt{00}_{16} \text{ to } \texttt{FF}_{16}$$

$$\texttt{FF}_{16} = 15 \times 16^1 + 15 \times 16^0 = 240 + 15 = 255_{10} = 11111111_2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "**Nibble = 1 hex digit, Byte = 2 hex digits, Word = 4 hex digits, Double Word = 8 hex digits**." This is a frequently tested KTU viva question.

### 1.4 Standard Forensic Metrics Used in Hex Analysis

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **File Signature Offset** | `0x00` (start) to `0x0A` (typical) | Identifies true file type regardless of extension |
| **Cluster Size (NTFS default)** | **4096 bytes (`0x1000`)** | Minimum allocation unit; governs slack space size |
| **Sector Size** | **512 bytes (`0x200`) or 4096 bytes (Advanced Format)** | Physical read unit on disk |
| **Hex Display Width (default)** | **16 bytes per row** | Standard editor convention |
| **MD5 Hash Length** | **128 bits = 32 hex chars** | File integrity verification |
| **SHA-1 Hash Length** | **160 bits = 40 hex chars** | File integrity verification |
| **SHA-256 Hash Length** | **256 bits = 64 hex chars** | File integrity verification (forensically preferred) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Synchronized Viewpanes of Hex Workshop

When a file is opened in Hex Workshop, the analyst is presented with **three parallel columns** that are mathematically synchronized — the same offset is shown in three different numeral systems.

| Viewpane | Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Hexadecimal (Hex) View** | `00`–`FF` per byte | Primary forensic view; compact, byte-aligned |
| **Decimal View** | `0`–`255` per byte | Cross-verification with header specifications |
| **Text (ASCII / EBCDIC) View** | Printable chars (0x20–0x7E) | Quick spotting of readable strings, URLs, paths |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "three synchronized" matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently test whether students can **mentally translate** between the three columns. Example: At offset `0x10`, hex `50 4B 03 04` corresponds to ASCII characters `P K` and the non-printable control characters `ETX` and `EOT` — which together form the **ZIP file magic number**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Workflow of a Forensic Hex Analysis

The KTU 2024 lab manual prescribes the following analytical flow. Each step has a forensic *why*:

1. **Acquire the evidence image** (using `dd`, `FTK Imager`, or `EnCase`) — never work on the original. Compute the **SHA-256 hash** of the original and verify after every operation.
2. **Open the acquired copy in Hex Workshop** via *File → Open* (or drag-and-drop). Note the file size in bytes displayed in the status bar.
3. **Verify the file signature (magic bytes)** at the start of the file. Cross-reference the first 4–16 bytes against a **known signature database** (e.g., `libmagic`, NIST NSRL).
4. **Map the structural regions** — header, payload, footer/trailer, padding. For structured formats (PDF, PE, PNG), the internal layout is documented in the format specification.
5. **Search for human-readable strings** using *Tools → Find → Text* or by visual scanning the ASCII viewpane. Useful artifacts: file paths, registry keys, URLs, error messages, author names.
6. **Search for binary patterns (byte sequences)** using *Tools → Find → Hex*. Useful for locating **deleted file headers** during data carving.
7. **Compare against a known-good baseline** using *Tools → Compare* to detect byte-level tampering.
8. **Compute and record checksums/hashes** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) via *Tools → Checksum*.
9. **Document every offset, every observation, every screenshot** in the chain-of-custody report.

### 2.3 File Signature (Magic Bytes) Quick-Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the first four bytes** of at least these formats. KTU viva questions routinely ask: *"You see the bytes `89 50 4E 47` at offset `0x00` of a file with a `.jpg` extension. What does this mean, and what should you do?"*

| File Format | Magic Bytes (Hex) | ASCII Representation | Offset |
|---|---|---|---|
| **JPEG (JFIF)** | `FF D8 FF E0` | `ÿØÿà` | `0x00` |
| **PNG** | `89 50 4E 47 0D 0A 1A 0A` | `‰PNG␍␊␚␊` | `0x00` |
| **GIF** | `47 49 46 38 39 61` (GIF89a) | `GIF89a` | `0x00` |
| **PDF** | `25 50 44 46 2D` | `%PDF-` | `0x00` |
| **ZIP / DOCX / XLSX / JAR** | `50 4B 03 04` | `PK␃␄` | `0x00` |
| **RAR (v1.5)** | `52 61 72 21 1A 07 00` | `Rar!␚␇␀` | `0x00` |
| **7-Zip** | `37 7A BC AF 27 1C` | `7z¼¯'␜` | `0x00` |
| **Windows PE (EXE/DLL)** | `4D 5A` | `MZ` | `0x00` |
| **ELF (Linux binary)** | `7F 45 4C 46` | `␡ELF` | `0x00` |
| **TIFF (little-endian)** | `49 49 2A 00` | `II*␀` | `0x00` |
| **TIFF (big-endian)** | `4D 4D 00 2A` | `MM␀*` | `0x00` |
| **Microsoft OLE2 (legacy DOC/XLS)** | `D0 CF 11 E0 A1 B1 1A E1` | Non-printable | `0x00` |
| **SQLite database** | `53 51 4C 69 74 65` | `SQLite` | `0x00` |
| **PNG end-of-file marker** | `49 45 4E 44 AE 42 60 82` | `IEND®B` | EOF |

### 2.4 The Magic Number Cross-Reference Procedure (KTU Lab Standard)

$$\text{True File Type} = \text{SignVerify}(\text{MagicBytes}_{0x00..0x0A}, \text{DB}_{\text{NSRL}})$$

$$\text{Discrepancy Flag} = \begin{cases} \texttt{TRUE} & \text{if } \text{MagicBytes} \neq \text{ExpectedDB}_{ext} \\ \texttt{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Forensic Implication of a Discrepancy Flag:** When the magic bytes do **not** match the file extension, the file is a candidate for being:
> 1. A **disguised executable** (e.g., a `photo.jpg` that is actually a renamed `.exe` for malware delivery).
> 2. A **corrupted file** (truncated header, disk error).
> 3. A **deliberately hidden payload** (polyglot files exploiting parser differential attacks).

### 2.5 Hex Workshop Tool Feature Map

| Feature | Menu Path | Forensic Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Open File** | `File → Open` | Load evidence file (read-only recommended) |
| **Open from Hex String** | `File → Open from Hex String` | Reconstruct file from known byte stream (data carving) |
| **Find Text** | `Tools → Find → Text` | Locate strings, URLs, registry keys |
| **Find Hex** | `Tools → Find → Hex` | Locate magic bytes, shellcode patterns |
| **Replace** | `Edit → Replace` | Modify bytes (requires write access; document every change) |
| **Compare** | `Tools → Compare` | Diff two files byte-by-byte (detect tampering) |
| **Checksum** | `Tools → Checksum` | Compute MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256 for integrity |
| **Byte Statistics** | `Tools → Statistics` | Frequency analysis (useful for crypto identification) |
| **Sector/Cluster View** | `View → Sector/Cluster` | Visualize disk-level allocation |
| **Bookmarks** | `Edit → Bookmark` | Mark offsets of interest for the report |
| **Export to File** | `File → Export` | Extract carved data to a new evidence file |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility Beyond Forensics

The same hex-analysis skillset is used by:
- **Reverse Engineers (malware analysts)** to disassemble executable headers and identify packing/obfuscation.
- **Embedded Systems Engineers** to inspect firmware images, EEPROM dumps, and bootloader binaries.
- **Network Engineers** to inspect raw packet captures (PCAP) at the byte level.
- **CTF (Capture-The-Flag) Competitors** for the *"Forensics"* and *"Misc"* challenge categories — a direct bridge to KTU's Cyber Forensics lab objectives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Worked Forensic Examples

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** Hex Analysis is a *skill-based* lab topic, not a formula-based theoretical one. The "derivations" below are therefore **analytical procedures** — step-by-step forensic protocols that a KTU student must internalize and reproduce in the lab record and the university exam.

### 3.1 Worked Procedure A: Magic-Number Verification of a Suspicious File

**Scenario:** During a live forensic triage, a security analyst discovers a file named `annual_report.pdf` on a suspect's USB drive. The file extension claims PDF, but a quick visual inspection of the file size (only **8 KB**) seems unusual. The analyst must verify the true file type.

**Step 1 — Hash the original file for chain of custody.**

The MD5 of the original is computed:

$$H_{\text{MD5}} = \text{MD5}(\text{annual\_report.pdf}) = \texttt{a3f1c9d47b8e02f5c1d9ab340e8a712c}$$

The SHA-256 of the original is:

$$H_{\text{SHA-256}} = \text{SHA256}(\text{annual\_report.pdf}) = \texttt{4f8a...e9b2} \quad \text{(truncated for brevity)}$$

These hashes are **recorded in the chain-of-custody log** and verified at the end of the analysis.

**Step 2 — Open the file in Hex Workshop** (`File → Open`). The status bar shows the file size: **8192 bytes = `0x2000`**.

**Step 3 — Read the first 16 bytes of the file** (the default first row in Hex Workshop).

Observed hex bytes at offset `0x00`:

| Offset | Hex | Decimal | ASCII |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0x00` | `4D` | `77` | `M` |
| `0x01` | `5A` | `90` | `Z` |
| `0x02` | `90` | `144` | Non-printable |
| `0x03` | `00` | `0` | Non-printable |
| `0x04` | `03` | `3` | Non-printable |
| `0x05` | `00` | `0` | Non-printable |
| `0x06` | `00` | `0` | Non-printable |
| `0x07` | `00` | `0` | Non-printable |
| `0x08` | `04` | `4` | Non-printable |
| `0x09` | `00` | `0` | Non-printable |
| `0x0A` | `00` | `0` | Non-printable |
| `0x0B` | `00` | `0` | Non-printable |
| `0x0C` | `FF` | `255` | Non-printable |
| `0x0D` | `FF` | `255` | Non-printable |
| `0x0E` | `00` | `0` | Non-printable |
| `0x0F` | `00` | `0` | Non-printable |

**Step 4 — Interpret the magic bytes.**

The first two hex bytes are `4D 5A`, whose ASCII representation is the characters `M` and `Z` — this is the canonical **DOS / Windows MZ executable header** (named after **Mark Zbikowski**, the original architect of MS-DOS).

**Step 5 — Cross-reference with the known-signature table.**

From the reference table in §2.3, the file format expected to produce `4D 5A` at offset `0x00` is the **Windows Portable Executable (PE)** — used for `.exe` and `.dll` files.

**Step 6 — Compare the observed signature with the extension.**

$$\text{Expected Magic for `.pdf`} = \texttt{25 50 44 46} \; (=\text{`%PDF`})$$

$$\text{Observed Magic} = \texttt{4D 5A} \; (=\text{`MZ`})$$

$$\text{Match?} = \texttt{25 50 44 46} \;\neq\; \texttt{4D 5A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Discrepancy Flag} = \texttt{TRUE}$$

**Step 7 — Forensic conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The file `annual_report.pdf` is **NOT a PDF document**. It is a **disguised Windows executable (PE/MZ binary)**. The `.pdf` extension is a social-engineering tactic. The file must be:
> 1. Quarantined in a sandboxed environment.
> 2. Submitted to a malware analysis pipeline (static → dynamic).
> 3. Documented with full hex dump, hash values, and offset annotations in the forensic report.

### 3.2 Worked Procedure B: Manual Conversion Between Hex, Decimal, Binary, and ASCII

**Scenario:** A KTU exam question presents the following bytes from a Hex Workshop dump and asks the student to decode the hidden ASCII string. The bytes (read from the **Hex viewpane**) are:

`48 65 78 20 57 6F 72 6B 73 68 6F 70`

**Step 1 — Read each byte individually.** Each pair of hex digits is exactly **one byte**.

| Hex Digit 1 | Hex Digit 2 | Full Hex | Decimal Conversion | Binary | ASCII |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `4` | `8` | `48` | $4 \times 16 + 8 = 72$ | `01001000` | `H` |
| `6` | `5` | `65` | $6 \times 16 + 5 = 101$ | `01100101` | `e` |
| `7` | `8` | `78` | $7 \times 16 + 8 = 120$ | `01111000` | `x` |
| `2` | `0` | `20` | $2 \times 16 + 0 = 32$ | `00100000` | ` ` (space) |
| `5` | `7` | `57` | $5 \times 16 + 7 = 87$ | `01010111` | `W` |
| `6` | `F` | `6F` | $6 \times 16 + 15 = 111$ | `01101111` | `o` |
| `7` | `2` | `72` | $7 \times 16 + 2 = 114$ | `01110010` | `r` |
| `6` | `B` | `6B` | $6 \times 16 + 11 = 107$ | `01101011` | `k` |
| `7` | `3` | `73` | $7 \times 16 + 3 = 115$ | `01110011` | `s` |
| `6` | `8` | `68` | $6 \times 16 + 8 = 104$ | `01101000` | `h` |
| `6` | `F` | `6F` | $6 \times 16 + 15 = 111$ | `01101111` | `o` |
| `7` | `0` | `70` | $7 \times 16 + 0 = 112$ | `01110000` | `p` |

**Step 2 — Concatenate the ASCII column.**

$$\text{Decoded String} = \text{`H'} + \text{`e'} + \text{`x'} + \text{` '} + \text{`W'} + \text{`o'} + \text{`r'} + \text{`k'} + \text{`s'} + \text{`h'} + \text{`o'} + \text{`p'}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Decoded String} = \text{`Hex Workshop'}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify using Hex Workshop's ASCII viewpane.** The same bytes, when viewed in the right-hand ASCII column, will display the readable string `Hex Workshop` directly — a built-in cross-check of the manual decoding.

### 3.3 Worked Procedure C: SHA-256 Integrity Verification Workflow

**Scenario:** A forensic examiner downloads a suspect image `evidence.img` and must verify it has not been corrupted during transfer.

**Step 1 — Compute SHA-256 in Hex Workshop** (`Tools → Checksum → SHA-256`):

$$H_{\text{SHA-256}}(\text{evidence.img}) = \texttt{e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855}$$

(This is the SHA-256 of an empty file — used here for pedagogical illustration.)

**Step 2 — Compute SHA-256 on the destination system** using an independent tool (e.g., `sha256sum` on Linux or `Get-FileHash` on PowerShell):

$$H'_{\text{SHA-256}}(\text{evidence.img}) = \texttt{e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855}$$

**Step 3 — Compare the two hashes byte-by-byte:**

$$\text{Verification} = \begin{cases} \texttt{VERIFIED} & \text{if } H = H' \\ \texttt{CORRUPTED — Re-acquire} & \text{if } H \neq H' \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit-flip analogy:** Even a **single-bit change** anywhere in the file (e.g., flipping bit 7 of byte `0x00`) will produce a **completely different** SHA-256 hash (avalanche effect). This is precisely why hashes are the gold standard for forensic integrity.

### 3.4 Worked Procedure D: File Comparison (Diffing) to Detect Tampering

**Scenario:** Two files — `original.docx` (seized from the suspect's machine) and `reported.docx` (attached to the FIR complaint) — must be compared for tampering.

**Step 1 — Open both files in Hex Workshop.**

**Step 2 — Invoke `Tools → Compare` and select the two files.**

**Step 3 — Interpret the output.**

| Offset (Hex) | Original Byte | Reported Byte | Forensic Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0x00–0x01` | `50 4B` | `50 4B` | ZIP container header — identical ✓ |
| `0x1A5B` | `4E` | `4D` | Modified name character `N` → `M` |
| `0x1A5C` | `65` | `61` | `e` → `a` |
| `0x1A5D` | `77` | `6C` | `w` → `l` |
| `0x1A5E` | `20` | `6C` | ` ` → `l` |
| `0x1A5F` | `6E` | `20` | `n` → ` ` |
| `0x1A60` | `61` | `66` | `a` → `f` |
| `0x1A61` | `6D` | `69` | `m` → `i` |
| `0x1A62` | `65` | `6C` | `e` → `l` |
| `0x1A63` | `00` | `65` | `NUL` → `e` |

**Step 4 — Read the modified bytes as ASCII.**

$$\texttt{4D 61 6C 6C 20 66 69 6C 65} = \text{`Mall file'}$$

The original contained the name `New name` (in the document metadata stored at that offset of the DOCX/ZIP archive), and the reported version contains the tampered name `Mall file`.

**Step 5 — Forensic conclusion.** The file `reported.docx` exhibits **byte-level tampering** at offset `0x1A5B`–`0x1A63`. The integrity of the submitted evidence is compromised. The original `original.docx` is admissible; the tampered copy must be flagged in the chain of custody.

### 3.5 Worked Procedure E: Data Carving Using Hex Search

**Scenario:** A user reports a deleted photograph from a formatted SD card. The examiner must carve it from the raw image using only the **JPEG end-of-image marker** (`FF D9`).

**Step 1 — Open the disk image `sd_card.img` in Hex Workshop.**

**Step 2 — Search for the JPEG end-of-image marker** (`Tools → Find → Hex`, pattern `FF D9`). The first match is found at offset `0x1A4F22`.

**Step 3 — Search backward from the end-marker to find the JPEG start-of-image marker** (`FF D8 FF E0`). The nearest match is at offset `0x1A0004`.

**Step 4 — Calculate the carved file size:**

$$S_{\text{carved}} = (\text{End Offset}) - (\text{Start Offset}) + 2 = (\texttt{0x1A4F22} - \texttt{0x1A0004}) + 2$$

Converting the offsets to decimal:

$$\texttt{0x1A0004}_{16} = 1 \times 16^5 + 10 \times 16^4 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 4 = 1703936$$

$$\texttt{0x1A4F22}_{16} = 1 \times 16^5 + 10 \times 16^4 + 4 \times 16^3 + 15 \times 16^2 + 2 \times 16 + 2 = 1723170$$

$$S_{\text{carved}} = 1723170 - 1703936 + 2 = 19236 \; \text{bytes}$$

**Step 5 — Export the carved bytes** (`File → Export`, range `0x1A0004`–`0x1A4F23`) to `recovered_photo.jpg`. Validate the recovered file by re-opening it in an image viewer.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** Always carve **at least 64 extra bytes before** the detected start-of-file marker, because some formats (notably TIFF and AVI variants) carry useful metadata slightly upstream of the formal signature.

### 3.6 Worked Procedure F: Identifying Endianness in a Hex Dump

A 32-bit integer value (representing, for instance, a file size) is stored in a header. Determine the byte order.

**Hex dump at the relevant offset:**

$$\texttt{0x00 1A 2B 3C 4D}$$

**Test for little-endian (LSB-first, the dominant x86/x64 convention):**

$$\text{Value} = \texttt{4D} \times 16^6 + \texttt{3C} \times 16^4 + \texttt{2B} \times 16^2 + \texttt{1A}$$

$$= 77 \times 16777216 + 60 \times 65536 + 43 \times 256 + 26 = 1291845658$$

**Test for big-endian (MSB-first, network byte order):**

$$\text{Value} = \texttt{1A} \times 16^6 + \texttt{2B} \times 16^4 + \texttt{3C} \times 16^2 + \texttt{4D}$$

$$= 26 \times 16777216 + 43 \times 65536 + 60 \times 256 + 77 = 439086141$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical decision rule:** On a Windows forensic image where the source OS is unknown, the analyst must consult the format specification. For BMP, the file-size field is **little-endian**; for PNG/JPEG, multi-byte integers are **big-endian** (network byte order).

### 4.2 Forensic Hex Analysis — Standard Operating Procedure Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[STEP 1 EVIDENCE ACQUISITION DD OR FTK IMAGER] --> p2[STEP 2 COMPUTE HASH OF ORIGINAL SHA-256]
    p2 --> p3[STEP 3 OPEN WORKING COPY IN HEX WORKSHOP READ ONLY]
    p3 --> p4[STEP 4 INSPECT MAGIC BYTES AT OFFSET 0X00]
    p4 --> p5{DO MAGIC BYTES MATCH EXTENSION}

    p5 -->|YES MATCH| p6[STEP 5A PROCEED TO STRUCTURAL MAPPING]
    p5 -->|NO MISMATCH| p7[STEP 5B FLAG FILE AS DISGUISED OR CORRUPTED]
    p7 --> p8[STEP 5C QUARANTINE AND DEEP ANALYSIS]

    p6 --> p9[STEP 6 SCAN ASCII PANE FOR STRINGS]
    p9 --> p10[STEP 7 HEX SEARCH FOR KNOWN PATTERNS]
    p10 --> p11[STEP 8 BYTE COMPARE AGAINST BASELINE]
    p11 --> p12[STEP 9 CHECKSUM VERIFICATION]
    p12 --> p13[STEP 10 DOCUMENT FINDINGS WITH OFFSETS AND HASHES]
    p13 --> p14[STEP 11 UPDATE CHAIN OF CUSTODY LOG]

    p8 --> p14
    p14 --> endA([EVIDENCE READY FOR LEGAL PROCEEDING])
```

### 4.3 File Memory Layout — Generalized Structural Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zoneA[ZONE 1 FILE HEADER MAGIC BYTES AND METADATA OFFSET 0X00] --> zoneB[ZONE 2 STRUCTURAL CHUNK HEADERS E.G. PNG IHDR OR PE COFF]
    zoneB --> zoneC[ZONE 3 PAYLOAD DATA OFFSET VARIES BY FORMAT]
    zoneC --> zoneD[ZONE 4 OPTIONAL METADATA E.G. EXIF OR AUTHORS]
    zoneD --> zoneE[ZONE 5 TRAILER OR FOOTER E.G. PNG IEND OR ZIP END OF CENTRAL DIRECTORY]
    zoneE --> zoneF[ZONE 6 PADDING AND SLACK TO CLUSTER BOUNDARY]

    zoneA -.-> noteA[FORENSICALLY CRITICAL FIRST 16 BYTES]
    zoneC -.-> noteB[VOLATILE MAY CONTAIN USER DATA]
    zoneE -.-> noteC[CRITICAL FOR CARVING CONFIRMS FILE COMPLETENESS]
    zoneF -.-> noteD[MAY LEAK RESIDUAL DATA FROM PREVIOUS FILE]
```

### 4.4 Three-Viewpane Synchronization Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bufA[BYTE AT OFFSET 0X10] --> vhex[HEX VIEW: 5A]
    bufA --> vdec[DECIMAL VIEW: 90]
    bufA --> vasc[ASCII VIEW: Z]

    vhex --> rel1[VALUE 5A IN HEX BASE 16]
    vdec --> rel2[VALUE 90 IN DECIMAL BASE 10]
    vasc --> rel3[CHARACTER Z IN ASCII TABLE]

    rel1 --> math[ARITHMETIC CONVERSION FORMULA]
    rel2 --> math
    rel3 --> math

    math --> formula[5A hex equals 5 times 16 plus 10 equals 90 decimal equals ASCII 90 which is letter Z]
```

### 4.5 Decision Tree: File Signature Mismatch Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    obs[OBSERVED MAGIC BYTES AT 0X00] --> cmp[COMPARE WITH KNOWN SIGNATURE DATABASE]
    cmp --> q1{DO OBSERVED BYTES MATCH EXTENSION}

    q1 -->|MATCH| r1[FILE IS CONSISTENT WITH ITS EXTENSION]
    q1 -->|MISMATCH| r2[FILE IS INCONSISTENT WITH ITS EXTENSION]

    r2 --> cat{WHAT IS THE LIKELY CAUSE}
    cat -->|CAUSE A MALWARE DISGUISE| act1[QUARANTINE SUBMIT TO SANDBOX REVERSE ENGINEER]
    cat -->|CAUSE B FILE CORRUPTION| act2[ATTEMPT REPAIR OR RECOVERY FROM BACKUP]
    cat -->|CAUSE C POLYGLOT FILE| act3[PARSE WITH MULTIPLE PARSERS COMPARE RESULTS]
    cat -->|CAUSE D STEGANOGRAPHIC PAYLOAD| act4[RUN STEGANOGRAPHY DETECTION TOOLS]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Bank Note:** Questions are modeled on KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) and Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) patterns. Marks are distributed per the official KTU template (Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks with internal choice and 7+7 sub-parts).

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**A1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`**

Define the term **"Magic Bytes"** in the context of digital forensics. Why are they considered more trustworthy indicators of a file's true type than the file extension? Give **two** real-world examples of magic bytes and their corresponding file formats.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Magic bytes (also called file signatures or magic numbers) are **fixed, predefined sequences of bytes located at the beginning (and sometimes the end) of a file** that uniquely identify the file's true format, irrespective of the file extension. They are considered more trustworthy than the file extension because (1) extensions are trivially editable by any user, and (2) malware authors routinely **rename executable files with harmless extensions** (e.g., `invoice.pdf.exe` displayed as `invoice.pdf` on Windows) to deceive users. Magic bytes, in contrast, are embedded in the file structure by the authoring application and cannot be changed without corrupting the file.

**Example 1:** `25 50 44 46 2D` (ASCII: `%PDF-`) — identifies a **PDF** document at offset `0x00`.

**Example 2:** `50 4B 03 04` (ASCII: `PK␃␄`) — identifies a **ZIP archive** (also used internally by `.docx`, `.xlsx`, `.jar`, `.apk`).

---

**A2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]`**

List and briefly explain the **three synchronized viewpanes** presented in Hex Workshop when a file is opened. How do they aid a forensic analyst in cross-verifying the contents of a binary file?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three synchronized viewpanes in Hex Workshop are:

1. **Hexadecimal Viewpane** — Displays each byte as exactly two hex digits (range `00`–`FF`). This is the **primary forensic view** because it is the most compact and byte-aligned representation. *(1 mark)*

2. **Decimal Viewpane** — Displays the same bytes as decimal integers (range `0`–`255`). Useful for **cross-referencing** with documentation that specifies field values in decimal (e.g., the IHDR chunk of a PNG declares its width and height as 4-byte big-endian decimal integers). *(1 mark)*

3. **ASCII / EBCDIC Text Viewpane** — Displays the byte as a printable character wherever possible (range `0x20`–`0x7E`), and as a dot for non-printable bytes. This allows rapid visual spotting of **human-readable strings** such as file paths, URLs, error messages, and author names. *(1 mark)*

Because the three panes are **synchronized to the same offset**, an analyst can verify a finding in two numeral systems simultaneously, dramatically reducing the risk of misreading.

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **architecture of Hex Workshop** as a forensic analysis tool. Explain the role of its **Checksum Engine** and **Compare (Diff) Module** in establishing the **integrity** and **authenticity** of digital evidence.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During a forensic examination of a USB drive, you find a file named `mydocument.pdf` whose size is **12,288 bytes (`0x3000`)**. When opened in Hex Workshop, the **first 8 bytes** at offset `0x00` are:

$$\texttt{52 61 72 21 1A 07 01 00}$$

**(i)** Identify the **true file type** based on the magic bytes. Justify your answer using the hex-to-ASCII decoding.

**(ii)** Is the file extension `.pdf` consistent with the actual content? Justify.

**(iii)** What is the **most likely forensic concern** in this case, and what immediate action should the examiner take?

---

**Model Answer — Question A:**

**(a) Architecture of Hex Workshop (7 Marks):**

Hex Workshop is a Windows-based **hexadecimal editor** organized around a central **in-memory byte buffer** that holds the contents of the opened file. Three synchronized viewpanes (Hex, Decimal, ASCII) render this buffer to the user. Auxiliary modules operate on the same buffer:

- The **Checksum Engine** (`Tools → Checksum`) computes one-way cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) of the file's byte stream. These hashes act as **unique digital fingerprints**; any subsequent bit-flip will produce a completely different hash (avalanche property). The hash computed at acquisition is **recorded in the chain-of-custody log** and **re-verified** after every analysis step. Hash match ⇒ **integrity preserved**; hash mismatch ⇒ **evidence tampered or corrupted**. *(3 marks)*

- The **Compare / Diff Module** (`Tools → Compare`) takes two files as input and performs a **byte-by-byte XOR-style comparison**, reporting all differing offsets. This module is critical for establishing **authenticity** by detecting tampering. For example, if a submitted PDF has been doctored to change a name, an invoice total, or a date, the diff module pinpoints exactly which bytes differ between the **original evidence copy** and the **challenged copy**, producing a court-admissible list of tampered offsets. *(3 marks)*

- The role of both modules is therefore complementary: **hashes prove the file has not changed overall**; **diffs prove which specific bytes have changed** (or prove that none have). *(1 mark)*

*(A diagram similar to §4.1 above should be redrawn in the answer script.)*

**(b) Magic-Byte Analysis (7 Marks):**

**(i) True file type (2 marks):**

Decode each byte to its ASCII character:

$$\texttt{52}_{16} = 5 \times 16 + 2 = 82_{10} = \text{`R'}$$

$$\texttt{61}_{16} = 6 \times 16 + 1 = 97_{10} = \text{`a'}$$

$$\texttt{72}_{16} = 7 \times 16 + 2 = 114_{10} = \text{`r'}$$

$$\texttt{21}_{16} = 2 \times 16 + 1 = 33_{10} = \text{`!'}$$

$$\texttt{1A}_{16} = 1 \times 16 + 10 = 26_{10} = \text{non-printable (SUB control char)}$$

$$\texttt{07}_{16} = 0 \times 16 + 7 = 7_{10} = \text{non-printable (BEL control char)}$$

$$\texttt{01}_{16} = 0 \times 16 + 1 = 1_{10} = \text{non-printable (SOH control char)}$$

$$\texttt{00}_{16} = 0_{10} = \text{non-printable (NUL control char)}$$

Concatenating the printable characters yields the ASCII string **`Rar!`**, followed by control bytes. This is the canonical magic signature for the **RAR archive format (version 1.5 onwards)**, officially defined as `Rar!\x1A\x07\x00` (or `\x01\x00` for RAR 5.0). **True file type: RAR archive.**

**(ii) Extension consistency (2 marks):**

The expected magic bytes for a **PDF** are `25 50 44 46` (ASCII: `%PDF`). The observed bytes are `52 61 72 21` (ASCII: `Rar!`). These do **not** match.

$$\text{Expected} = \texttt{25 50 44 46} \;\neq\; \text{Observed} = \texttt{52 61 72 21} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Discrepancy} = \texttt{TRUE}$$

**The `.pdf` extension is INCONSISTENT with the file content.** The file is a **disguised RAR archive**.

**(iii) Forensic concern and immediate action (3 marks):**

**Most likely concern:** The file is a **renamed RAR archive masquerading as a PDF** — a common social-engineering / malware-delivery technique. The RAR may contain malicious executables, contraband data, or exfiltrated evidence compressed to evade superficial inspection. The non-printable control bytes (`0x1A`, `0x07`, `0x01`, `0x00`) at the start confirm the binary header integrity and rule out accidental corruption.

**Immediate actions the examiner must take:**

1. **Do NOT open the file** in any standard archive tool on the host machine — it could trigger a malicious payload. *(1 mark)*
2. **Compute and record the SHA-256 hash** of the file in the chain-of-custody log. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Quarantine the file** in an isolated, write-protected forensic sandbox and submit it to a **password-protected extraction workflow** (since RAR files are often password-protected) and to **static and dynamic malware analysis pipelines**. *(1 mark)*
4. **Document the finding** (offset, bytes, ASCII interpretation, expected vs observed, forensic conclusion) in the official report with a screenshot of the Hex Workshop view. *(0.5 mark)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **Magic Number / File Signature**? Construct a **comparison table** of at least **six common file formats** with their magic bytes (in hex), the corresponding ASCII interpretation, and the offset at which the signature occurs. Explain the **forensic significance** of detecting a mismatch between the signature and the file extension, with **two real-world attack scenarios**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A forensic image of a 16 GB SD card is opened in Hex Workshop. The analyst needs to **carve a deleted JPEG photograph** from the raw image. Describe the **complete step-by-step procedure** for performing this carving operation, including the search patterns, the offset arithmetic, the export step, and the post-carving validation.

---

**Model Answer — Question B:**

**(a) Magic Numbers and File Signatures (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A magic number (or file signature) is a **constant, well-known byte sequence at a fixed offset within a file** that uniquely identifies the file's true format. It is embedded by the authoring application and is independent of the (user-editable) file extension.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| File Format | Magic Bytes (Hex) | ASCII | Offset |
|---|---|---|---|
| JPEG | `FF D8 FF E0` | `ÿØÿà` | `0x00` |
| PNG | `89 50 4E 47` | `‰PNG` | `0x00` |
| GIF | `47 49 46 38 39 61` | `GIF89a` | `0x00` |
| PDF | `25 50 44 46 2D` | `%PDF-` | `0x00` |
| ZIP / DOCX | `50 4B 03 04` | `PK` + control | `0x00` |
| Windows EXE (PE) | `4D 5A` | `MZ` | `0x00` |
| RAR | `52 61 72 21 1A 07` | `Rar!` + control | `0x00` |

**Forensic significance of a signature-extension mismatch (3 marks):**

A mismatch indicates that the file has been **deliberately mislabelled** or is **corrupted**. Two real-world attack scenarios:

1. **Malware Disguise (Double-Extension Attack):** A user receives an email attachment `Invoice.pdf.exe`. Windows may hide the trailing `.exe` and display only `Invoice.pdf`, leading the victim to believe it is a document. When opened, the file executes as a Windows PE binary (magic `4D 5A`). The signature mismatch (the file is PE, not PDF) is the forensic smoking gun that proves intent to deceive. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Polyglot / Steganographic Hiding:** An attacker concatenates a malicious payload (e.g., a ZIP archive) to a legitimate JPEG file. The file opens normally as an image viewer, but the trailing `50 4B 03 04` signature is detectable using Hex Workshop's hex-search function. The hidden archive may contain additional malicious files. This is a classic **steganography-by-concatenation** attack used in advanced persistent threats (APTs). *(1.5 marks)*

**(b) Data Carving Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Evidence preparation (1 mark):**
Create a **forensic image** of the SD card using `dd` (Linux) or `FTK Imager` (Windows). Compute the **SHA-256 hash** of the image and the original SD card; verify they match. **Work only on the image**, never on the original.

**Step 2 — Open the image in Hex Workshop** (`File → Open`, file type: `All Files *.*`).

**Step 3 — Search for the JPEG end-of-image (EOI) marker** (1 mark):
- Navigate to `Tools → Find → Hex`.
- Enter the search pattern: `FF D9`.
- Note the **first match** at some offset, say `0x2B4A12`.

**Step 4 — Search for the JPEG start-of-image (SOI) marker** (1 mark):
- Search backward from offset `0x2B4A12` for the pattern `FF D8 FF E0` (or `FF D8 FF E1` for Exif JPEGs).
- Note the **nearest preceding match**, say `0x2A0004`.

**Step 5 — Compute the carve window** (1 mark):
Convert hex offsets to decimal, subtract, and add 2 (to include both EOI bytes):

$$S = (\text{End}_{10} - \text{Start}_{10}) + 2$$

**Step 6 — Export the carved bytes** (1 mark):
Use `File → Export` (or `Edit → Copy Range` followed by `File → New` and `Paste`) to save the bytes from `Start` through `End+1` as `recovered_photo.jpg`.

**Step 7 — Post-carving validation (2 marks):**

1. **Hash the carved file** with SHA-256 and record in the case log.
2. **Re-open the carved JPEG** in a forensic image viewer (e.g., `exiftool` for metadata, an image viewer for visual integrity).
3. **Verify the file's magic bytes** at offset `0x00` are now `FF D8 FF E0` — this confirms the carve window was correctly aligned.
4. **Cross-check the metadata** (EXIF timestamps, GPS coordinates, camera model) to establish provenance and link the photograph to a specific device, time, and location — completing the chain of evidence.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (High-Yield):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse `Tools → Find → Text` with `Tools → Find → Hex`.** They serve different purposes. Text search works on the ASCII viewpane (case-sensitive substring); Hex search works on raw bytes (use a space or no separator between byte pairs, e.g., `FFD8FFE0`).
> 2. **Do NOT use decimal offsets in the Find dialog.** Hex Workshop's default offset display is hexadecimal. Mixing bases is the single most common arithmetic error in KTU lab records.
> 3. **Do NOT forget the +2 byte offset adjustment** when carving JPEG files. The EOI marker (`FF D9`) is **two bytes**; failing to include both will corrupt the carved file's footer.
> 4. **Do NOT modify the original evidence file.** All Hex Workshop operations that mutate data (Replace, Fill) must be performed on a **working copy**; the original is preserved read-only and re-hashed after analysis.
> 5. **Do NOT report an ASCII string without quoting the offset** where it was found. KTU evaluators deduct marks for offset-less observations.
> 6. **Endianness confusion** is a frequent 1-mark loss: PNG/JPEG use big-endian; BMP/PE/most x86 executables use little-endian. Always state your endianness assumption explicitly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Hexadecimal** is base-16 using digits `0`–`9`, `A`–`F`. Two hex digits = exactly **one byte** (8 bits).
- ✅ **Hex Workshop** is a Windows hex editor with three synchronized viewpanes: **Hex / Decimal / ASCII**.
- ✅ **Magic bytes** are fixed file signatures at known offsets (usually `0x00`) that reveal the **true file type**, independent of the extension.
- ✅ A **signature–extension mismatch** is a **red flag** for malware disguise, corruption, or steganographic hiding.
- ✅ **Key signatures to memorize:** `FF D8 FF` (JPEG), `89 50 4E 47` (PNG), `25 50 44 46` (PDF), `50 4B 03 04` (ZIP), `4D 5A` (PE/EXE), `52 61 72 21` (RAR).
- ✅ **Hash functions** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) provide **integrity** verification via the **avalanche effect**.
- ✅ **Compare / Diff module** provides **authenticity** verification by pinpointing tampered bytes.
- ✅ **Data carving** uses **search for end-marker → search backward for start-marker → export slice → validate**.
- ✅ **Chain of custody** is paramount: **hash the original, work on a copy, re-hash on completion, log every offset**.
- ✅ **Endianness:** little-endian = LSB-first (x86); big-endian = MSB-first (network, PNG, JPEG). Always declare your assumption.
- ✅ **Hex Workshop's Find dialog uses hexadecimal offsets and hex byte patterns by default** — confirm before each search.
- ✅ **Default row width** in Hex Workshop is **16 bytes**, aligning neatly with the **16-byte MD5 input block** — a useful cross-check.
- ✅ **Forensic lab mantra:** *Acquire → Hash → Open → Inspect → Document → Re-hash → Preserve.*

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bit-Level Forensic Analysis** is the microscopic, granular-level examination of an evidential image (such as `.jpg`, `.png`, `.bmp`, `.gif`, `.raw`, or `.dd` disk image) by interpreting the raw **binary bits and bytes** that constitute the file at its most fundamental structural layer, in order to validate file integrity, identify tampering, recover hidden data, extract metadata, and establish forensic authenticity for court-admissible evidence.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Cyber Forensics Lab, this experiment involves using a commercial or open-source forensic suite — **FTK (Forensic Toolkit)**, **EnCase**, or **ProDiscover** — to load an image file as evidence, compute cryptographic hash values, parse internal file structures (headers, magic bytes, data segments, trailers), analyze slack space, scan for steganographic payloads, and generate a verifiable forensic report.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Sealed Letter" Model of a Digital Image:**
> Imagine a physical sealed envelope (the image file). To a normal user, only the printed letter inside is visible. To a forensic examiner, however, the envelope itself is the evidence. The examiner inspects the **envelope paper (file header)**, the **wax seal (digital signature/hash)**, the **paperweight (EXIF metadata)**, any **invisible ink (steganography)**, and any **marks on the back (trailer/footer bytes)**. Bit-level analysis is the equivalent of holding this envelope under ultraviolet light, weighing it on a precision scale, and comparing its wax seal against a known reference.

A computer file is nothing more than a long sequence of **0s and 1s** organized into 8-bit **bytes**. Every byte has a forensic meaning. The first few bytes form a **Magic Number / File Signature** that identifies the file type, the middle bytes contain the actual image data, and the last bytes may contain a trailer with checksums or hidden payloads. Forensic tools read these bytes byte-by-byte and present them to the investigator in a human-readable, decoded, and searchable form.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Emphasis on Standard Metrics)

- **Bit (b):** The smallest unit of data, value `0` or `1`.
- **Byte (B):** A group of **8 bits**, ranges from `0x00` (0) to `0xFF` (255).
- **File Signature / Magic Number:** A constant byte sequence at the start of a file used to identify its format (e.g., JPEG always begins with `FF D8 FF`).
- **Hash Function:** A one-way cryptographic function that maps arbitrary input to a fixed-length output. Standards used in this lab: **MD5 (128-bit)**, **SHA-1 (160-bit)**, **SHA-256 (256-bit)**.
- **Endianness:** Byte-ordering convention. **Little-Endian (LE)** stores the least significant byte first (used by Intel x86); **Big-Endian (BE)** stores the most significant byte first (used in network protocols and JPEG/JFIF).
- **Slack Space:** The unused space between the logical end of a file and the end of its last allocated disk cluster.
- **EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format):** A metadata standard embedded in image files by cameras and smartphones.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The lab explicitly requires students to *interpret* the bit pattern, not just generate it. Marks are awarded for explaining *why* a specific byte pattern indicates tampering.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Forensic Model of an Image File

A digital image, when analyzed at bit level, is composed of three forensic layers. Each tool (FTK, EnCase, ProDiscover) is designed to surface these layers to the examiner.

| Layer | Forensic Content | Tool Representation |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Container Layer** | File header, magic bytes, file size, MIME type, end-of-file marker | FTK "File Header" pane; EnCase "View > File Content"; ProDiscover "Image Header" |
| **L2 — Metadata Layer** | EXIF tags, GPS, camera model, timestamps, ICC color profile, XMP/IPTC | FTK "Properties" tab; EnCase "Record Structure"; ProDiscover "Metadata View" |
| **L3 — Payload Layer** | Pixel data, hidden regions, slack space, steganographic bits, appended files | FTK "Hex View"; EnCase "Disk View + Picture Finder"; ProDiscover "Cluster View" |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Forensic Workflow (Why & How)

1. **Acquisition (How):** The original image is acquired in **forensic soundness** by creating a bit-stream copy (`.E01`, `.DD`, or `.RAW`). 
   - **Why:** Working on the original risks altering it and breaking the chain of custody.

2. **Hashing (How):** The forensic tool computes cryptographic hashes of the acquired image.
   - **Why:** Hashes act as a unique digital fingerprint. Any single-bit modification changes the hash completely (**Avalanche Effect**).

3. **Header Inspection (How):** The tool opens the image in **Hex (hexadecimal) view**.
   - **Why:** Magic bytes confirm the file type declared in the extension. A `.jpg` not starting with `FF D8 FF` is a red flag.

4. **Metadata Parsing (How):** EXIF tags are extracted and cross-verified with OS timestamps.
   - **Why:** A photo claiming to be from 2018 with a "Date Modified" of 2024 indicates possible anti-forensic manipulation.

5. **Slack & Trailing Data Analysis (How):** The tool scans beyond the logical end of the file.
   - **Why:** Attackers often hide data (a second image, a ZIP, or malware) past the JPEG end-marker `FF D9`.

6. **Steganography / Anomaly Detection (How):** LSB (Least Significant Bit) patterns and entropy anomalies are flagged.
   - **Why:** A normal image has uniform LSB distribution; stego-images exhibit statistical bias.

## 2.3 The Cryptographic Hashing Engine — The Heart of Bit-Level Forensics

The integrity of every forensic workflow rests on the **hash function**. For any arbitrary input message $M$ of length $L$ bits, a cryptographic hash function $H$ produces a fixed-length digest $d$:

$$ d = H(M), \quad L \geq 1 \text{ bit},\ d \in \{0,1\}^{n} $$

where $n$ is the output bit-length of the algorithm. The three properties that make a hash *forensically useful* are:

1. **Pre-image Resistance:** Given $d$, finding $M$ is computationally infeasible.
2. **Second Pre-image Resistance:** Given $M_1$, finding $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ is infeasible.
3. **Collision Resistance:** Finding any pair $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ is infeasible.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the industry, bit-level analysis is the bedrock of:

- **Digital Rights Management (DRM):** Verifying watermarks in copyrighted images.
- **Incident Response (IR):** Confirming whether a phishing screenshot was fabricated.
- **E-Discovery & Litigation:** Producing court-admissible evidence with hash-verified chain-of-custody (CFR Title 28, Rule 26 compliance).
- **Military & Intelligence:** Detecting Covert Channels in image attachments where LSB carries C2 (Command & Control) data.
- **Insurance Claim Investigation:** Verifying timestamp and GPS authenticity of damage photographs.
