<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Hash Code Generation and Comparison of Files Using HashCalc

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cryptographic Hash Function (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> A *cryptographic hash function* is a deterministic mathematical algorithm that accepts an input (or 'message') of arbitrary length and returns a fixed-size string of bytes, typically a *digest*, *hash value*, or *hash code*. The output is uniquely representative of the input data such that any unintentional or intentional modification of the input will, with overwhelming probability, produce a different output.

A **Hash Code** (also called a *message digest*, *digital fingerprint*, or *checksum* in forensics terminology) is the output of a hash function. It is rendered as a hexadecimal string of fixed length regardless of the source file's actual size.

**HashCalc** is a popular, lightweight, Windows-based utility developed by *SlavaSoft* that simultaneously computes the message digests of a file using multiple algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512, CRC32, RIPEMD-160, etc.) in a single graphical user interface. It is widely deployed in digital forensic laboratories for **evidence authentication** and **chain-of-custody integrity verification**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Human Fingerprint of a File":**
> Imagine every person in the world has a unique fingerprint. Two different people *cannot* (in practice) have identical fingerprints. Now, suppose you have a paper document — you can stamp it with a unique "fingerprint" that proves it is genuine. If a single character, a single dot of ink, or even a single space on the paper is changed, the resulting fingerprint will be completely different. **A cryptographic hash is exactly this — a unique mathematical "fingerprint" of a digital file.**

Consider a 5 GB movie file and a 2 KB text file. A hash function will:
- Compress them into a small, fixed-length string (e.g., exactly **64 hexadecimal characters** for SHA-256).
- Produce a totally different output if even **a single bit** in the file is altered.

This is why hash values are the **gold standard for evidence integrity** in cyber forensics investigations registered under the **Indian IT Act, 2000** and its subsequent amendments.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

| Algorithm | Output Length (bits) | Hex Characters | Status in 2024 |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------|----------------|
| **MD5** | **128** | **32** | Cryptographically broken — used only for legacy verification |
| **SHA-1** | **160** | **40** | Deprecated by NIST since 2011 — collision attacks demonstrated |
| **SHA-256** | **256** | **64** | **Industry standard — Recommended by NIST (FIPS 180-4)** |
| **SHA-512** | **512** | **128** | High-security variant of SHA-2 family |
| **CRC32** | **32** | **8** | Non-cryptographic; used for error detection only |
| **RIPEMD-160** | **160** | **40** | Used in Bitcoin address generation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** The most frequently asked question in Part A is to identify the hex length of MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 outputs. Memorize: bits $\div 4 = $ hex characters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Properties of a Cryptographic Hash Function

A function $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ is considered *cryptographically secure* if and only if it satisfies the following three properties:

### (a) Pre-image Resistance (One-Way Property)
Given a hash value $h = H(m)$, it must be **computationally infeasible** to find any input $m'$ such that $H(m') = h$.

> **Forensic Significance:** An attacker cannot reverse-engineer the original file from its hash digest.

### (b) Second Pre-image Resistance (Weak Collision Resistance)
Given an input $m_1$, it must be **computationally infeasible** to find a different input $m_2 \neq m_1$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$.

> **Forensic Significance:** An attacker cannot create a "look-alike" malicious file that produces the same hash as the original evidence file.

### (c) Collision Resistance (Strong Collision Resistance)
It must be **computationally infeasible** to find any two distinct inputs $m_1 \neq m_2$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$.

> **Forensic Significance:** Two different files cannot legitimately have the same hash, preserving the integrity of forensic evidence.

### (d) Determinism (Auxiliary Property)
The same input $m$ must *always* produce the *same* output $h$ every time the function is run on any machine in the universe.

### (e) Avalanche Effect (Auxiliary Property)
A change of a single bit in the input must result in a change of approximately **50% of the bits** in the output.

## 2.2 Why Hashing Matters in Cyber Forensics — The Chain of Custody

In a court of law, digital evidence is *inadmissible* unless its integrity can be mathematically proven. The hash function serves three forensic roles:

1. **Integrity Verification:** A hash taken at the *time of seizure* (T1) must match a hash taken at the *time of analysis* (T2). If $H_{\text{T1}} = H_{\text{T2}}$, the evidence is unaltered.
2. **Authentication:** The hash acts as a digital signature of authenticity for the forensic image (e.g., a `.E01` or `.DD` file).
3. **Non-repudiation:** The accused cannot deny tampering because a hash mismatch is a *mathematical proof* of modification.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Memorize This Table**

| Property / Metric | Formula or Value | Engineering Interpretation |
|-------------------|------------------|----------------------------|
| Bit-length to Hex conversion | $\text{Hex chars} = \dfrac{\text{Bits}}{4}$ | 256 bits = 64 hex characters |
| Block size (SHA-256) | **512 bits** | Data processed in 512-bit chunks |
| Word size (SHA-256) | **32 bits** | 64 rounds of processing |
| Rounds (SHA-256) | **64** | Compression iterations |
| MD5 block size | **512 bits** | Processed in 512-bit blocks |
| MD5 rounds | **64** | 4 rounds × 16 operations |
| Theoretical MD5 collisions (birthday attack) | $2^{64}$ | Half the bit-length |
| Theoretical SHA-256 collisions | $2^{128}$ | $2^{n/2}$ for an $n$-bit hash |
| Avalanche threshold | $\approx 50\%$ bit change | Gold standard for hash quality |
| Hexadecimal base | **16** | Digits: 0–9, A–F |
| ASCII byte size | **8 bits = 1 byte** | Standard storage unit |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Digital Forensics (FTK, EnCase, Autopsy):** Every forensic image is hashed at acquisition and verified at every subsequent stage.
- **Blockchain (Bitcoin, Ethereum):** SHA-256 is used in the proof-of-work consensus and to derive wallet addresses.
- **Software Distribution:** Linux ISO images and open-source releases are published with SHA-256 checksums to defeat *Man-in-the-Middle* supply-chain attacks.
- **Password Storage:** Websites store $\text{SHA-256}(\text{salt} \vert\vert \text{password})$ instead of plaintext credentials.
- **Digital Signatures (RSA + SHA-256):** Modern PDF and code-signing certificates hash the document with SHA-256 before encrypting the hash with the sender's private key.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Bit-length to Hex-character Conversion

The hexadecimal number system uses a base of **16**, where each digit represents 4 bits. Therefore, the conversion is:

$$
\text{Total Hex Characters} = \frac{\text{Hash Output in Bits}}{4}
$$

**Derivation Walk-through:**

**Step 1 — Identify the bit-length of the algorithm.**
For SHA-256, the output digest length is $n = 256$ bits.

**Step 2 — Divide by 4 (since 1 hex digit = 4 bits).**

$$
N_{\text{hex}} = \frac{256}{4} = 64 \text{ characters}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-verify with the MD5 case.**

$$
N_{\text{hex, MD5}} = \frac{128}{4} = 32 \text{ characters}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-verify with SHA-1.**

$$
N_{\text{hex, SHA-1}} = \frac{160}{4} = 40 \text{ characters}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-verify with SHA-512.**

$$
N_{\text{hex, SHA-512}} = \frac{512}{4} = 128 \text{ characters}
$$

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation: Theoretical Collision Resistance (Birthday Paradox)

According to the *Birthday Bound Theorem* in probability theory, a collision can be found in a hash function with an $n$-bit output after approximately $2^{n/2}$ trials.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — State the input space size.**
For an $n$-bit hash, there are $2^n$ possible outputs.

**Step 2 — Apply the Birthday Paradox approximation.**
The expected number of random samples required before a duplicate (collision) is observed:

$$
k \approx 1.177 \sqrt{2^n} \approx 2^{n/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate for MD5 (n = 128).**

$$
k_{\text{MD5}} \approx 2^{128/2} = 2^{64} \text{ hash operations}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for SHA-256 (n = 256).**

$$
k_{\text{SHA-256}} \approx 2^{256/2} = 2^{128} \text{ hash operations}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude on practical security.**
Since $2^{128}$ operations is computationally infeasible (estimated at $\approx 10^{38}$ operations), SHA-256 remains cryptographically secure as of **2024**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Avalanche Effect Demonstration

The following analytical matrix demonstrates the **Avalanche Effect** on a sample input. We use the ASCII string `"Hello"` versus `"hello"` — only a **single bit change** (the 6th bit of the first character, i.e., $0 \times 48 \rightarrow 0 \times 68$).

| Property | Input: `Hello` (0x48 65 6C 6C 6F) | Input: `hello` (0x68 65 6C 6C 6F) |
|----------|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Bytes changed | 0 | 1 byte (0x48 → 0x68) |
| Bits changed | 0 | 1 bit |
| SHA-256 digest (first 16 hex chars) | `185f8db32271fe25...` | `2cf24dba5fb0a30e...` |
| Digest (last 16 hex chars) | `...f561a6c7d2a1` | `...26d57f5e7b4a` |
| **Matching hex characters** | 0 of 64 | 0 of 64 |
| **Avalanche confirmed?** | **YES** — 100% of output bits changed | **YES** |

> [!NOTE]
> The above table illustrates that **changing a single bit in the input completely randomizes the entire 256-bit SHA-256 output**. This is the mathematical definition of a *strong avalanche effect*.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Hashing a 1 KB File with SHA-256

**Step 1 — Compute the number of 512-bit blocks required.**

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{File size in bits}}{512} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute 1 KB = 1024 bytes = 8192 bits.**

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{8192}{512} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 16 \right\rceil = 16 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the SHA-256 padding rule.**
After 16 blocks, an additional padding block is added containing:
- A `1` bit followed by `0` bits.
- A 64-bit big-endian integer representing the original message length.

**Step 4 — Total blocks processed = 17.**

$$
\text{Computational cost} = 17 \text{ blocks} \times 64 \text{ rounds} = 1088 \text{ compression rounds}
$$

**Step 5 — Output a 256-bit (64 hex character) digest.**

This is the value that HashCalc displays in its GUI for that file.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: HashCalc Workflow in a Cyber Forensics Investigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Digital Evidence File]:::input --> B[HashCalc Tool Initialization]
    B --> C[Select Hash Algorithms MD5, SHA1, SHA256, SHA512, CRC32, RIPEMD160]
    C --> D[Read File as Binary Stream]
    D --> E[Apply MD5 Algorithm 128-bit output]
    D --> F[Apply SHA1 Algorithm 160-bit output]
    D --> G[Apply SHA256 Algorithm 256-bit output]
    D --> H[Apply SHA512 Algorithm 512-bit output]
    D --> I[Apply CRC32 Algorithm 32-bit output]
    E --> J[Compile Hash Results Table]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Display Hexadecimal Digests in HashCalc GUI]
    K --> L{Forensic Decision Hash Match?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Evidence Integrity Verified Proceed with Analysis]
    L -- No --> N[Evidence Tampered Chain of Custody Broken]
    M --> O[Document Hash in Chain of Custody Form]
    N --> P[Reject Evidence Seizure Report Filed]
    O --> Q[Archive Original Evidence on Write-Blocked Media]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Court Admissible Evidence]

    classDef input fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef alert fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram: SHA-256 Internal Compression Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[512-bit Input Block] --> B[Message Schedule W0 to W63]
    B --> C[Initialize Hash Values H0 to H7]
    C --> D[Round 1 Compression Function]
    D --> E[Round 2 to Round 63 Iterative Processing]
    E --> F[Round 64 Final Round]
    F --> G[Add to Previous Hash Values]
    G --> H[Updated Hash Values H0 to H7]
    H --> I{All Blocks Processed?}
    I -- No --> A
    I -- Yes --> J[Concatenate H0 to H7]
    J --> K[256-bit Final SHA-256 Digest]

    classDef block fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef round fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,C block
    class D,E,F round
    class H,J,K output
```

## 4.3 Forensic Chain-of-Custody Flow with Hash Verification Checkpoints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crime Scene Seizure of Device] --> B[Acquire Forensic Image DD, E01]
    B --> C[Generate Hash Set MD5 and SHA256]
    C --> D[Store Image on Write-Blocked Media]
    D --> E[Open Case File in Lab]
    E --> F[Re-compute Hash on the Working Copy]
    F --> G{Compare Hash with Original}
    G -- Match --> H[Integrity OK Begin Forensic Analysis]
    G -- Mismatch --> I[Integrity FAIL Halt Analysis]
    H --> J[Analyze Files, Registry, Logs]
    J --> K[Generate Final Forensic Report]
    K --> L[Re-hash Analyzed Artifacts]
    L --> M[Submit to Court of Law]
    I --> N[Investigate Cause Bit-Rot, Tampering, or Media Failure]
    N --> O[Re-acquire Image if Necessary]

    classDef seizure fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hash fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef success fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fail fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B seizure
    class C,F,L hash
    class H,J,K,M success
    class I,N fail
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define a cryptographic hash function. List any three of its essential properties with one-line justifications.*

**Model Answer:**

A *cryptographic hash function* $H$ is a deterministic algorithm that converts an input of arbitrary length into a fixed-size output (the *message digest*), such that the output uniquely represents the input data.

**Three Essential Properties:**

1. **Pre-image Resistance:** Given a hash $h$, it is computationally infeasible to determine the original input $m$ such that $H(m) = h$. This prevents reverse-engineering of the original file.
2. **Collision Resistance:** It is computationally infeasible to find two distinct inputs $m_1$ and $m_2$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$. This guarantees the uniqueness of the digital fingerprint.
3. **Avalanche Effect:** A change of even a single bit in the input causes a drastic, unpredictable change in approximately 50% of the output bits, ensuring sensitivity to tampering.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is HashCalc? State the hash algorithms it supports and explain its forensic significance.*

**Model Answer:**

**HashCalc** is a Windows-based, freeware utility developed by SlavaSoft that computes the message digest of any file (or text string) using one or more cryptographic hash algorithms simultaneously.

**Algorithms Supported:** MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512, MD4, RIPEMD-128, RIPEMD-160, PANAMA, TIGER, ADLER32, CRC32, and eDonkey2000 (ed2k).

**Forensic Significance:**
- **Evidence Integrity:** It verifies that a digital evidence file has not been altered from the time of seizure to the time of court presentation by comparing hashes.
- **Authentication:** It generates a unique mathematical fingerprint of evidence that can be recorded in the chain-of-custody documentation.
- **Non-repudiation:** A hash mismatch provides irrefutable mathematical proof of tampering, eliminating any defense of "the file was not modified."

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the SHA-256 algorithm in detail, including its block size, word size, number of rounds, and the structure of the message schedule. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Demonstrate, with an example, how the Avalanche Effect manifests in SHA-256. Why is this property critical for digital forensic evidence authentication? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a)

**SHA-256 (Secure Hash Algorithm 256-bit) — Detailed Description:**

SHA-256 is a member of the SHA-2 family, designed by the U.S. National Security Agency (NSA) and standardized by NIST in *FIPS PUB 180-4*.

**1. Output Size:** Produces a 256-bit (64 hexadecimal character) message digest.

**2. Block Size:** The input message is divided into fixed-size blocks of **512 bits (64 bytes)** each.

**3. Word Size:** Operates on 32-bit words. Hence, 8 working variables ($a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h$) are used.

**4. Number of Rounds:** Each 512-bit block is processed through exactly **64 compression rounds**.

**5. Message Schedule Construction:**
- The 512-bit block is divided into 16 words of 32 bits each: $W_0, W_1, \ldots, W_{15}$.
- The remaining 48 words ($W_{16}$ to $W_{63}$) are generated using the recurrence:

$$
W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16}
$$

where $\sigma_0$ and $\sigma_1$ are bitwise rotation and shift functions.

**6. Compression Function:** Each round uses six logical functions — $\text{Ch}$ (Choose), $\text{Maj}$ (Majority), $\Sigma_0$, $\Sigma_1$, $\sigma_0$, $\sigma_1$ — combined with 64 predetermined 32-bit constants $K_t$.

**7. Padding:** The original message is padded with a `1` bit, followed by `0` bits, followed by a 64-bit representation of the original message length, ensuring the total length is a multiple of 512.

**8. Initialization Hash Values:** Eight 32-bit initial values $H_0$ to $H_7$ are specified by the FIPS 180-4 standard (derived from the square roots of the first 8 prime numbers).

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Demonstration of the Avalanche Effect:**

**Example Setup:**
- Input A: ASCII string `"Forensic"` (binary: `01000110 01101111 01110010 ...`)
- Input B: ASCII string `"forensic"` — note the lowercase `f`. This differs from Input A by exactly **one bit** (the case bit of the first character).

**Observation:**
- The SHA-256 digest of Input A begins with: `a3b2c1d4e5...`
- The SHA-256 digest of Input B begins with: `7f8e9a0b1c...`
- **Result:** Out of 256 bits in the output, approximately 128 bits (50%) have changed — a perfect manifestation of the avalanche effect.

**Critical Importance for Forensic Evidence Authentication:**

1. **Tamper Detection:** A forensic examiner can be mathematically certain that a one-bit modification in the evidence file will be detected because the hash will change drastically.
2. **Court Admissibility:** The avalanche effect provides the *evidentiary weight* required under the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (as amended by the IT Act, 2000) to prove that the file was not silently corrupted or manipulated.
3. **Chain-of-Custody Integrity:** At every checkpoint of the chain of custody, the hash is recomputed. Even a single-byte alteration caused by malware, accidental modification, or intentional tampering is immediately detected.
4. **Defense Against Forgery:** An attacker attempting to substitute a malicious file for the original cannot predict how their modifications will affect the hash, making forgery computationally infeasible.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure to generate and compare the hash code of two files using HashCalc. What observations confirm that the files are identical or different? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 in terms of digest size, security status, and practical forensic applicability. Justify which one is recommended in 2024. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Procedure to Generate and Compare Hash Codes Using HashCalc:**

**Step 1: Download and Install HashCalc**
- Download HashCalc (a freeware Windows application) from the official SlavaSoft website and install it on the forensic workstation.

**Step 2: Launch HashCalc**
- Open the application. A graphical user interface appears with options for *File*, *Text*, and *Hex* input modes.

**Step 3: Select the Input Mode**
- Click on the **"File"** radio button to compute the hash of a file.
- Click the **"..." (Browse)** button to navigate to the first file (e.g., `evidence_file_1.dd`).

**Step 4: Choose the Hash Algorithms**
- From the list of checkboxes on the right side, select: **MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512, and CRC32** (selecting multiple algorithms is recommended for layered verification).

**Step 5: Click "Calculate"**
- HashCalc processes the file and displays the digest in hexadecimal format in the corresponding fields. The hash values are now ready for documentation.

**Step 6: Repeat for the Second File**
- Clear the input field and load the second file (e.g., `evidence_file_2.dd`).
- Click **"Calculate"** again. The new digests are displayed.

**Step 7: Compare the Hash Values**
- **If all corresponding hash values match exactly (character-by-character):** The two files are byte-for-byte identical, confirming the evidence has not been modified.
- **If even a single character in any hash differs:** The files are different. The original evidence has been tampered with, and the chain of custody is broken.

**Step 8: Document the Results**
- Record all hash values in the chain-of-custody form, sign and date the entry, and preserve the printed output as part of the case file.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Comparative Analysis of MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256:**

| Comparison Criterion | MD5 | SHA-1 | SHA-256 |
|----------------------|-----|-------|---------|
| **Digest Size (bits)** | 128 | 160 | 256 |
| **Hex Character Length** | 32 | 40 | 64 |
| **Block Size (bits)** | 512 | 512 | 512 |
| **Number of Rounds** | 64 (4 × 16) | 80 | 64 |
| **Year of Publication** | 1991 (Rivest) | 1995 (NSA) | 2001 (NSA, NIST) |
| **Security Status (2024)** | **Broken** — collisions found in seconds | **Deprecated** — SHAttered attack (2017) | **Secure** — Industry standard |
| **Collision Resistance** | $\approx 2^{64}$ operations | $\approx 2^{80}$ operations | $\approx 2^{128}$ operations |
| **Practical Attack Feasibility** | High — feasible on a laptop | Moderate — feasible for nation-states | Infeasible — quantum threat only |
| **Forensic Use in 2024** | Legacy systems only | Backward compatibility | **Recommended** |

**Justification for SHA-256 in 2024:**

SHA-256 is the recommended algorithm for 2024 forensic practice for the following reasons:

1. **Cryptographic Strength:** With a $2^{128}$ collision resistance, no practical attack has been published even after two decades of public cryptanalysis.
2. **NIST Standardization:** It is published as *FIPS 180-4* and is the U.S. federal government standard.
3. **Industry Adoption:** Used in TLS 1.3, Bitcoin, digital signatures (RSA-PSS), and forensic tools such as FTK and EnCase.
4. **Court Acceptance:** Indian courts, as well as international jurisdictions (e.g., the U.S. Federal Rules of Evidence), accept SHA-256 as a *reliable scientific method* under the *Daubert Standard*.
5. **Future-Proofing:** While MD5 and SHA-1 have been broken, SHA-256 is expected to remain secure against classical computing attacks for at least 20–30 more years.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse the *digest size* in bits with the *block size* in bits. The **digest size** of MD5 is 128 bits, but the **block size** is 512 bits. Losing **1–2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students write that SHA-1 is "secure" or "still in use" in 2024. SHA-1 has been **deprecated by NIST since 2011** and the SHAttered collision attack was demonstrated in 2017. Losing **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students forget to mention the **avalanche effect** when explaining the Avalanche Effect question. The 50% bit-change criterion is mandatory. Losing **2–3 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In the procedure-based question, students skip the documentation step. Always mention **chain-of-custody documentation**. Losing **1 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Cyber Forensics Lab, Module Experiment 2**

- **Hash Function:** Deterministic algorithm that maps arbitrary-length input to a fixed-length output.
- **Three Core Properties:** Pre-image resistance, Second pre-image resistance, Collision resistance.
- **Auxiliary Properties:** Determinism, Avalanche effect, fixed output length, quick computation.
- **MD5:** 128-bit output (32 hex chars); **broken**; legacy use only.
- **SHA-1:** 160-bit output (40 hex chars); **deprecated**; not recommended in 2024.
- **SHA-256:** 256-bit output (64 hex chars); **NIST-recommended**; used in modern forensics.
- **SHA-512:** 512-bit output (128 hex chars); high-security variant.
- **CRC32:** 32-bit output; non-cryptographic; used only for error detection.
- **Conversion Formula:** $\text{Hex characters} = \text{Bits} \div 4$.
- **Collision Bound (Birthday Paradox):** $\approx 2^{n/2}$ operations for an $n$-bit hash.
- **SHA-256 Block Size:** 512 bits; Word Size: 32 bits; Rounds: 64.
- **HashCalc:** SlavaSoft freeware Windows utility; supports multiple simultaneous algorithms.
- **Forensic Role:** Integrity verification, authentication, non-repudiation.
- **Avalanche Effect:** ~50% bit change in output for a 1-bit input change.
- **Chain of Custody:** Hash at seizure (T1) must equal hash at analysis (T2).
- **NIST Standard Reference:** FIPS PUB 180-4 (Secure Hash Standard).
- **Practical Tip:** Always compute **both** MD5 (for legacy compatibility) and SHA-256 (for modern verification) when generating forensic hash values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Command Line Analysis of Disk Images Using The Sleuth Kit (TSK) Tool
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCL607 - CYBER FORENSICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Command Line Analysis of Disk Images Using The Sleuth Kit (TSK) Tool

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**The Sleuth Kit (TSK)** is an open-source collection of **command-line digital forensics tools** written in C/C++ that allows forensic investigators to analyze disk images, file systems, and individual files without altering the original evidence. It is the **command-line backbone** of the well-known **Autopsy Forensic Browser** GUI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCCL607):** TSK is a UNIX/Windows-based toolkit that provides tools for analyzing **NTFS, FAT, Ext2/3/4, HFS+, ISO 9660, and UFS** file systems from raw (`.dd`, `.raw`, `.img`, `.E01`) or AFF (Advanced Forensic Format) disk images.

It operates in a **read-only mode** on evidence, thereby preserving the **Chain of Custody** and maintaining forensic soundness — a mandatory legal requirement under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Disk X-Ray Machine"** 🩻
>
> Imagine a forensic investigator arriving at a crime scene (a seized hard disk). They cannot open the disk case (since that would damage evidence). They instead use an **X-ray machine** that lets them see inside — list the files, recover deleted entries, and read every byte — *without ever opening the casing*.
>
> - **Disk Image (`.dd` file)** = A perfect 1:1 photocopy of the disk's magnetic surface.
> - **TSK Tools** = The X-ray machine that lets us inspect this photocopy layer by layer.
> - **Each TSK tool** = A different lens (partition lens, file-system lens, file-content lens, timeline lens).

## 1.3 Why TSK and Not Just Hex Editors?

| Feature | Hex Editor (e.g., HxD) | The Sleuth Kit (TSK) |
|---|---|---|
| Image Handling | Mostly physical | **Logical + Physical layers** |
| File System Awareness | No | **Yes (NTFS, FAT, ext, etc.)** |
| Deleted File Recovery | Manual search | **Built-in (`fls -r -d`)** |
| Automation (Scripting) | Limited | **Shell-script friendly** |
| Forensic Integrity | Risk of writes if not careful | **Strictly read-only design** |
| Cross-Platform | OS-Dependent | **Linux, Windows, macOS** |

> [!TIP]
> TSK tools **never modify the source image**. This is a legal, procedural, and academic requirement for any evidence to be admissible in a court of law.

## 1.4 The Three Logical Layers of a Disk

A disk image analyzed by TSK is treated as a **three-layer abstraction**:

$$
\text{Physical Layer (sectors)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Partition Layer (volumes)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{File System Layer (files \& metadata)}
$$

Each TSK tool targets one specific layer. Choosing the right tool is the first step of any forensic workflow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 TSK Architecture

The Sleuth Kit is organized into a **layered modular architecture**. A forensic examiner typically moves from the **physical** layer upward to the **logical** layer.

### Layer 1 — Physical / Image Layer
Handles raw sectors of the disk image. Tools: `img_stat`, `disk_stat`, `sigfind`.

### Layer 2 — Partition / Volume Layer
Identifies partitions (MBR, GPT, Apple Partition Map, Sun VTOC). Tools: `mmls`, `pstat`.

### Layer 3 — File System Layer
Parses file-system structures (MFT in NTFS, inode tables in ext, FAT chains). Tools: `fsstat`, `fls`, `icat`, `istat`, `ffind`, `ifind`, `fiwalk`.

### Layer 4 — Content / File Layer
Recovers and reads file content. Tools: `icat`, `srch_strings`, `blkls`, `blkcat`.

### Layer 5 — Timeline / Correlation Layer
Builds MAC (Modified, Accessed, Changed/Created) time timelines. Tools: `mactime`.

## 2.2 The TSK Workflow (Logical Flow)

$$
\text{Acquire Image} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Verify Hash} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Identify Partitions} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Identify File System} \;\rightarrow\; \text{List Files (incl. deleted)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Inspect Inodes/MFT Entries} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Extract Content} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Correlate Timelines}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulae below are **High-Yield for KTU 2024 Lab Exam** (viva + record).

| Concept | Formula / Convention | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Sector Size (most HDDs) | **512 bytes** (legacy) or **4096 bytes** (Advanced Format) | bytes |
| Image to Byte Offset | $\text{Offset} = \text{Sector Number} \times \text{Sector Size}$ | bytes |
| Inode Address (ext2/3/4) | $\text{Logical Inode} = \text{Block Group} \times \text{Inodes per Group} + \text{Index in Group}$ | dimensionless |
| MFT Entry Number (NTFS) | $\text{MFT Entry} = \lfloor \text{Byte Offset} / 1024 \rfloor$ for $1024$-byte MFT records | dimensionless |
| Hash Verification | $\text{SHA-256} / \text{MD5}$ must match **bit-for-bit** | hex string |
| Offset Notation (TSK) | `-o 63` means **skip first 63 sectors** before reading the file system | sectors |
| Sector Notation (TSK) | `-s 2048` means start reading at **sector 2048** | sectors |
| File Type in NTFS | Inode $\equiv$ **MFT Entry Number** | dimensionless |
| File Type in FAT | Inode $\equiv$ **Directory Entry Sector Position** | dimensionless |
| Block Size Extraction | $2^{\text{s\_log\_block\_size}}$ returned by `fsstat` | bytes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** In all TSK command outputs, the delimiter character is often `|`. When documenting output in your KTU record, **replace it with a tab or comma** to avoid any confusion with the markdown table `|` separator used in this document.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Law Enforcement (Kerala Police Cyber Cell):** Investigating seized hard drives in cases of online fraud, child exploitation material, and ransomware.
- **Incident Response:** Determining lateral movement of an attacker via NTFS timestamp anomalies.
- **e-Discovery (Corporate):** Quickly indexing millions of files in legal hold without booting the suspect machine.
- **Academic Research:** Studying deleted-file recovery rates across different file systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setting Up the Environment

### Step 1 — Install TSK on Ubuntu/Kali Linux

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install sleuthkit -y
```

### Step 2 — Verify Installation

```bash
mmls -V
# Expected output: 'The Sleuth Kit ver 4.x.x'
```

### Step 3 — Create a Working Directory

```bash
mkdir ~/tky_lab
cd ~/tky_lab
```

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Experiment: Analyzing a Disk Image

Assume we have an evidence image `evidence.dd` (raw format) located in `~/tky_lab/`.

---

### **Sub-Experiment A: Image-Level Metadata (`img_stat`)**

**Purpose:** Confirm the basic geometry of the image before touching partitions.

```bash
img_stat evidence.dd
```

**Sample Output Analysis:**

| Field | Typical Value | Forensic Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `Image Type` | raw | No compression; bit-exact copy |
| `Size in Bytes` | 500107862016 | Confirms size matches seized HDD |
| `Sector Size` | 512 | Aligns with classical HDD geometry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Record Tip:** Always paste the *exact* output of `img_stat` into your lab record along with the **SHA-256 hash** generated by:
> ```bash
> sha256sum evidence.dd
> ```

---

### **Sub-Experiment B: Partition Layout (`mmls`)**

**Purpose:** List partitions inside the image (works on MBR, GPT, etc.).

```bash
mmls evidence.dd
```

**Sample Output:**

```
DOS Partition Table
Offset Sector: 0
Units are in 512-byte sectors

      Slot      Start        End          Length       Description
000:  Meta      0000000000   0000000000   0000000001   Primary Table (#0)
001:  -------   0000000001   0000002047   0000002047   Unallocated
002:  000:00    0000002048   0000511999   0000509952   NTFS (0x07)
003:  -------   0000512000   0000514047   0000002048   Unallocated
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **NTFS partition starts at sector 2048**. This number is critical — we will use it with the `-o` (offset) flag in all subsequent file-system commands.

**Derivation of the partition start byte-offset:**

$$
\text{Byte Offset} = 2048 \times 512 = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MiB}
$$

This is the exact byte location where the NTFS Volume Boot Record (VBR) resides.

---

### **Sub-Experiment C: File System Statistics (`fsstat`)**

**Purpose:** Inspect metadata of the detected file system.

```bash
fsstat -o 2048 evidence.dd
```

**Sample Key Output:**

| Field | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| `File System Type` | NTFS | Confirmed Windows file system |
| `Volume Serial Number` | 0A2B3C4D | Unique volume ID; matches `C:\` properties on suspect PC |
| `Cluster Size` | 4096 bytes | Block size used for allocation |
| `MFT Start Cluster` | 786432 | Master File Table physical location |
| `MFT Block Size` | 4096 | MFT record size used |

---

### **Sub-Experiment D: File Listing (Including Deleted) (`fls`)**

**Purpose:** Recursively list files and directories, **including deleted entries**.

```bash
fls -r -o 2048 evidence.dd > file_listing.txt
```

For **deleted files only**:

```bash
fls -r -d -o 2048 evidence.dd > deleted_files.txt
```

**Sample Output Snippet:**

```
r/r 24: secret_plan.docx
r/r 28: budget.xlsx
d/d 35: Pictures
v/v 1024: old_passwords.txt   (deleted)
r/r 1029: malware.exe        (deleted)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - `r/r` = regular file, allocated.
> - `d/d` = directory, allocated.
> - `v/v` = **virtual** entry corresponding to a **deleted** file or directory. This is gold for KTU exam answers.

---

### **Sub-Experiment E: Inode/MFT Statistics (`istat`)**

**Purpose:** Print detailed metadata (timestamps, sizes, resident data) of a specific MFT entry.

```bash
istat -o 2048 evidence.dd 24
```

**Sample Output:**

```
MFT Entry Header Values:
Entry: 24    Sequence: 1
$STANDARD_INFORMATION Attribute Values:
    Created:  2024-08-12 14:22:01 (IST)
    File Modified: 2024-09-03 09:11:45
    MFT Modified: 2024-09-03 09:11:45
    Accessed: 2024-09-03 09:11:45
$FILE_NAME Attribute Values:
    Name: secret_plan.docx
    Parent Dir: 5  (root)
$DATA Attribute Values:
    Non-Resident, Clusters: (1423, 4)  (size: 28672)
```

**Derivation of file data byte-offset on disk:**

$$
\text{Start Byte} = \text{Cluster Number} \times \text{Cluster Size} = 1423 \times 4096 = 5{,}828{,}608 \text{ bytes}
$$

---

### **Sub-Experiment F: File Content Extraction (`icat`)**

**Purpose:** Output the **raw content** of a file from the disk image based on its inode/MFT number.

```bash
icat -o 2048 evidence.dd 24 > secret_plan.docx
```

For a **deleted file**:

```bash
icat -o 2048 evidence.dd 1029 > recovered_malware.exe
```

> [!CAUTION]
> Always **verify the extracted file** with:
> ```bash
> file secret_plan.docx
> sha256sum secret_plan.docx
> ```
> The hash of the extracted file **will not match** the original (if the file was modified), but `file` should still correctly identify its type.

---

### **Sub-Experiment G: Filename from Inode (`ffind`)**

**Purpose:** Given an inode number, find the **filename(s)** that reference it. Useful when you have a sector/inode from a log but not the file path.

```bash
ffind -o 2048 evidence.dd 1029
```

---

### **Sub-Experiment H: Signature Search (`sigfind`)**

**Purpose:** Locate files by their **magic bytes** (e.g., JPEG `FF D8 FF`, PDF `%PDF`).

```bash
sigfind -b 512 0xFFD8FFE0 evidence.dd
```

Output gives the **sector offset** where the first JPEG header was found.

---

### **Sub-Experiment I: Unallocated Space (`blkls`)**

**Purpose:** Extract data from **unallocated clusters** (a treasure trove of deleted fragments).

```bash
blkls -o 2048 evidence.dd > unallocated.bin
strings -a unallocated.bin | less
```

---

### **Sub-Experiment J: MAC Timeline (`mactime`)**

**Purpose:** Build a complete timeline of file activity from `fls` output.

```bash
fls -r -m / -o 2048 evidence.dd > body.txt
mactime -b body.txt -d > timeline.csv
```

The `timeline.csv` can be opened in Excel/LibreOffice to filter by date — extremely useful in viva questions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection

| Investigator's Question | Correct TSK Tool | Command Snippet |
|---|---|---|
| What partitions exist? | `mmls` | `mmls evidence.dd` |
| What file system is it? | `fsstat` | `fsstat -o 2048 evidence.dd` |
| What files are present? | `fls` | `fls -r -o 2048 evidence.dd` |
| What deleted files exist? | `fls -d` | `fls -r -d -o 2048 evidence.dd` |
| What is the metadata of file X? | `istat` | `istat -o 2048 evidence.dd <inode>` |
| Extract content of file X? | `icat` | `icat -o 2048 evidence.dd <inode>` |
| Find filename from inode? | `ffind` | `ffind -o 2048 evidence.dd <inode>` |
| Find JPEG signatures? | `sigfind` | `sigfind -b 512 0xFFD8FFE0 evidence.dd` |
| Dump unallocated space? | `blkls` | `blkls -o 2048 evidence.dd` |
| Build timeline? | `mactime` | `mactime -b body.txt -d` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TSK Three-Layer Forensics Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquired Disk Image evidence_dd] --> B[Layer 1 Physical]
    B --> B1[img_stat]
    B --> B2[disk_stat]
    B --> B3[sigfind]
    A --> C[Layer 2 Partition]
    C --> C1[mmls]
    C --> C2[pstat]
    C --> D[Layer 3 File System]
    D --> D1[fsstat]
    D --> D2[fls]
    D --> D3[istat]
    D --> D4[icat]
    D --> D5[ffind]
    D --> D6[blkls]
    A --> E[Layer 4 Timeline]
    E --> E1[mactime]
    E1 --> F[Investigator Report]
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
```

## 4.2 Forensic Workflow: From Seizure to Report

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Seized HDD] --> S2[Write Blocker Attached]
    S2 --> S3[Acquire dd Image via dcfldd or FTK Imager]
    S3 --> S4[Compute SHA256 Hash]
    S4 --> S5{Verify Hash}
    S5 -->|Match| S6[Load Image into TSK]
    S5 -->|Mismatch| S7[Re-Acquire]
    S6 --> S8[Run mmls]
    S8 --> S9[Identify NTFS Partition at Sector 2048]
    S9 --> S10[Run fsstat -o 2048]
    S10 --> S11[Run fls -r]
    S11 --> S12[Cross-Reference with istat and icat]
    S12 --> S13[Run mactime]
    S13 --> S14[Generate Forensic Report]
```

## 4.3 MFT Entry Internal Structure (NTFS Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[MFT Entry 1024 bytes] --> M1[FILE Signature 4 bytes]
    M --> M2[Standard Information Attribute]
    M --> M3[File Name Attribute]
    M --> M4[Data Attribute]
    M --> M5[Security Descriptor]
    M2 --> M2a[Created Time]
    M2 --> M2b[Modified Time]
    M2 --> M2c[Accessed Time]
    M2 --> M2d[MFT Modified Time]
    M4 --> M4a{Resident?}
    M4a -->|Yes| M4b[Data stored inside MFT]
    M4a -->|No| M4c[Data run list points to clusters]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any six command-line tools provided by The Sleuth Kit (TSK) and state the primary function of each.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| # | Tool | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `mmls` | Displays the layout of partitions in a disk image |
| 2 | `fsstat` | Shows file-system-level statistics and metadata |
| 3 | `fls` | Lists file and directory names, including deleted entries |
| 4 | `icat` | Outputs the raw content of a file given its inode |
| 5 | `istat` | Displays detailed metadata of a specific inode/MFT entry |
| 6 | `mactime` | Generates MAC-time timeline from `fls` output |

*(Any six correctly paired tool-functions → 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between the `fls` and `icat` commands of TSK. In what scenario would an investigator use each?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- `fls` is a **listing tool** that traverses the file-system structure and outputs **file/directory names and their inode numbers**. It does **not** read the actual content. *(1 mark)*
- `icat` is a **content-extraction tool** that takes an inode number and outputs the **raw byte stream of that file** (e.g., a `.docx` or `.exe`). *(1 mark)*
- **Scenario:** An investigator first uses `fls -r` to *identify* suspicious files (such as `malware.exe`). Once the inode number is known (e.g., 1029), they use `icat -o 2048 evidence.dd 1029` to **extract the file for malware analysis**. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to identify the partitions and the file system of a raw disk image `evidence.dd` using TSK. Mention the relevant commands and interpret a sample output. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Demonstrate how an investigator would recover a **deleted file** and verify its metadata using TSK. Include the relevant flags and the importance of the `-o` parameter. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Image Verification (1 mark)**

```bash
sha256sum evidence.dd
img_stat evidence.dd
```

The output confirms the image type (raw), sector size (512), and total size, matching the seized HDD geometry.

**Step 2 — Partition Identification using `mmls` (3 marks)**

```bash
mmls evidence.dd
```

**Sample Output:**

```
DOS Partition Table
Units are in 512-byte sectors

Slot    Start        End          Length       Description
000:    0000000000   0000000000   0000000001   Primary Table
002:    0000002048   0000511999   0000509952   NTFS (0x07)
```

**Interpretation:** The image contains a single NTFS partition starting at **sector 2048** (i.e., byte offset $2048 \times 512 = 1{,}048{,}576$).

**Step 3 — File-System Statistics using `fsstat` (3 marks)**

```bash
fsstat -o 2048 evidence.dd
```

This outputs:
- File System Type: **NTFS**
- Cluster Size: **4096 bytes**
- Volume Serial Number
- MFT Start Cluster

**Conclusion (no marks lost):** The combination of `mmls` and `fsstat` confirms a **Windows NTFS file system** starting at sector 2048. *(1 mark for clear conclusion)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Locate Deleted Entries (3 marks)**

```bash
fls -r -d -o 2048 evidence.dd
```

The `-d` flag restricts output to **deleted entries**, which appear with the `v/v` marker (virtual entries).

**Step 2 — Read Inode Statistics (2 marks)**

```bash
istat -o 2048 evidence.dd 1029
```

This shows `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` timestamps, parent directory, and data run information — confirming the file's existence prior to deletion.

**Step 3 — Extract the File (2 marks)**

```bash
icat -o 2048 evidence.dd 1029 > recovered_malware.exe
file recovered_malware.exe
```

**Importance of the `-o 2048` flag:**
The `-o` parameter tells TSK to **skip the first 2048 sectors** before addressing the file system. Since the NTFS partition starts at sector 2048, **omitting `-o 2048`** would cause TSK to misread the MBR region as the file system, resulting in **garbage output and silent analysis errors**. *(Full 2 marks only if the offset-justification is provided)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Alternative]

**(a)** With neat examples, describe the role of `mactime` in TSK-based forensic analysis. How is the `body.txt` file generated and consumed? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An investigator discovers a suspicious JPEG with broken metadata in `unallocated.bin`. Outline the TSK-based workflow to **recover and validate** this JPEG, including `blkls`, `sigfind`, and `strings`. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Concept of MAC Time (2 marks):**
Every file in NTFS/ext has four timestamps: **Modified (M), Accessed (A), Changed/Created (C)**. These are critical for reconstructing an attacker's activity.

**Generating `body.txt` (2 marks):**
```bash
fls -r -m / -o 2048 evidence.dd > body.txt
```
- `-r` = recursive
- `-m /` = prepend `/` (mount-point prefix) to filenames
- Output is the canonical TSK **body file** format

**Sample Line of `body.txt`:**
```
0|/secret_plan.docx|24|rrwxrwxrwx|0|0|24|1725372161|1725372161|1725372161|0
```

The trailing integers are Unix epoch timestamps for the four MAC times.

**Consuming with `mactime` (3 marks):**
```bash
mactime -b body.txt -d > timeline.csv
```
- `-b` specifies the body file
- `-d` flag outputs **human-readable dates** instead of Unix epochs
- The `timeline.csv` can be opened in a spreadsheet to filter by date, providing a **chronological narrative** of all file activity.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — Dump Unallocated Space (2 marks):**
```bash
blkls -o 2048 evidence.dd > unallocated.bin
```
`blkls` outputs the raw content of all **unallocated clusters** in the NTFS volume.

**Step 2 — Search for File Signatures (2 marks):**
```bash
sigfind -b 512 0xFFD8FFE0 unallocated.bin
```
The magic bytes `FF D8 FF E0` mark the beginning of every JPEG. `sigfind` reports the sector where the first signature was located.

**Step 3 — Carve the File (2 marks):**
```bash
dd if=unallocated.bin of=recovered.jpg bs=512 skip=<sector_number> count=<estimated_blocks>
```

**Step 4 — Validate (1 mark):**
```bash
file recovered.jpg
exiftool recovered.jpg
```
A successful `file` output of `JPEG image data` confirms recovery; EXIF data may reveal the original camera or device.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting `-o` offset** → Loses ~2 marks in any file-system question.
> 2. **Confusing `fls` with `icat`** → `fls` *lists*, `icat` *extracts*. Mixing these up is the most common mistake.
> 3. **Not mentioning hash verification** → Examiners explicitly award marks for forensic integrity (SHA-256/MD5).
> 4. **Using wrong file system in the command** → Specifying `-f ext2` for an NTFS image is a guaranteed 0 for that sub-part.
> 5. **Ignoring deleted entries** → Always include `fls -d` in your answer to recover deleted files.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- 🔑 **TSK = Read-Only Toolkit** for analyzing disk images; preserves forensic integrity.
- 🔑 **Three Layers:** Physical (sectors) → Partition (volumes) → File System (files).
- 🔑 **First Command:** Always `img_stat` and `mmls` to understand the image geometry.
- 🔑 **Critical Flag `-o <sector>`:** Skips the first N sectors to reach the partition's file system. **Never omit it after `mmls`.**
- 🔑 **`fls` for listing, `icat` for extraction, `istat` for metadata, `ffind` for inode-to-name, `blkls` for unallocated.**
- 🔑 **`-d` flag on `fls`** = **Deleted entries only** — always mention this in viva.
- 🔑 **`v/v` marker** in `fls` output = **deleted** (virtual) entry. Memorize it.
- 🔑 **`sigfind`** uses **magic bytes** (e.g., `0xFFD8FFE0` for JPEG) — not file extensions.
- 🔑 **`mactime`** = MAC timeline generator; consumes `fls -m` body file.
- 🔑 **NTFS MFT entry = Inode equivalent** in TSK. `istat` and `icat` both address it numerically.
- 🔑 **Hash verification** is mandatory: `sha256sum evidence.dd` after acquisition.
- 🔑 **Cluster / Sector offset conversion:** $\text{Byte Offset} = \text{Sector Number} \times \text{Sector Size}$.
- 🔑 **Output of `mmls` → Start Sector → use as `-o` value** in all subsequent commands.
- 🔑 **Examiner-friendly answer style:** Always end with a one-line *interpretation* of the output.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: File System Forensic Analysis Using Autopsy Tool
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCL607 - CYBER FORENSICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** File System Forensic Analysis Using Autopsy Tool

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**File System Forensic Analysis** is the systematic, scientifically validated process of examining, identifying, recovering, and interpreting data stored within the logical structures of a digital file system (such as NTFS, FAT32, exFAT, EXT3/EXT4, HFS+, APFS) for the purpose of producing legally admissible evidence. It targets the on-disk artifacts left behind by user activity, operating system processes, and application interactions.

**Autopsy** is an open-source, GUI-based **digital forensics platform** built on top of The Sleuth Kit (TSK) and other command-line forensic utilities. It is the de-facto standard tool endorsed by NIST and the academic community for academic forensic laboratories, providing automated ingest modules, keyword searching, file carving, hash filtering, and timeline analysis of disk images.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** *File system forensics* reconstructs the state and history of a storage medium without altering the original evidence by analyzing the file allocation tables, directory entries, metadata streams, and unallocated/slack spaces.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Digital Archaeologist

Imagine a crime scene inside a vast, abandoned library. The books (files) are still on the shelves, but someone has torn out pages (deleted data), left fingerprints on the spines (metadata/MAC times), scribbled notes in the margins (alternate data streams), and even hidden messages between the lines of otherwise normal pages (steganography in slack space).

A **File System Forensic Analyst** is essentially a **digital archaeologist**:

- The **library catalog** = **File Allocation Table (FAT)** or **Master File Table (MFT)** in NTFS.
- The **books on the shelf** = **Allocated clusters** in a partition.
- The **empty gaps on shelves** = **Unallocated space** (where deleted files once lived).
- The **dust on the floor** = **Slack space** (residue of a file that was once written but did not fully fill its sector).
- The **library's logbook of who visited when** = **MAC timestamps** (Modified, Accessed, Created/Changed).
- The **forensic tool (Autopsy)** = A complete **field archaeology kit** — brushes, sieves, UV lights, and chemical reagents — that the archaeologist uses to surface hidden artifacts without destroying the crime scene.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** When a user "deletes" a file, the operating system does NOT erase the bytes on the disk. It merely removes the *pointer* (e.g., the MFT entry or FAT chain link). The data physically remains until overwritten. This is the **fundamental principle** that makes file system forensic analysis possible.

---

## 1.3 Standard File System Metrics (KTU High-Yield Values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following values appear in **almost every KTU lab exam** and must be memorized by the student.

- **Sector Size (Bytes per Sector)**: **512 bytes** (legacy) or **4096 bytes** (4 Kn / Advanced Format) — the smallest addressable unit on a physical disk.
- **Cluster Size (Allocation Unit)**: Typically **4 KB** (8 sectors × 512 bytes) for modern NTFS volumes.
- **Block Size (Linux EXT4)**: Commonly **4096 bytes**.
- **Default Hash Algorithms used in Autopsy**: **MD5 (128-bit)**, **SHA-1 (160-bit)**, **SHA-256 (256-bit)**.
- **NIST Standard Reference**: NIST CFTT (Computer Forensic Tool Testing) project validates Autopsy for forensic soundness.

---

## 1.4 KTU Course Outcomes Mapped (CO Alignment)

| Course Outcome | CO Description | Module 2 Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Apply forensic tools to analyze file systems | ✅ Core focus |
| **CO3** | Interpret digital artifacts for evidence | ✅ Timeline + metadata |
| **CO4** | Document findings in forensic reports | ✅ Reporting module |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Layers of a File System

A forensic analyst must understand the **hierarchical layering** of a file system. Autopsy presents this hierarchy in its **Tree View** pane.

### Layer 1 — Physical Layer (The Disk)
- **Platters, tracks, sectors, cylinders**.
- Addressed via **LBA (Logical Block Addressing)**: a linear 48-bit address.
- Each sector carries a **CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check)** and a **512-byte** or **4096-byte** payload.

### Layer 2 — Volume / Partition Layer
- The **Volume Boot Record (VBR)** or **BIOS Parameter Block (BPB)** at **sector 0** of the partition declares:
  - Bytes per sector ($S$)
  - Sectors per cluster ($K$)
  - Total sectors, reserved sectors, FAT count, root directory entries (FAT only).
- The **Master File Table ($MFT$)** in NTFS begins at a location pointed to by the VBR.

### Layer 3 — Logical / File Layer
- The **Directory Structure** (folders, inode tables, B-trees).
- **File Metadata** (name, size, timestamps, security descriptors, data runs).
- **File Content** (the actual user bytes).

---

## 2.2 Core NTFS Concepts (Required for KTU Lab Viva)

> [!NOTE]
> NTFS = **New Technology File System**, the default file system of all modern Windows operating systems. KTU examiners expect NTFS fluency.

- **$MFT (Master File Table):** A special system file (MFT Entry 0) that is itself a file. Every file and folder on an NTFS volume has **at least one MFT entry** (typically 1024 bytes long).
- **$MFTMirr:** A backup copy of the first 4 MFT entries stored in the middle of the disk (for recovery if the start of $MFT is corrupted).
- **$LogFile:** A journal of all metadata transactions (analogous to a database WAL log).
- **$UsnJrnl (Update Sequence Number Journal):** Records every file system change (creation, deletion, rename) with USN values.
- **$Bitmap:** Tracks cluster allocation (1 bit per cluster).
- **Alternate Data Streams (ADS):** NTFS allows multiple data streams per file. ADS are a **classic KTU viva question** and a favorite hiding place for malware.

---

## 2.3 FAT File System (Frequently Tested)

- The **FAT (File Allocation Table)** is a linked-list array at the beginning of the volume.
- **FAT12 / FAT16 / FAT32** differ in the number of bits used per entry in the table.
- **Directory Entry structure (32 bytes):** 8.3 filename, attribute byte, creation date/time, first cluster, file size.
- The **deleted file marker** is `0xE5` placed as the first byte of the short filename. Autopsy highlights these in its Deleted Files tree.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize every row below. The **File Slack** and **RAM Slack** derivations appear in nearly every KTU cyber-forensics exam.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cluster Size | $C = S \times K$ | $S$ = bytes/sector, $K$ = sectors/cluster |
| 2 | Total Clusters | $N = \dfrac{T}{C}$ | $T$ = total volume bytes |
| 3 | File Slack | $F_{\text{slack}} = C - (F_{\text{size}} \bmod C)$ | Bytes; residue of last cluster |
| 4 | RAM Slack | $R_{\text{slack}} = S - (F_{\text{size}} \bmod S)$ | Bytes; pad to sector boundary |
| 5 | Drive Slack | $D_{\text{slack}} = F_{\text{slack}} - R_{\text{slack}}$ | Bytes; between sector and cluster end |
| 6 | MD5 Hash | 128-bit digest, $\mathcal{H}_{\text{MD5}}(F)$ | Hex output = 32 chars |
| 7 | SHA-1 Hash | 160-bit digest, $\mathcal{H}_{\text{SHA1}}(F)$ | Hex output = 40 chars |
| 8 | SHA-256 Hash | 256-bit digest, $\mathcal{H}_{\text{SHA256}}(F)$ | Hex output = 64 chars |
| 9 | Unallocated Space | $U = N - N_{\text{used}}$ | Clusters marked 0 in $Bitmap |
| 10 | MFT Entry Size | $M_{\text{size}} = 1024$ | Bytes (typical NTFS) |
| 11 | Slack Percentage | $\sigma = \dfrac{F_{\text{slack}}}{C} \times 100$ | Forensic efficiency metric |
| 12 | Hash Match Confidence | $\mathbb{P}_{\text{match}} = 1 - p^{n}$ | For $n$ independent hashes |

> **Symbol Convention:** $F$ = file, $S$ = sector, $C$ = cluster, $K$ = sectors per cluster, $T$ = total size, $N$ = cluster count, $M$ = MFT entry, $U$ = unallocated, $\sigma$ = slack ratio, $\mathbb{P}$ = probability.

---

## 2.5 The Four Slack Spaces (Engineering Utility)

Slack space is the **residue** between where a file logically ends and where its storage allocation physically ends. It is one of the **richest sources of forensic evidence** and is a **recurring KTU viva topic**.

| Slack Type | Location | Cause | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RAM Slack** | End of last sector of file | Sector size not divisible by file size | Residual memory content |
| **Drive Slack** | Between end-of-file sector and end-of-cluster | OS writes whole cluster | Old data from previously allocated files |
| **File Slack** | RAM + Drive Slack (total) | Combined effect | **Anti-forensics hiding zone** |
| **Volume Slack** | Between end-of-partition and end-of-disk | Partition does not fill disk | Boot sector / bootkit artifacts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** A junior malware analyst once recovered a stolen SSH private key from the **drive slack** of a deleted log file. The original log was 3,847 bytes in a 4,096-byte cluster — the *last 249 bytes* held the attacker's plaintext key. This is why every KTU examiner hammers the slack-space topic.

---

## 2.6 Autopsy Architecture & Ingest Modules

Autopsy is a **modular** application. Each ingest module is a Java/Python plugin that processes the case database.

| Ingest Module | Function | Forensic Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Recent Activity** | Parses Windows artifacts (UserAssist, Prefetch, RunMRU) | Execution history |
| **Hash Lookup** | Compares file hashes against NSRL known-good and custom known-bad sets | Rapid triage |
| **File Type Identification** | Magic-number based file signature analysis | Detects masquerading files |
| **Extension Mismatch Detector** | Flags `.exe` with PDF header | Anti-forensics detection |
| **Keyword Search** | Indexes strings and supports live/regular-expression search | Evidence surfacing |
| **Email Parser** | Decodes MBOX, PST, OST | Communication evidence |
| **EXIF Parser** | Extracts camera/location metadata from JPEGs | Geolocation timeline |
| **Carving** | Recovers files by signature from unallocated space | Deleted file recovery |
| **Plaso / Log2Timeline** | Builds a super-timeline across all artifacts | Chronological reconstruction |
| **Android Analyzer** | Parses SQLite databases from mobile images | Mobile forensics integration |

---

## 2.7 The Sleuth Kit (TSK) Backend

Autopsy is the **GUI shell**; the **engine** is The Sleuth Kit (TSK), a suite of C-based CLI tools:

- `fls` — list file names in a directory (including deleted)
- `icat` — output a file's contents by inode/MFT entry number
- `istat` — display inode/MFT metadata
- `mmls` — show the partition layout of a disk image
- `fsstat` — display file system statistics
- `img_stat` — show image metadata

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"What is the relationship between Autopsy and The Sleuth Kit?"* — Answer: **Autopsy is the graphical front-end; The Sleuth Kit is the command-line back-end. They share the same evidence-reading code path, ensuring forensic soundness.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every transition below is shown explicitly. **No step-skipping** is permitted by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol. The KTU examiner rewards intermediate algebraic clarity in the answer script.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cluster Size from Boot Sector

A student finds a Windows NTFS volume and wants to know the **cluster size** for forensic analysis.

**Given:**
- Bytes per sector (read from BPB field at offset `$0x0B$`): $S = 512$ bytes
- Sectors per cluster (read from BPB field at offset `$0x0D$`): $K = 8$

**Step 1 — Recall the geometric relationship.**

A cluster is the smallest unit of disk space that the file system can allocate to a file. By definition, a cluster is composed of $K$ consecutive sectors.

**Step 2 — Apply the cluster size formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= S \times K \\
C &= 512 \text{ bytes/sector} \times 8 \text{ sectors/cluster} \\
C &= 4096 \text{ bytes/cluster} \\
C &= 4 \text{ KiB/cluster}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

Every file on this volume will be allocated in multiples of 4,096 bytes. A file of just 1 byte still consumes one full 4,096-byte cluster on disk. This is the **minimum granularity of allocation** and the source of file slack.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — File Slack, RAM Slack, Drive Slack

**Given:**
- A text file `secret.txt` of size $F_{\text{size}} = 3,847$ bytes
- Sector size $S = 512$ bytes
- Cluster size $C = 4,096$ bytes (derived above)

### Step 1 — Compute File Slack

The OS rounds the file's footprint up to the nearest cluster boundary. The residue is **file slack**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{slack}} &= C - (F_{\text{size}} \bmod C) \\
F_{\text{size}} \bmod C &= 3{,}847 \bmod 4{,}096 \\
&= 3{,}847 \text{ (since } 3{,}847 < 4{,}096) \\
F_{\text{slack}} &= 4{,}096 - 3{,}847 \\
F_{\text{slack}} &= 249 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The last cluster is mostly empty — 249 bytes of *wasted-but-allocated* space.

### Step 2 — Compute RAM Slack

The OS only writes complete sectors, but the file does not end on a sector boundary. The OS pads with whatever was in RAM at the time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{slack}} &= S - (F_{\text{size}} \bmod S) \\
F_{\text{size}} \bmod S &= 3{,}847 \bmod 512 \\
&= 3{,}847 - 7 \times 512 \\
&= 3{,}847 - 3{,}584 \\
&= 263 \text{ bytes (residue within the 8th sector)} \\
R_{\text{slack}} &= 512 - 263 \\
R_{\text{slack}} &= 249 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The 8th sector (the file's last sector) holds 263 bytes of file data plus 249 bytes of RAM residue.

### Step 3 — Compute Drive Slack

Drive slack is the *gap* between the end of the last used sector and the end of the last cluster. Since our file ends mid-sector and the cluster contains exactly 8 sectors (sectors 0 through 7), sectors 8 through 7 of the cluster are *all* drive slack, and within sector 7, the bytes from $263$ to $511$ are also drive slack. However, the conventional formula simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{slack}} &= F_{\text{slack}} - R_{\text{slack}} \\
D_{\text{slack}} &= 249 - 249 \\
D_{\text{slack}} &= 0 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Because $3{,}847 \bmod 512 = 263$ and the cluster has exactly 8 sectors, the *file slack* and *RAM slack* are numerically equal in this edge case. The drive slack would be **non-zero** only when the file ends in a sector that is *not* the last sector of a cluster.

### Step 4 — Sanity Check (alternate path)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of full sectors in last cluster} &= \lfloor F_{\text{size}} / S \rfloor = \lfloor 3{,}847 / 512 \rfloor = 7 \\
\text{Last used sector index} &= 7 \text{ (zero-based)} \\
\text{Cluster has } K &= 8 \text{ sectors, indices } 0 \ldots 7 \\
\text{Drive slack sectors} &= 8 - 1 - 7 = 0 \text{ sectors} \\
\text{Within-sector drive slack} &= (S - 1) - 263 + 1 = 249 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(inside sector 7)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The above confirms the formula result: in this geometry, the RAM slack absorbs the within-sector residue, leaving $D_{\text{slack}} = 0$ at the cluster granularity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Slack Ratio for Forensic Triage

> [!NOTE]
> The *slack ratio* $\sigma$ is a metric the KTU examiner uses to assess the **forensic richness** of a volume.

**Given:**
- $F_{\text{slack}} = 249$ bytes
- $C = 4{,}096$ bytes

**Step 1 — Apply the ratio formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \dfrac{F_{\text{slack}}}{C} \times 100 \\
\sigma &= \dfrac{249}{4{,}096} \times 100 \\
\sigma &= 0.060791 \times 100 \\
\sigma &\approx 6.08\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret.**

About **6 % of every randomly sampled cluster** is forensic slack. On a 1 TB drive with 4,096-byte clusters (~244 million clusters), the cumulative slack space is enormous — a treasure trove of residual artifacts.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Multi-Hash Confidence (Hash Set Intersection)

Autopsy's Hash Lookup module can compare a file against multiple hash databases simultaneously. The probability that a malicious file *evades* all $n$ independent hash checks is:

**Step 1 — Define the single-check evasion probability.**

For each hash, the probability of a *false negative* (a malicious file not present in the database) is $p$ (assumed small, e.g., $p = 0.01$ for a comprehensive known-bad set).

**Step 2 — Define the joint evasion probability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}_{\text{evade}} &= p^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the detection confidence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}_{\text{match}} &= 1 - p^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example with three independent hash databases.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= 0.01 \\
n &= 3 \\
\mathbb{P}_{\text{match}} &= 1 - (0.01)^{3} \\
&= 1 - 0.000001 \\
&= 0.999999 \\
&= 99.9999\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

With three independent hash databases, evasion probability drops to **one in a million** — a near-certain identification. This is why Autopsy supports **simultaneous MD5 + SHA-1 + SHA-256** matching.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Unallocated Space Estimation

**Given:**
- Total volume size $T = 500 \text{ GB} = 500 \times 2^{30} \text{ bytes}$
- Cluster size $C = 4{,}096 \text{ bytes} = 2^{12} \text{ bytes}$

**Step 1 — Compute the total cluster count.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{T}{C} \\
N &= \dfrac{500 \times 2^{30}}{2^{12}} \\
N &= 500 \times 2^{18} \\
N &= 500 \times 262{,}144 \\
N &= 131{,}072{,}000 \text{ clusters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Read the used cluster count from the NTFS $Bitmap.**

Suppose the OS reports $N_{\text{used}} = 98{,}304{,}000$ clusters (75 %).

**Step 3 — Compute unallocated clusters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= N - N_{\text{used}} \\
U &= 131{,}072{,}000 - 98{,}304{,}000 \\
U &= 32{,}768{,}000 \text{ clusters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes for forensic disk-space budget.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{bytes}} &= U \times C \\
U_{\text{bytes}} &= 32{,}768{,}000 \times 4{,}096 \\
U_{\text{bytes}} &= 134{,}217{,}728{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
U_{\text{bytes}} &= 125 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

125 GB of unallocated space exists where deleted-file residues may be carved. This is the **search universe** for the carving ingest module.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — File System Artifact vs. Evidence Type

> This matrix is a **high-density KTU revision artifact**. Examiners often present a single artifact name and ask the student to identify the file system and evidence type.

| # | Artifact Name | File System / Location | Tool Used | Evidence Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `$MFT` | NTFS, Entry 0 | Autopsy Tree → $MFT | File metadata timeline |
| 2 | `$LogFile` | NTFS | Autopsy → LogFile Parser | Transaction journal |
| 3 | `$UsnJrnl` | NTFS | Autopsy → Recent Activity | File system change log |
| 4 | FAT table | FAT12/16/32 | `fsstat` from TSK | Allocation chain |
| 5 | Volume Boot Record | All | Autopsy → Image Details | File system parameters |
| 6 | $I (Index) file | NTFS folder | `fls -r` | Folder timestamps |
| 7 | Alternate Data Stream | NTFS $DATA attr | `icat` with attribute ID | Hidden data |
| 8 | File Slack | All | Hex viewer / Autopsy Raw | Residual memory data |
| 9 | `Prefetch` files | Windows OS | Autopsy Recent Activity | Program execution |
| 10 | LNK files | Windows shortcut | Autopsy Recent Docs | User-clicked files |
| 11 | Registry hives | NTUSER.DAT, SYSTEM | Autopsy Registry Parser | User/system config |
| 12 | Recycle Bin `$I` file | NTFS | Autopsy Deleted Files | Original path + deletion time |
| 13 | Page/Swap file | `pagefile.sys` | Autopsy Keyword Search | Memory dump on disk |
| 14 | Hibernation file | `hiberfil.sys` | Volatility (companion tool) | Full memory state |
| 15 | Event logs | `.evtx` | Autopsy Log Parser | System/security events |

---

## 3.7 Forensic Soundness Equation — The Order of Volatility

Following RFC 3227, evidence must be acquired in order of **decreasing volatility**. A KTU viva question is: *"Why do we image the RAM before the disk?"*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{RAM}} &\sim \text{seconds} \\
\tau_{\text{Process State}} &\sim \text{minutes} \\
\tau_{\text{Network}} &\sim \text{minutes-to-hours} \\
\tau_{\text{Disk}} &\sim \text{years} \\
\tau_{\text{Optical/Backup}} &\sim \text{decades}
\end{aligned}
$$

The longest-lived evidence is acquired first *only if* the more volatile evidence has already been preserved; otherwise, volatile evidence is preserved first. For **Module 2 (File System Analysis)**, the disk is long-lived, so a **bit-stream image** (e.g., via `dd`, `FTK Imager`, or `Guymager`) is taken and **write-blocked** at the hardware level before analysis.

---

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example — Hash Verification Workflow

**Scenario:** A forensic analyst has extracted a file `evidence.exe` (2,048,576 bytes) and wants to verify its integrity.

**Step 1 — Compute the MD5 hash.**

Autopsy's Hash Lookup module invokes OpenSSL internally:

$$
\mathcal{H}_{\text{MD5}}(F) = \text{OpenSSL}_{\text{MD5}}(\text{evidence.exe}) = \texttt{d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the SHA-1 hash.**

$$
\mathcal{H}_{\text{SHA1}}(F) = \text{OpenSSL}_{\text{SHA1}}(\text{evidence.exe}) = \texttt{da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the SHA-256 hash.**

$$
\mathcal{H}_{\text{SHA256}}(F) = \text{OpenSSL}_{\text{SHA256}}(\text{evidence.exe}) = \texttt{e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare against NSRL (National Software Reference Library).**

If the MD5 is present in the NSRL **RDS (Reference Data Set)**, the file is **known-good** and can be deprioritized. If present in a custom known-bad set, the file is flagged for further analysis.

**Step 5 — Document.**

The chain-of-custody form records:
- Original hash triple
- Timestamp of verification
- Tool version (Autopsy 4.x)
- Operator signature
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Autopsy End-to-End Investigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire Bit-Stream Image] --> B[Verify Image Hash MD5 SHA1 SHA256]
    B --> C[Create New Autopsy Case]
    C --> D[Add Data Source Type Disk Image File Folder]
    D --> E[Select Ingest Modules]
    E --> F[Run Ingest Pipeline]
    F --> G[Analyze Tree Views]
    G --> H[Keyword and Timeline Analysis]
    H --> I[File Carving and Recovery]
    I --> J[Hash Set Matching]
    J --> K[Generate Forensic Report]
    K --> L[Archive Case and Chain of Custody]
    L --> M[Present Evidence in Court]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each node represents a **discrete forensic stage** in the Autopsy workflow. The arrows represent the **mandatory sequence** — skipping a stage (e.g., hash verification) is a **fatal procedural error** per KTU lab evaluation.

---

## 4.2 NTFS File System Architecture (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Vol1[NTFS Volume Layout]
        VBR1[VBR Boot Sector 512 Bytes]
        VBR1 --> MFT0[$MFT Master File Table]
        VBR1 --> MFTM[$MFTMirr Backup]
        VBR1 --> LOGF[$LogFile Journal]
        VBR1 --> VOLU[$Volume Information]
        VBR1 --> DETF[$Define Attributes]
        VBR1 --> ROOT[$Root Directory Index]
        VBR1 --> BMAP[$Bitmap Cluster Map]
        VBR1 --> BOOT[$Boot Sector Backup]
        VBR1 --> BAD[$Bad Cluster File]
        VBR1 --> SEC[$Secure Security Descriptors]
        VBR1 --> USNJ[$UsnJrnl Change Journal]
        VBR1 --> QUOT[$Quota Tracking]
        MFT0 --> DATA[Data Area User Files]
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **VBR** anchors the volume. The system files (`$MFT`, `$LogFile`, `$Bitmap`, etc.) are themselves files with reserved MFT entries (0 through ~10). The **Data Area** is where user files and their slack space physically reside.

---

## 4.3 Autopsy Ingest Module Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMG[Disk Image] --> IR1[Hash Lookup]
    IMG --> IR2[File Type ID]
    IMG --> IR3[Extension Mismatch]
    IMG --> IR4[Keyword Index]
    IMG --> IR5[EXIF Parser]
    IMG --> IR6[Email Parser]
    IMG --> IR7[Recent Activity]
    IMG --> IR8[Plaso Timeline]
    IR1 --> DB[(Central Case Database SQLite)]
    IR2 --> DB
    IR3 --> DB
    IR4 --> DB
    IR5 --> DB
    IR6 --> DB
    IR7 --> DB
    IR8 --> DB
    DB --> UI[Autopsy GUI Presentation Layer]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** All ingest modules write their results to a **centralized SQLite case database**. This decouples analysis from the raw image and enables multi-user collaboration on the same case.

---

## 4.4 File Allocation vs. Deletion Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Allocated: User creates file
    Allocated --> Modified: User edits file
    Modified --> Modified: Re-saved
    Modified --> Deleted: User deletes file
    Deleted --> Unallocated: MFT entry unlinked FAT chain cleared
    Unallocated --> Recovered: Forensic carving finds signature
    Unallocated --> Overwritten: New file allocated to same cluster
    Recovered --> Analyzed: Autopsy presents to analyst
    Analyzed --> [*]
    Overwritten --> [*]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The transition from `Deleted` to `Unallocated` is the **point of forensic opportunity**. The data is intact but the metadata pointers are gone. Autopsy's **carving module** exploits the time window between `Unallocated` and `Overwritten`.

---

## 4.5 Slack Space Anatomy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cluster C 4096 bytes] --> B[File Data 3847 bytes]
    B --> C[RAM Slack 249 bytes]
    C --> D[Sector Boundary 512 byte line]
    D --> E[Drive Slack 0 bytes in this geometry]
    E --> F[Cluster End]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Visualizes the **nested relationship** between file data, RAM slack, and drive slack within a single 4,096-byte cluster. The RAM slack fills the *incomplete last sector*; the drive slack fills the *unused sectors* (none in this example).

---

## 4.6 Hash Verification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EVD[Evidence File on Disk] --> H1[Compute MD5 128 bit]
    EVD --> H2[Compute SHA1 160 bit]
    EVD --> H3[Compute SHA256 256 bit]
    H1 --> CMP{Hash Match with Reference}
    H2 --> CMP
    H3 --> CMP
    CMP -->|Yes Match| OK[Mark as Known Good or Known Bad]
    CMP -->|No Match| UNK[Mark as Unknown Tag for Manual Review]
    OK --> LOG[Update Chain of Custody Log]
    UNK --> LOG
    LOG --> REP[Append to Forensic Report]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
