# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Mark distribution is strictly **3 marks for Part A** and **14 marks for Part B** (with internal choice). Sub-parts of Part B carry **7 marks each**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term *file slack* and explain why it is relevant in forensic investigations.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **File slack** is the difference between the **logical end of a file** (as reported by the file system) and the **physical end of the last cluster** allocated to that file. Formally,
> $$F_{\text{slack}} = C - (F_{\text{size}} \bmod C)$$
> where $C$ is the cluster size and $F_{\text{size}}$ is the file's logical size. **(1 mark)**
>
> File slack is composed of two sub-portions: **RAM slack** (the bytes padding the last sector to a sector boundary) and **drive slack** (the bytes from the end of the last used sector to the end of the cluster). **(1 mark)**
>
> **Forensic relevance:** When a file is deleted, the slack space is *not* automatically wiped. It may contain fragments of previously allocated files, residual RAM content (including passwords, encryption keys, or fragments of sensitive documents), and metadata remnants. Investigators routinely search slack space to recover evidence that the user thought was securely deleted. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three ingest modules of Autopsy and briefly state their forensic purpose.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per module):**
>
> 1. **Hash Lookup Module** — Computes MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256 hashes of every file in the image and compares them against known-good (NSRL RDS) and known-bad hash sets. Allows rapid triage and identification of contraband or system files. **(1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Recent Activity Module** — Parses Windows-specific artifacts such as Prefetch files, UserAssist registry keys, and LNK shortcut files to reconstruct the **execution and access history** of programs on the suspect system. **(1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Keyword Search Module** — Builds a full-text index of all strings in the image and supports live search and regular expressions. Used to surface evidence containing specific terms (e.g., "bomb", "fraud", account numbers). **(1 mark)**
>
> *(Acceptable alternatives: File Type Identification, Extension Mismatch Detector, EXIF Parser, Email Parser, Plaso/Log2Timeline, File Carving — each with correct purpose statement.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **two sub-parts, 7 marks each**. The student is given an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both questions below are independent and equivalent in mark weight.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the **NTFS file system**. With a neat diagram, describe the role of the **$MFT**, **$Bitmap**, **$LogFile**, and **$UsnJrnl** system files in forensic analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Windows NTFS volume has the following parameters extracted from its boot sector:

- Bytes per sector: $S = 512$
- Sectors per cluster: $K = 8$
- A text file `notes.txt` is 5,830 bytes in size

Calculate:
   (i) The **cluster size** in bytes. **(1 mark)**
   (ii) The **file slack**. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The **RAM slack**. **(2 marks)**
   (iv) The **drive slack**. **(1 mark)**
   (v) The **slack ratio** as a percentage. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) — NTFS Architecture (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Volume Boot Record (1 mark)**

The NTFS volume begins with a **VBR (Volume Boot Record)** at sector 0 of the partition. The VBR contains the **BIOS Parameter Block (BPB)** which specifies the bytes per sector, sectors per cluster, total sectors, and the **location of the $MFT**.

**Step 2 — Master File Table ($MFT) (2 marks)**

The `$MFT` is the heart of NTFS. It is a structured relational file in which every file and directory on the volume is represented by a **record** (typically 1,024 bytes). Each record contains **attributes** (metadata streams) such as `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (timestamps, permissions), `$FILE_NAME` (long and short names), `$DATA` (the file content or a pointer to it), and `$INDEX_ROOT` (for directories). In forensic analysis, the `$MFT` is the **primary source of timestamp and filename information** for all files, including those that have been deleted (because the MFT entry persists in the slack of the MFT itself until it is recycled).

**Step 3 — Bitmap (1 mark)**

The `$Bitmap` file is a single-bit-per-cluster array that indicates which clusters are **allocated (1)** and which are **free (0)**. Forensic use: by scanning the `$Bitmap` against the actual on-disk content, an analyst can detect **slack space inconsistencies, hidden partitions, or anti-forensic wiping tools** that clear the bitmap without zeroing the data.

**Step 4 — LogFile (1 mark)**

The `$LogFile` is the NTFS **transactional journal**. Every metadata change (file create, rename, delete, attribute update) is first written to `$LogFile` as a **redo log entry** before being committed to the MFT. Forensic use: even if an MFT entry is overwritten or corrupted, the `$LogFile` may still contain the **transactional history** allowing partial recovery of the metadata state.

**Step 5 — UsnJrnl (1 mark)**

The **$UsnJrnl** (Update Sequence Number Journal) records a sequential **USN change record** for every file system modification. Each record includes the filename, the MFT reference number, the timestamp, and the **reason flag** (data extend, data truncate, file create, file delete, rename, etc.). Forensic use: it provides a **chronological audit log** of file system activity that is *complementary* to the MFT's per-file timestamps.

**Step 6 — Diagram (1 mark)**

*(Student should reproduce a block diagram with the VBR at the top, the $MFT as the central spine, and the $Bitmap, $LogFile, $UsnJrnl as lateral system files — see Section 4.2.)*

---

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$S = 512$ bytes, $K = 8$, $F_{\text{size}} = 5{,}830$ bytes.

**(i) Cluster size (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= S \times K \\
C &= 512 \times 8 \\
C &= 4{,}096 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) File slack (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{slack}} &= C - (F_{\text{size}} \bmod C) \\
F_{\text{size}} \bmod C &= 5{,}830 \bmod 4{,}096 \\
&= 5{,}830 - 1 \times 4{,}096 \\
&= 1{,}734 \\
F_{\text{slack}} &= 4{,}096 - 1{,}734 \\
&= 2{,}362 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) RAM slack (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{slack}} &= S - (F_{\text{size}} \bmod S) \\
F_{\text{size}} \bmod S &= 5{,}830 \bmod 512 \\
&= 5{,}830 - 11 \times 512 \\
&= 5{,}830 - 5{,}632 \\
&= 198 \\
R_{\text{slack}} &= 512 - 198 \\
&= 314 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Drive slack (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{slack}} &= F_{\text{slack}} - R_{\text{slack}} \\
&= 2{,}362 - 314 \\
&= 2{,}048 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Slack ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \dfrac{F_{\text{slack}}}{C} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}362}{4{,}096} \times 100 \\
&\approx 57.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $C = 4{,}096$ bytes, $F_{\text{slack}} = 2{,}362$ bytes, $R_{\text{slack}} = 314$ bytes, $D_{\text{slack}} = 2{,}048$ bytes, $\sigma \approx 57.67\%$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is **Autopsy**? Explain the **ingest module architecture** of Autopsy and describe the role of at least **four** ingest modules in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a forensic examination, an analyst recovers a deleted file and needs to verify its integrity. The file size is $1{,}048{,}576$ bytes ($1$ MiB). The MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 hashes are computed and compared against three independent known-bad hash databases. If the probability of a false negative (a malicious file not being in any one database) is $p = 0.02$, calculate:
   (i) The probability that the file **evades all three** hash checks. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) The probability that the file is **detected by at least one** database. **(1 mark)**
   (iii) If the analyst now adds **two more** independent databases, what is the new detection probability? **(2 marks)**
   (iv) Comment on the **forensic value of using multiple hash algorithms** versus a single algorithm. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) — Autopsy and Ingest Modules (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark)**

Autopsy is an **open-source, GUI-based digital forensics platform** developed by Basis Technology and now maintained as a community project under the Apache License. It is built atop **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)** and integrates additional tools such as **Plaso/Log2Timeline** and **Volatility** for comprehensive forensic analysis.

**Step 2 — Ingest Module Concept (2 marks)**

An **ingest module** is a Java or Python plugin that processes every file in a case and writes results to a central **SQLite case database**. Modules run **automatically** when a data source is added. The architecture is **modular** — analysts can enable or disable modules per case, and third parties can develop custom modules.

**Step 3 — Four Ingest Modules in Detail (4 marks — 1 mark each)**

**1. Hash Lookup Module:** Computes cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) of every file and compares them to the NSRL (National Software Reference Library) Reference Data Set of known system files, plus any user-supplied known-good or known-bad hash sets. **Forensic value:** Filters out ~70–90 % of files (operating system binaries, common applications) and flags files of interest.

**2. File Type Identification Module:** Uses **libmagic** (the same engine behind the Unix `file` command) to determine the *true* file type from its **magic bytes / signature** in the header, independent of the file extension. **Forensic value:** Detects **masquerading** — a file named `report.pdf` that is actually an `exe` or a ZIP archive. This is a classic anti-forensics technique.

**3. Recent Activity Module:** Parses Windows-specific artifacts — **Prefetch files** (in `C:\Windows\Prefetch`), **UserAssist registry keys** (which encode the last 19 execution times of each program), **LNK shortcut files** (in `Recent\`), and **Jump Lists** — to reconstruct the **execution history** of programs on the suspect system. **Forensic value:** Establishes the **last-known program execution state** even after the executable itself is deleted.

**4. Keyword Search Module:** Builds a full-text **inverted index** of all strings extracted from files in the image, supporting both literal and regular-expression search. **Forensic value:** Allows rapid surfacing of evidence containing specific terms (e.g., "ransom", "drug", IP addresses, account numbers, email addresses) without manual string scanning.

*(Acceptable alternative modules: EXIF Parser, Email Parser, Plaso/Log2Timeline, File Carving, Android Analyzer, Registry Parser — each with a 1-mark explanation.)*

---

**(b) — Hash Detection Probability (7 marks)**

**Given:** $p = 0.02$, $n = 3$ (initial), then $n = 5$ (extended).

**(i) Evasion probability with 3 databases (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}_{\text{evade}} &= p^{n} \\
&= (0.02)^{3} \\
&= 8 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 0.000008 \\
&= 0.0008\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Detection probability with 3 databases (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}_{\text{match}} &= 1 - \mathbb{P}_{\text{evade}} \\
&= 1 - 0.000008 \\
&= 0.999992 \\
&= 99.9992\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Detection probability with 5 databases (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}_{\text{evade}}^{\text{new}} &= (0.02)^{5} \\
&= 32 \times 10^{-10} \\
&= 3.2 \times 10^{-9} \\
\mathbb{P}_{\text{match}}^{\text{new}} &= 1 - 3.2 \times 10^{-9} \\
&\approx 0.9999999968 \\
&= 99.99999968\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Forensic comment (2 marks):**

Using **multiple independent hash algorithms** (e.g., MD5 + SHA-1 + SHA-256) is **not strictly equivalent** to using multiple databases of the same algorithm. If an adversary crafts a **hash collision** for MD5, the SHA-256 of the same file will differ, and the file will not match a SHA-256-based known-bad set. Thus, using multiple algorithms provides **defense in depth against collision attacks**. Furthermore, MD5 is cryptographically broken; relying on it alone is insufficient. SHA-256, with its 256-bit output space, has a **collision probability of $2^{-128}$** (by the birthday bound), which is computationally infeasible to defeat. The forensic best practice is therefore to **record all three hashes** for chain-of-custody integrity, and to **match on SHA-256** as the primary decision criterion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FAT with NTFS metadata** — FAT uses a 32-byte directory entry; NTFS uses MFT records with multiple attributes. Mixing them up loses **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — The cluster size derivation must show $S \times K$ with explicit units (bytes/sector × sectors/cluster = bytes). Skipping units loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up RAM slack vs. drive slack** — RAM slack is *inside* the last sector; drive slack is *after* the last sector but *inside* the last cluster. The two are **not interchangeable**.
> 4. **Omitting the modulus step in the slack calculation** — Writing $F_{\text{slack}} = C - F_{\text{size}}$ directly is **wrong** when $F_{\text{size}} > C$. Always show $F_{\text{size}} \bmod C$.
> 5. **Stating "Autopsy is a command-line tool"** — Autopsy is the **GUI front-end**; the CLI backend is **The Sleuth Kit**. This single sentence has cost students 2 marks in viva.
> 6. **Skipping the chain-of-custody step** — Every KTU forensic answer **must** end with a chain-of-custody note (hash, timestamp, operator, tool version). Forgetting it loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This checklist is the **last-page glance** before entering the KTU exam hall.

### A. Conceptual Vocabulary (must know)
- [ ] File system forensics
- [ ] Autopsy & The Sleuth Kit (TSK)
- [ ] Bit-stream image, write-blocker
- [ ] Cluster, sector, allocation unit
- [ ] MFT, $MFTMirr, $Bitmap, $LogFile, $UsnJrnl
- [ ] FAT12 / FAT16 / FAT32
- [ ] Alternate Data Streams (ADS)
- [ ] File slack, RAM slack, drive slack, volume slack
- [ ] Unallocated space
- [ ] MAC times (Modified, Accessed, Created/Changed)
- [ ] Magic bytes / file signature
- [ ] Chain of custody

### B. Formulae (must memorize)
- [ ] $C = S \times K$ — cluster size
- [ ] $F_{\text{slack}} = C - (F_{\text{size}} \bmod C)$
- [ ] $R_{\text{slack}} = S - (F_{\text{size}} \bmod S)$
- [ ] $D_{\text{slack}} = F_{\text{slack}} - R_{\text{slack}}$
- [ ] $\sigma = (F_{\text{slack}} / C) \times 100$
- [ ] $\mathbb{P}_{\text{match}} = 1 - p^{n}$

### C. Tools (must recognize)
- [ ] **Autopsy** (GUI) + **The Sleuth Kit** (CLI)
- [ ] **FTK Imager** (image acquisition)
- [ ] **Volatility** (RAM forensics — companion tool)
- [ ] **Plaso / Log2Timeline** (super-timeline)
- [ ] **NIST CFTT** validation suite

### D. Ingest Modules (must list 4+)
- [ ] Hash Lookup
- [ ] File Type Identification
- [ ] Extension Mismatch Detector
- [ ] Keyword Search
- [ ] Recent Activity (Prefetch, LNK, UserAssist)
- [ ] EXIF Parser
- [ ] Email Parser
- [ ] Plaso Timeline
- [ ] Carving
- [ ] Registry Parser

### E. Procedural Sequence (must recite in order)
1. Acquire bit-stream image (write-blocker, forensic duplicator)
2. Verify image hash (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256)
3. Create Autopsy case file
4. Add image as data source
5. Select and run ingest modules
6. Analyze Tree Views, Result Views, Timeline
7. Apply keyword search and hash set matching
8. Carve deleted files from unallocated space
9. Document findings in forensic report
10. Archive case with chain of custody

### F. Numerical Defaults (must memorize)
- [ ] Bytes per sector: **512** (legacy) or **4096** (4 Kn)
- [ ] Default NTFS cluster: **4 KB**
- [ ] MFT entry size: **1,024 bytes**
- [ ] MD5 length: **32 hex chars**
- [ ] SHA-1 length: **40 hex chars**
- [ ] SHA-256 length: **64 hex chars**
- [ ] Deleted-file marker in FAT: **`0xE5`** (first byte of 8.3 name)

### G. Forensic Soundness Principles
- [ ] **Never** work on the original evidence — always work on a verified image.
- [ ] **Hash** the image at acquisition, before analysis, and after analysis.
- [ ] **Document** every action with timestamp and operator.
- [ ] **Use** write-blockers to prevent accidental modification.
- [ ] **Follow** RFC 3227 — order of volatility for volatile evidence.
- [ ] **Prefer** multiple hash algorithms for defense in depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Acquire → Verify → Image → Analyze → Hash → Report → Custody."* Master this seven-word loop and the 14-mark Part B question is yours.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Protocol Analysis Using Wireshark and nmap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCL607 - CYBER FORENSICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Network Protocol Analysis Using Wireshark and nmap

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Network Protocol Analysis** is the systematic process of intercepting, decoding, and evaluating data packets traversing a computer network to investigate communication behavior, detect anomalies, and gather legally admissible digital evidence. In the context of cyber forensics, it is the foundational Live Acquisition technique used to reconstruct communication sessions, identify malicious traffic, and verify chain-of-custody during incident response.

**Wireshark** is a free, open-source **packet analyzer** (formerly known as Ethereal) used for network troubleshooting, analysis, software and communications protocol development, and education. It operates at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) through the Application Layer (Layer 7) of the **OSI model**.

**nmap (Network Mapper)** is a free and open-source utility for **network discovery and security auditing**. Many systems and network administrators use it for tasks such as **network inventory**, managing service upgrade schedules, and monitoring host or service uptime. In forensics, nmap is used for **port scanning, OS fingerprinting, and service enumeration** during the pre-investigation reconnaissance phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is mandatory under the Live Forensics / Network Forensics module. Students are expected to capture live traffic, apply display filters, and generate forensic reports. Viva-voce questions frequently target the difference between **promiscuous mode** and **monitor mode** capture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal System

Imagine a city's entire postal system. Every letter (packet) has a sender address (Source IP), a receiver address (Destination IP), a service type (Protocol — like First Class or Registered Post), and content (Payload). 

- **Wireshark** is like a **transparent post office sorting room**. Instead of just forwarding letters, every postman dumps their bag on a glass table. You can pick up any letter, read the outside envelope (Headers), and even open the sealed letter to read the content (Payload). The different colored stamps are the **Protocols** (TCP, UDP, HTTP, DNS).

- **nmap** is like a **building inspector walking down a street**. He walks up to every building (Host), checks how many doors and windows are open (**Open Ports**), reads the nameplate to see what business operates there (**Service Banner Grabbing**), and guesses the architectural style of the building (**OS Fingerprinting**).

## 1.3 Key Terminology Reference

| Term | Definition | Forensic Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Promiscuous Mode** | NIC accepts all packets, not just those addressed to it | Required for Wireshark to see other hosts' traffic |
| **Monitor Mode** | Wi-Fi card captures all wireless frames in the air | Required for 802.11 wireless sniffing |
| **Three-Way Handshake** | SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK connection establishment | Vital to identify port scans and DDoS origins |
| **PCAP File** | Packet Capture file format (.pcap / .pcapng) | Standard forensic evidence format, accepted in court |
| **Banner Grabbing** | Reading service software/version information | Identifies vulnerable services for exploitation tracing |
| **Fingerprinting** | Identifying OS/Software based on stack behavior | Links an attack to a specific system type |

> [!NOTE]
> A standard Ethernet frame carries **1500 bytes** of MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) by default. Jumbo frames can extend this up to **9000 bytes**, a critical detail when reconstructing large exfiltrated files from packet captures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Wireshark Architecture (Capture & Display Engines)

Wireshark is built using a modular architecture that separates the **packet capture engine** from the **packet dissection and display engine**. This separation is what makes it a forensic-grade tool.

### 2.1.1 The Capture Pipeline

1. **Network Interface Card (NIC)** receives raw bits from the physical medium (copper/fiber/radio).
2. The NIC's driver, when set to **promiscuous mode**, forwards every frame to the kernel's **packet capture library** (`libpcap` on Linux/macOS, `Npcap`/`WinPcap` on Windows).
3. The captured frames are passed to the **capture engine** (`dumpcap`), which writes them to a `.pcapng` file with strict **timestamps** (microsecond precision).
4. The **dissection engine** (`tshark` for CLI, Wireshark GUI for visual) parses the binary stream against known protocol **dissectors** to build a layered decode tree.
5. **Display filters** are applied at the user level (e.g., `tcp.port == 80`) without modifying the original capture file — this is critical for evidence integrity.

### 2.1.2 The Dissector Tree

Every packet in Wireshark is decoded as a hierarchical tree:

> **Frame** → **Ethernet II** → **Internet Protocol Version 4** → **Transmission Control Protocol** → **Hypertext Transfer Protocol**

Each layer is a **dissector** that decodes the header fields of that protocol and passes the payload to the next dissector. The **"Follow TCP Stream"** function reconstructs the entire bidirectional session by reassembling TCP segments in sequence order.

## 2.2 nmap Operation Phases

nmap follows a strict, phased approach to network discovery, often remembered as the **"nmap scan stages"**:

1. **Host Discovery (-sn)** — Sends ICMP echo, TCP SYN to port 443, TCP ACK to port 80, and ICMP timestamp to determine if a host is alive.
2. **Port Scanning** — Probes ports to determine their **state**: `open`, `closed`, `filtered`, `unfiltered`, `open|filtered`, or `closed|filtered`.
3. **Service & Version Detection (-sV)** — Probes open ports and matches responses against the `nmap-services` and `nmap-service-probes` databases.
4. **OS Detection (-O)** — Analyzes TCP ISN sampling, IP ID sequence, TCP timestamp option, and the **DF (Don't Fragment)** bit to fingerprint the target OS.
5. **NSE Scripting Engine (--script)** — Runs Lua scripts for advanced enumeration (vulnerability detection, brute force, malware backdoor detection).
6. **Traceroute/Topology** — Discovers intermediate routers using varying TTL values.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Cheat Sheet

| Category | Command / Filter | Purpose | Forensic Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wireshark Capture | `tcpdump -i eth0 -w evidence.pcap` | CLI capture (libpcap) | Headless server forensics |
| Wireshark Display | `http.request.method == "POST"` | Filter HTTP POST requests | Detect data exfiltration |
| Wireshark Display | `dns.qry.name contains "malicious"` | Filter DNS queries | Identify C2 beaconing |
| Wireshark Display | `ip.src == 192.168.1.5` | Filter by source IP | Isolate suspect host traffic |
| Wireshark Display | `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0` | Find SYN packets | Detect SYN scan attacks |
| nmap Host Discovery | `nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24` | Ping sweep | Live host inventory |
| nmap Port Scan (Stealth) | `nmap -sS 192.168.1.5` | TCP SYN scan (half-open) | Detect open services without logging |
| nmap Full Connect | `nmap -sT 192.168.1.5` | TCP connect scan | When raw sockets unavailable |
| nmap Service Detect | `nmap -sV 192.168.1.5` | Probe service versions | Identify vulnerable software |
| nmap OS Fingerprint | `nmap -O 192.168.1.5` | Detect remote OS | Attribute attack source |
| nmap Aggressive | `nmap -A 192.168.1.5` | OS + Version + Scripts + Traceroute | Comprehensive audit |
| nmap UDP Scan | `nmap -sU 192.168.1.5` | Scan UDP services | Find DNS/SNMP/NTP open ports |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **-sS (SYN stealth) scan** never completes the TCP handshake, so it does not appear in most application logs. However, modern **IDS/IPS** and Wireshark capture can still detect it because the **SYN packets are visible on the wire** — there is no such thing as a "truly stealthy" scan, only "less noisy" scans.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

In **Incident Response (IR)**, Wireshark is the gold standard for confirming whether a breach actually occurred. For instance, the **2013 Target Corporation breach** was ultimately reconstructed using packet captures showing exfiltration of 40 million credit card numbers to attacker-controlled servers. 

In **penetration testing**, nmap is the **first tool run** (reconnaissance phase) to build the attack surface map. The output directly drives the choice of subsequent exploitation tools (Metasploit modules are named after open ports found by nmap).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Lab Walkthrough

## 3.1 KTU Lab Procedure — Experiment 2 (Network Protocol Analysis)

The standard KTU 2024 Scheme lab record for this experiment follows a structured procedure. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step methodology students must document in their **Lab Record Manual**.

### 3.1.1 Part A: Wireshark Packet Capture & Analysis

**Step 1 — Environment Setup**
Ensure Wireshark is installed. On Kali Linux/Ubuntu:
```
sudo apt update && sudo apt install wireshark
```
Add the user to the `wireshark` group to allow non-root capture:
```
sudo usermod -aG wireshark $USER
```

**Step 2 — Start a Live Capture**
Open Wireshark, select the active network interface (e.g., `eth0` or `wlan0`), and click the **blue shark-fin icon** to start. Alternatively, double-click the interface. A timestamped list of packets begins populating in real-time.

**Step 3 — Generate Traffic for Analysis**
Open a terminal in parallel and execute:
```
ping -c 5 www.google.com
curl -I http://testphp.vulnweb.com
nslookup ktu.edu.in
```

**Step 4 — Apply Display Filters**
Stop the capture (red square icon). In the display filter bar, type:
- `icmp` → to isolate the ping traffic
- `dns` → to isolate the DNS query/response
- `http.request` → to see the curl request only

**Step 5 — Reconstruct the Session**
Right-click any TCP packet from the curl session → **Follow** → **TCP Stream**. A new window shows the complete request/response in ASCII, perfect for forensic reporting.

**Step 6 — Export Evidence**
Navigate to **File → Export Specified Packets** and save as `evidence_case01.pcapng`. Compute the **SHA-256 hash** for chain-of-custody:
```
sha256sum evidence_case01.pcapng
```

### 3.1.2 Part B: nmap Network Reconnaissance

**Step 1 — Live Host Discovery**
```
nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 -oN hosts.txt
```
This performs an **ARP ping scan** on the local subnet (the fastest and most reliable method) and lists all live hosts with their MAC addresses.

**Step 2 — TCP SYN Stealth Scan**
```
nmap -sS 192.168.1.5 -p 1-1000 -v
```
The `-v` (verbose) flag shows real-time scan progress. The `-p 1-1000` restricts to the most common 1000 ports to reduce scan time.

**Step 3 — Service & Version Detection**
```
nmap -sV 192.168.1.5
```
nmap probes each open port with crafted packets and matches the response banner against its signature database. Output resembles:
```
22/tcp   open  ssh     OpenSSH 8.2p1 Ubuntu
80/tcp   open  http    Apache httpd 2.4.41
3306/tcp open  mysql   MySQL 5.7.33
```

**Step 4 — OS Fingerprinting**
```
sudo nmap -O 192.168.1.5
```
Requires **root privileges** because it uses raw socket probes. Output:
```
Running: Linux 4.15 - 5.6
OS CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel:4.15
OS details: Linux 4.15 - 5.6
```

**Step 5 — Aggressive Comprehensive Scan**
```
nmap -A 192.168.1.5 -T4
```
Combines OS detection, version detection, script scanning, and traceroute. The `-T4` flag sets the **timing template** to "Aggressive" (parallel probes).

**Step 6 — Generate Forensic Report**
Save outputs in all three nmap formats:
```
nmap -sS -sV -O 192.168.1.5 -oN report.txt -oX report.xml -oG report.gnmap
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Filter Construction Logic

The most common viva question is **"How do you build a complex Wireshark display filter?"** The following matrix trains this analytical skill:

| Investigation Goal | Filter Logic | Explanation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| All HTTP traffic from a suspect | `ip.src == 192.168.1.5 && http` | Boolean AND of two conditions |
| Failed TCP connections | `tcp.flags.reset == 1` | RST flag set indicates aborted connection |
| DNS queries for a specific domain | `dns.qry.name == "evil.com"` | Exact match on query name field |
| Detect port scan (SYN flood) | `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0` | Many SYNs without ACKs = scan |
| Large outbound transfers | `tcp.len > 1000 && ip.dst != 192.168.1.0/24` | High payload to external network |
| ICMP unreachable errors | `icmp.type == 3` | Port unreachable during scan detection |
| SSH brute force attempts | `tcp.dstport == 22 && tcp.flags.syn == 1` | Multiple SYNs to port 22 |
| Reconstruct a file | `http.request.uri contains "secret.pdf"` | Filter to find file transfer |
| TLS handshake only | `tls.handshake.type == 1` | Show only ClientHello messages |
| Time-windowed analysis | `frame.time >= "2024-01-15 10:00:00" && frame.time <= "2024-01-15 11:00:00"` | Restrict to investigation period |

## 3.3 Statistics & Conversations View (Forensic Triage)

Wireshark's **Statistics** menu is often tested in KTU viva. The key views are:

- **Conversations** (`Statistics → Conversations`) — Tabulates all Layer 3 (IP) and Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) conversations with byte counts. Identifies the "top talkers" — the hosts with the most traffic volume.
- **Endpoints** (`Statistics → Endpoints`) — Lists unique MAC/IP endpoints. Useful to count how many devices are on a network.
- **I/O Graphs** (`Statistics → I/O Graphs`) — Plots packet rate over time. A sudden spike at 03:00 AM is highly suspicious in a corporate environment.
- **Protocol Hierarchy** (`Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy`) — Shows percentage distribution of protocols. An unusual presence of **BitTorrent** traffic on a corporate network is an immediate red flag.
- **Expert Information** (`Analyze → Expert Information`) — Highlights warnings, errors, and notes. TCP retransmissions, duplicate ACKs, and resets are flagged here.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Output Analysis Example

Suppose the lab target is `192.168.1.10`. Running `nmap -sS -sV -O 192.168.1.10` produces:

```
PORT     STATE  SERVICE    VERSION
22/tcp   open   ssh        OpenSSH 7.6p1 Ubuntu
80/tcp   open   http       Apache httpd 2.4.29
443/tcp  closed https
3306/tcp open   mysql      MySQL 5.7.25
```

**Analytical Interpretation:**
- Port **22 (SSH)** open → Remote login possible → Check for weak passwords.
- Port **80 (HTTP)** Apache 2.4.29 → Known CVEs exist (e.g., CVE-2021-41773 path traversal).
- Port **3306 (MySQL)** exposed to the network → Should be localhost-only; this is a misconfiguration and forensic finding.
- Port **443 closed** → TLS not directly served; HTTP traffic to the server is unencrypted.

The forensic report would list these as **vulnerabilities and configuration weaknesses** in the system.

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate between Wireshark and nmap. State one forensic use-case for each tool. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Wireshark | nmap |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Primary Function | Packet capture and deep protocol dissection | Network discovery and port scanning |
| OSI Layer Focus | Layers 2 through 7 (full stack decode) | Layers 3 and 4 (mostly transport layer) |
| Operation Mode | Passive — listens to existing traffic | Active — sends probe packets |
| Typical Output | .pcapng file with dissected frames | Text/XML report of hosts, ports, services |
| Forensic Use-Case | Capturing exfiltration traffic to reconstruct data theft | Identifying all exposed services on a compromised server |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for key difference, 1 for use-case)*

### Question 2: What is **promiscuous mode** in a network interface card? Why is it mandatory for Wireshark packet capture? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Promiscuous mode** is an operational mode of a Network Interface Card (NIC) in which the card accepts and forwards to the CPU **all packets** traversing the network medium, regardless of whether the destination MAC address matches the card's own hardware address. By default, a NIC operates in **non-promiscuous (normal) mode** and drops frames not addressed to it, which is a fundamental Ethernet design feature for efficiency.

Wireshark requires promiscuous mode because, in a switched Ethernet network, unicast traffic between two other hosts is normally never delivered to a third machine. Without promiscuous mode, the investigator's machine would only see broadcast traffic, its own traffic, and traffic specifically sent to it — making comprehensive network forensics impossible. Promiscuous mode can usually be enabled by the `ip link set eth0 promisc up` command in Linux or via the Wireshark capture options dialog.

> [!NOTE]
> In modern switched networks, **port mirroring (SPAN)** on the switch is often preferred over promiscuous mode because it provides a copy of all traffic directly to the forensic analyst's port without depending on hub behavior.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for default NIC behavior, 1 for forensic necessity)*

## Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Network Protocol Analysis and Forensic Capture `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **TCP three-way handshake** with proper packet diagram. Demonstrate how Wireshark can be used to detect an **incomplete handshake** indicative of a SYN port scan. Provide the exact display filter and explain the expected packet sequence.

**Model Solution:**

The **TCP three-way handshake** is the foundational connection establishment procedure defined in **RFC 793**. It involves three segments exchanged between a client (Initiator) and a server (Listener):

1. **SYN (Synchronize)** — The client sends a TCP segment with the **SYN flag set to 1**, an **Initial Sequence Number (ISN)** chosen randomly (e.g., `ISNc = 1000`), and optionally a **Maximum Segment Size (MSS)** option. The client enters the `SYN_SENT` state.
2. **SYN-ACK (Synchronize-Acknowledge)** — The server responds with both the **SYN flag and ACK flag set to 1**. It acknowledges the client's ISN by sending `ACK = 1001` (= `ISNc + 1`), and provides its own ISN (e.g., `ISNs = 5000`). The server enters the `SYN_RECEIVED` state.
3. **ACK (Acknowledge)** — The client sends a final segment with the **ACK flag set to 1** and `ACK = 5001` (= `ISNs + 1`). Both sides now enter the `ESTABLISHED` state, and data transfer can begin.

**Packet Sequence Diagram (ASCII representation):**

```
Client                              Server
  | ---- SYN (Seq=1000) ---->      |  [SYN_SENT]
  |                                 |  [SYN_RECEIVED]
  | <--- SYN-ACK (Seq=5000, Ack=1001) --- |
  |                                 |
  | ---- ACK (Seq=1001, Ack=5001) -> |  [ESTABLISHED]
  |                                 |  [ESTABLISHED]
  | <==== DATA TRANSFER ====>       |
```

**Detecting SYN Scan in Wireshark:**

A **TCP SYN scan** (such as nmap `-sS`) only sends the first packet (SYN) and **never sends the final ACK**. The attacker either drops the SYN-ACK response or sends a **RST** to close the half-open connection. The classic forensic signature is:

- A high volume of **SYN packets** from a single source IP to many destination ports.
- **SYN-ACK responses** received from the target (indicating ports are open).
- **No corresponding ACK** packets completing the handshake.
- If the port is closed, the target responds with **RST-ACK**.

**Wireshark Display Filter:**

```
tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0
```

This filter isolates only the initial SYN segments (where SYN=1 and ACK=0). In a normal network, these should always be paired 1:1 with a follow-up ACK from the initiator. A long list of unmatched SYNs from one IP to multiple ports of another IP is **prima facie evidence of a port scan**.

**Steps to Investigate in Wireshark:**

1. Open the suspect `.pcap` capture file.
2. Apply the filter `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0`.
3. Navigate to **Statistics → Conversations** and sort by **Packets** to identify the top talker as the scanner.
4. Right-click the scanner IP, select **Apply as Filter → Selected**, then add `&& tcp.flags.ack == 0` to confirm no completion.
5. Export the filtered packets as a separate evidence file with SHA-256 hash for the forensic report.

*(7 marks — 2 for handshake diagram, 2 for SYN scan theory, 2 for filter application, 1 for investigation procedure)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **nmap port states** `open`, `closed`, `filtered`, and `unfiltered` with example scenarios. Explain the command `nmap -sS -sV -O -A 192.168.1.10` flag by flag.

**Model Solution:**

**nmap Port States (RFC 793 / nmap documentation):**

1. **`open`** — An application is actively accepting TCP connections or UDP packets on this port. The scanner received a positive response. *Example scenario:* A web server listening on port 80, a MySQL daemon on 3306. From a forensic perspective, every open port represents a potential attack surface and must be catalogued.

2. **`closed`** — The port is reachable (no firewall blocking), but no application is listening. The target responds with an **RST (Reset)** segment when probed. *Example scenario:* A desktop computer with no services running, where a SYN probe to port 8080 returns RST. This state is useful because it confirms the host is online even if no services are exposed.

3. **`filtered`** — nmap cannot determine whether the port is open or closed because **packet filtering (firewall, ACL, or NAT rules)** is actively blocking the probe. The probe receives **no response** or an **ICMP unreachable error** (types 3, 9, 10, or 13). *Example scenario:* A corporate firewall drops all incoming SYN packets to port 23 (Telnet). This state is the most common defensive posture for hardened servers.

4. **`unfiltered`** — The port is reachable and responds to probes, but nmap cannot determine if it is open or closed. This state is only returned by the **ACK scan (-sA)**. *Example scenario:* An ACK probe to port 80 returns RST, meaning the port is reachable but its open/closed state requires further probing. This state is a forensic indicator that a firewall is **not** blocking the port, even if no service is listening.

**Flag-by-Flag Explanation of `nmap -sS -sV -O -A 192.168.1.10`:**

| Flag | Meaning | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `-sS` | TCP SYN Stealth Scan | Sends SYN, never completes handshake, requires root |
| `-sV` | Service and Version Detection | Probes open ports to identify software/version |
| `-O` | OS Detection | Uses TCP/IP stack fingerprinting to identify OS |
| `-A` | Aggressive Scan Mode | Enables OS + Version + Script Scan + Traceroute |
| `192.168.1.10` | Target IP | The single host to be scanned |

The combined command performs a **comprehensive forensic audit**: it identifies open ports stealthily, determines the exact software version on each port, fingerprints the operating system, and runs the Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) for vulnerability enumeration — all in a single execution. The output is ideal for inclusion in a forensic investigation report.

*(7 marks — 4 for port states with examples, 3 for flag-by-flag explanation)*

### Question B: Comparative Tool Analysis and Forensic Reporting `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Wireshark display filters** and **Wireshark capture filters**. Explain the syntax of each with two examples. Why are capture filters preferred for high-volume production environments?

**Model Solution:**

Wireshark provides two fundamentally different filtering mechanisms that operate at different stages of the packet processing pipeline:

**Capture Filters (BPF Syntax — Berkeley Packet Filter):**

Capture filters are applied **at the kernel/driver level**, **before** the packet is written to the capture file. They use the **BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter)** syntax inherited from `tcpdump`. Because they discard non-matching packets before they are even saved, they dramatically reduce the size of the capture file and the CPU/memory load on the analysis machine.

Syntax uses **primitive keywords** like `host`, `port`, `net`, `src`, `dst`, and protocol names. Operators include `and` (`&&`), `or` (`||`), and `not` (`!`).

**Examples:**
- `host 192.168.1.5 and port 80` — Captures only traffic to/from IP 192.168.1.5 on port 80 (HTTP).
- `not arp and not icmp` — Captures everything except ARP and ICMP noise (e.g., during malware analysis where broadcast traffic is irrelevant).

**Display Filters (Wireshark-Specific Syntax):**

Display filters are applied **after** the packet has been captured and stored in memory/disk. They do not reduce capture file size but allow the analyst to **dynamically refine** the view of an existing capture. Syntax uses **field references** like `tcp.flags.syn`, `ip.src`, `http.request.method`, with operators `==`, `!=`, `contains`, `matches`, and logical `&&`, `||`, `!`.

**Examples:**
- `tcp.port == 443 && tls.handshake.type == 1` — Shows only TLS ClientHello messages on the HTTPS port.
- `frame.time >= "2024-01-15 10:00:00" && frame.time <= "2024-01-15 11:00:00"` — Restricts view to a specific investigation window.

**Why Capture Filters Are Preferred in High-Volume Production Environments:**

1. **Storage Efficiency** — A 10 Gbps link can generate **terabytes per hour**. Capturing everything is infeasible. Capture filters reduce the volume to only relevant traffic.
2. **Performance** — Filtering at the kernel level avoids the overhead of dissecting irrelevant packets.
3. **Forensic Completeness** — In incident response, you must decide **before** capture what evidence to preserve. A capture filter targeting the suspect's IP ensures you don't miss anything while the incident is active.
4. **Reduced File Size for Evidence** — Smaller `.pcap` files are easier to hash, transfer, store, and present in court.

**Disadvantage:** Capture filters risk **discarding evidence** if the filter is too restrictive. This is why experienced analysts often run a **wide capture filter** combined with **multiple narrow display filters** during analysis.

*(7 marks — 2 for capture filter theory, 2 for display filter theory, 2 for examples, 1 for production rationale)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE)**. Write and explain the command `nmap --script=vuln 192.168.1.10`. List any four pre-defined NSE script categories with one example script from each.

**Model Solution:**

The **Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE)** is one of nmap's most powerful features, allowing users to write (or reuse) **Lua-based scripts** that perform sophisticated network tasks beyond simple port scanning. NSE was introduced in nmap version 4.50 (2007) and has since grown to include hundreds of community-contributed scripts.

Each NSE script is composed of four main sections: a **rule** that determines when the script runs (e.g., only on port 80), an **action** function (the Lua code that executes), and optional pre-action and post-action hooks. Scripts can manipulate packets, perform brute force, query databases, or detect known CVEs.

**Command Explanation: `nmap --script=vuln 192.168.1.10`**

- `nmap` — Invokes the nmap utility.
- `--script=vuln` — Tells nmap to load all scripts whose category tag is `vuln`. These scripts are designed to detect common vulnerabilities.
- `192.168.1.10` — The target host to be scanned.

The output typically lists detected vulnerabilities such as `smb-vuln-ms17-010` (EternalBlue) with severity ratings, allowing the forensic analyst to confirm whether the target was vulnerable to known exploits that may have been used in the breach.

**Four NSE Script Categories with Examples:**

1. **`auth`** — Scripts that test authentication strength. *Example:* `ssh-brute` — Performs brute-force login attempts against SSH to identify weak passwords. Forensically used to confirm a credential was guessable.

2. **`vuln`** — Scripts that detect specific known vulnerabilities. *Example:* `http-csrf` — Detects Cross-Site Request Forgery vulnerabilities in web applications. Used in web application forensic audits.

3. **`discovery`** — Scripts that query services for additional information. *Example:* `snmp-sysdescr` — Extracts the system description string from SNMP-enabled devices. Used to fingerprint network equipment.

4. **`safe`** — Scripts that are non-intrusive and considered safe for production. *Example:* `http-title` — Retrieves the HTML title of web pages to identify the application. Used during reconnaissance without altering the target.

5. **`malware`** — Scripts that detect backdoors and malware-infected hosts. *Example:* `smb-vuln-conficker` — Detects systems infected with the Conficker worm.

*(7 marks — 3 for NSE theory, 2 for command explanation, 2 for categories with examples)*
